topics.title: Biometric Fundamentals, Biometric Technologies, Biometrics vs Traditional Techniques, Characteristics of a Good Biometric System, Benefits of Biometrics, and Key Biometric Processes: Verification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biometric Fundamentals, Biometric Technologies, Biometrics vs Traditional Techniques, Characteristics of a Good Biometric System, Benefits of Biometrics, and Key Biometric Processes: Verification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Biometric Fundamentals — The Science of "You Are the Password"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Biometrics* refers to the automated recognition of individuals based on their **physiological** (fingerprint, iris, face) and **behavioral** (gait, keystroke dynamics, voice) characteristics. It is a measurable biological (anatomical or physiological) and behavioral trait that can be used for automated recognition of an individual.

In the 2024 KTU scheme, biometrics is classified under the domain of **Identity Assurance and Access Control Engineering**, sitting at the intersection of pattern recognition, signal processing, cryptography, and human-computer interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Etymology:** The term "Biometric" originates from the Greek words *bios* (life) and *metron* (measure) — literally meaning the *measurement of life*.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are returning to your childhood home after 20 years. The house key you once used no longer fits, the lock may have been changed, and the passcode your parents gave you might be forgotten. However, your mother recognizes you **instantly** — by the way you walk, the shape of your face, the sound of your voice. This is biometrics: **recognition based on inherent, intrinsic, and difficult-to-forge traits**, rather than something you *carry* (a token) or something you *know* (a password).

---

### 1.2 Biometric Technologies — Taxonomy of Recognizable Traits

KTU 2024 classifies biometric technologies into two super-categories:

#### A. Physiological (Static / Biological) Biometrics
These are based on the *static, physical structure* of the body.

| Trait | Capture Sensor | Universality | Uniqueness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fingerprint** | Optical/Capacitive | High | Very High |
| **Face** | 2D/3D Camera | Very High | Moderate-High |
| **Iris** | IR Camera | Very High | Extremely High |
| **Retina** | IR Fundus Camera | High | Extremely High |
| **Hand Geometry** | Optical Scanner | High | Moderate |
| **Finger Vein / Palm Vein** | NIR Camera | High | High |
| **DNA** | Chemical Analyzer | 100% | 100% |
| **Ear Shape** | Camera | High | Moderate |

#### B. Behavioral (Dynamic) Biometrics
These are based on *patterns in the way an individual acts*.

| Trait | Acquisition | Stability Over Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Voice (Speaker Recognition)** | Microphone | Moderate |
| **Signature Dynamics** | Digitizing Tablet | High |
| **Keystroke Dynamics** | Keyboard Logger | Moderate |
| **Gait** | Accelerometer/Camera | High |
| **Gesture** | Touchscreen | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Soft Biometrics** (height, weight, age, gender, skin color) are sometimes used to *augment* primary biometrics — they are not unique enough to authenticate alone but improve the speed of recognition in large-scale systems (e.g., 1:N identification in airports).

---

### 1.3 Biometrics vs. Traditional Techniques — The Paradigm Shift

Traditional authentication relies on **three classical factors**:
1. **Something You KNOW** (Passwords, PINs)
2. **Something You HAVE** (Smart cards, Tokens, Keys)
3. **Something You ARE** (Biometrics)

The first two are *extrinsic* — separate from the user. The third is *intrinsic*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Triad of Authentication:**
> - **Knowledge-based** (Passwords, PIN, Security Questions)
> - **Possession-based** (Smart Cards, RFID Tokens, OTP devices)
> - **Biometric-based** (Fingerprints, Iris, Face, Voice)

| Parameter | Password / PIN | Smart Card | Biometric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Can be forgotten?** | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Can be shared/stolen?** | Yes | Yes | Extremely Hard |
| **Can be copied/cloned?** | Phishing | Yes | Very Difficult |
| **User convenience** | Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Revocation** | Easy | Easy (re-issue) | Hard (cannot change iris) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Anti-spoofing** | Weak | Moderate | Strong (with liveness) |

#### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a password as a *written letter* left under your doormat — anyone who finds it can read it. A smart card is a *physical key* — anyone who steals the key can use it. A biometric is *the lock itself being part of your body* — you cannot "lose" yourself.

---

### 1.4 Characteristics of a Good Biometric System

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (mapped to ISO/IEC 2382-37 and the classical Jain et al. framework), a biometric trait should ideally satisfy **seven properties**, memorized by the acronym **"7-U H-CAP-PA"**:

1. **Universality (U):** Every person should possess the trait.
2. **Uniqueness (U):** No two persons should share identical biometric features.
3. **Permanence (H):** The trait should be invariant over time.
4. **Collectability (C):** The trait must be quantitatively measurable.
5. **Acceptability (A):** People must be willing to provide the trait.
6. **Performance (P):**Accuracy, speed, robustness under environmental conditions.
7. **Circumvention (P):** Resistance to spoofing, mimicry, and forgery.

Additionally, KTU emphasizes four **operational characteristics** for the *system* itself:
- **Accuracy** (low FAR, low FRR)
- **Speed** (real-time matching)
- **Scalability** (1:1 vs 1:N matching)
- **Interoperability** (standard template formats like ISO 19794)

> [!NOTE]
> **No single biometric is perfect.** Face is highly acceptable but low in permanence under aging. DNA is highly unique but unacceptable (invasive). This is why **multimodal biometric systems** combine two or more traits to compensate for individual weaknesses.

---

### 1.5 Benefits of Biometrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 module lists the following **6 Core Benefits**:

1. **Improved Security:** Cannot be guessed, phished, or brute-forced like passwords.
2. **Non-Transferability:** Physically bound to the user (cannot be lent out).
3. **User Convenience:** No need to remember multiple passwords — *you are the credential*.
4. **Accountability:** Provides a strong, non-repudiable audit trail ("John was there at 10:02 AM").
5. **Scalability & Cost:** Once deployed, reduces password-reset helpdesk costs (Gartner estimates 20–50% reduction).
6. **Fraud Prevention:** Eliminates duplicate identities (one-person-many-IDs) and synthetic identities in national-ID programs (Aadhaar, e-Passports).

---

### 1.6 Key Biometric Process: VERIFICATION

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Verification* is a **1:1 (one-to-one) matching** process where the system compares the live biometric sample of a claimed identity against the **pre-stored template(s) of that specific user** to confirm (or reject) the claim.

#### Conceptual Analogy
Verification is like a **bouncer at a VIP party** who already has your photo on the guest list. He checks whether the person standing in front of him matches *the one specific photo* — not the entire database of party-goers. This is fundamentally different from **Identification (1:N)**, where the bouncer must scan the entire crowd to find you.

#### The Two Phases of Verification

**Phase 1 — ENROLLMENT (Offline, One-time):**
- Sensor captures raw biometric sample (e.g., fingerprint image).
- Preprocessing: noise removal, normalization, segmentation.
- Feature Extraction: ridges, minutiae, eigenvalues, etc.
- **Template Generation:** A compact mathematical representation is stored in the database or on a smart card.

**Phase 2 — AUTHENTICATION (Online, Repeated):**
- User **claims** an identity (e.g., enters user-ID or inserts smart card).
- Live sample is captured and processed into a *query template*.
- **Matcher** compares the query template against the *claimed template* using a similarity/dissimilarity score.
- **Decision Module** applies a threshold to output **Accept (Genuine)** or **Reject (Impostor)**.

#### Verification vs. Identification

| Property | Verification (1:1) | Identification (1:N) |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Identity + Biometric | Biometric only |
| Search | Single template | Entire database |
| Decision | Match/Non-Match | Best candidate ID |
| Speed | Fast (milliseconds) | Slower (scales with N) |
| Use case | Login, ATM, eKYC | Forensic, surveillance, watchlist |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundations of Biometric Verification

At its core, biometric verification is a **statistical hypothesis testing problem**. Given a query feature vector $X_q$ and a stored template $X_t$, we test:

$$
H_0 : X_q \text{ comes from the genuine user} \quad \text{(Null Hypothesis)}
$$
$$
H_1 : X_q \text{ comes from an impostor}
$$

The matcher computes a **similarity score** $S(X_q, X_t)$ (or equivalently a **dissimilarity / distance score** $D$).

---

### 2.2 Core Performance Metrics — The "Big Four"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU question on biometric verification will involve these four metrics. Memorize them in this exact notation.

#### A. False Acceptance Rate (FAR) — Type II Error
The probability that an **impostor** is wrongly accepted as a genuine user.

$$
\text{FAR}(\tau) = \frac{\text{Number of impostor scores } \geq \tau}{\text{Total number of impostor attempts}}
$$

- FAR is also called **False Match Rate (FMR)** in ISO standards.
- FAR decreases as the threshold $\tau$ is *raised* (stricter).

#### B. False Rejection Rate (FRR) — Type I Error
The probability that a **genuine user** is wrongly rejected as an impostor.

$$
\text{FRR}(\tau) = \frac{\text{Number of genuine scores} < \tau}{\text{Total number of genuine attempts}}
$$

- FRR is also called **False Non-Match Rate (FNMR)**.
- FRR increases as $\tau$ is raised.

#### C. Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR)
$$
\text{GAR}(\tau) = 1 - \text{FRR}(\tau)
$$

#### D. Equal Error Rate (EER)
The operating point where $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$. This is the **single most important scalar metric** for comparing biometric algorithms.

$$
\text{EER} = \text{FAR}(\tau^*) = \text{FRR}(\tau^*)
$$

where $\tau^*$ is the threshold at which both error rates are equal.

#### E. Failure to Enroll Rate (FTE)
Percentage of users who cannot successfully provide a usable biometric sample during enrollment (e.g., 2% of population has unreadable fingerprints due to manual labor).

#### F. Failure to Capture Rate (FTC)
Percentage of authentication attempts where the sensor fails to acquire a usable sample (e.g., dirty fingerprint sensor, poor lighting for face).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value (Good System) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FAR}(\tau) = \dfrac{N_{FA}}{N_{I}}$ | False Acceptance Rate | $0.001\% - 0.1\%$ |
| 2 | $\text{FRR}(\tau) = \dfrac{N_{FR}}{N_{G}}$ | False Rejection Rate | $0.1\% - 1\%$ |
| 3 | $\text{GAR}(\tau) = 1 - \text{FRR}(\tau)$ | Genuine Acceptance Rate | $99\% - 99.99\%$ |
| 4 | $\text{EER} = \text{FAR}(\tau^*) = \text{FRR}(\tau^*)$ | Equal Error Rate | $< 1\%$ (Excellent) |
| 5 | $\text{FTE} = \dfrac{N_{FTE}}{N_{Total}}$ | Failure to Enroll | $< 2\%$ |
| 6 | $\text{FTC} = \dfrac{N_{FTC}}{N_{Auth}}$ | Failure to Capture | $< 0.5\%$ |
| 7 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall Accuracy | $> 99\%$ |
| 8 | $\tau^*$ | Optimal Threshold (EER point) | Application-specific |
| 9 | $D(X_q, X_t) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{q,i} - x_{t,i})^2}$ | Euclidean Distance (Matcher) | Used for feature vectors |
| 10 | $S(X_q, X_t) = \cos(\theta) = \dfrac{X_q \cdot X_t}{\vert X_q \vert \cdot \vert X_t \vert}$ | Cosine Similarity (Matcher) | Used for normalized vectors |
| 11 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{T_{match}}$ | Matches per second | Real-time: $> 1000$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Trade-off Law (KTU High-Yield Insight):**
> - **Lowering** $\tau$ (lenient): FAR $\uparrow$, FRR $\downarrow$ → System is permissive.
> - **Raising** $\tau$ (strict): FAR $\downarrow$, FRR $\uparrow$ → System is restrictive.
> - **High-security** applications (nuclear, military) prioritize **low FAR**.
> - **Convenience-oriented** applications (smartphone unlock) tolerate higher FAR for lower FRR.

---

### 2.4 Decision Theory Behind Verification

The matcher produces a score $S$. The decision rule is:

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{ACCEPT (Genuine)} & \text{if } S \geq \tau \\[4pt]
\text{REJECT (Impostor)} & \text{if } S < \tau
\end{cases}
$$

The probability distributions of genuine and impostor scores are typically modeled as:

$$
f_{genuine}(s) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_G, \sigma_G^2)
$$
$$
f_{impostor}(s) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_I, \sigma_I^2)
$$

where $\mu_G > \mu_I$ (genuine scores are higher on average).

The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve plots GAR vs. FAR as $\tau$ varies — it is the **standard benchmarking tool** for biometric systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real deployments, the ROC curve is never a clean step function. It is always a smooth S-curve (sigmoid-like). The steeper the ROC, the better the biometric modality's separability between genuine and impostor distributions.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aadhaar (UIDAI, India):** Uses 1:N fingerprint + iris + face multimodal identification at 1.3 billion scale. FAR target $< 0.057\%$.
- **e-Passport (ICAO 9303):** Uses face + fingerprint verification at border gates. Threshold tuned to FAR $\approx 0.0001$.
- **Apple Face ID:** 30,000 IR dots → 3D face map → verification in $< 1$ sec with EER $\approx 1$ in 1,000,000.
- **Forensic DNA matching:** Used in 1:1 "cold-hit" verification against criminal databases; uses CODIS loci.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deriving EER from the Underlying Distributions

Assume the genuine and impostor score distributions are **Gaussian**:

$$
f_G(s) = \frac{1}{\sigma_G \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(s - \mu_G)^2}{2\sigma_G^2}\right)
$$
$$
f_I(s) = \frac{1}{\sigma_I \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(s - \mu_I)^2}{2\sigma_I^2}\right)
$$

**Step 1: Express FRR in terms of the genuine distribution.**

FRR is the probability that a genuine score falls below the threshold $\tau$:

$$
\text{FRR}(\tau) = P(S < \tau \mid H_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\tau} f_G(s) \, ds
$$

Substituting the Gaussian PDF:

$$
\text{FRR}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\tau} \frac{1}{\sigma_G \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(s - \mu_G)^2}{2\sigma_G^2}\right) ds
$$

**Step 2: Express FAR in terms of the impostor distribution.**

FAR is the probability that an impostor score exceeds the threshold:

$$
\text{FAR}(\tau) = P(S \geq \tau \mid H_1) = \int_{\tau}^{+\infty} f_I(s) \, ds
$$
$$
\text{FAR}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma_I \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(s - \mu_I)^2}{2\sigma_I^2}\right) ds
$$

**Step 3: Use the complementary error function (erfc) to obtain closed form.**

Recall the identity:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\tau} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(s-\mu)^2 / 2\sigma^2} ds = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\mu - \tau}{\sigma \sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{FRR}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\mu_G - \tau}{\sigma_G \sqrt{2}}\right)
$$
$$
\text{FAR}(\tau) = 1 - \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\mu_I - \tau}{\sigma_I \sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_I}{\sigma_I \sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Apply the EER condition $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ to solve for $\tau^*$.**

$$
\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\mu_G - \tau^*}{\sigma_G \sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\tau^* - \mu_I}{\sigma_I \sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

Since erfc is monotonically decreasing, the arguments must be equal:

$$
\frac{\mu_G - \tau^*}{\sigma_G \sqrt{2}} = \frac{\tau^* - \mu_I}{\sigma_I \sqrt{2}}
$$

The $\sqrt{2}$ cancels:

$$
\frac{\mu_G - \tau^*}{\sigma_G} = \frac{\tau^* - \mu_I}{\sigma_I}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\tau^*$.**

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\sigma_I (\mu_G - \tau^*) = \sigma_G (\tau^* - \mu_I)
$$

Expanding:

$$
\sigma_I \mu_G - \sigma_I \tau^* = \sigma_G \tau^* - \sigma_G \mu_I
$$

Collecting $\tau^*$ terms:

$$
\sigma_I \mu_G + \sigma_G \mu_I = \tau^* (\sigma_G + \sigma_I)
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau^* = \frac{\sigma_I \mu_G + \sigma_G \mu_I}{\sigma_G + \sigma_I}}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute $\tau^*$ back to find EER.**

$$
\text{EER} = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{\mu_G - \tau^*}{\sigma_G \sqrt{2}}\right)
$$

This is the **closed-form EER** for two Gaussian distributions.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Biometric Modalities (KTU 2024)

| Modality | Universality | Uniqueness | Permanence | Collectability | Acceptability | Circumvention Resistance | Typical EER |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Face (2D)** | High | Low-Med | Low (Aging) | High | Very High | Low (Photos fool it) | $2\% - 5\%$ |
| **Face (3D / IR)** | High | High | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | $< 0.1\%$ |
| **Fingerprint** | High | High | High | Medium | Medium | Medium (Latent prints) | $0.1\% - 0.5\%$ |
| **Iris** | Very High | Very High | Very High | Medium | Medium | High | $0.0001\%$ |
| **Retina** | High | Very High | Very High | Low | Very Low | Very High | $< 0.0001\%$ |
| **Voice** | High | Low | Low (Cold, Emotion) | High | High | Low (Recordings) | $2\% - 10\%$ |
| **Hand Geometry** | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium | $1\% - 2\%$ |
| **Signature** | High | Medium | Medium | High | High | Medium | $1\% - 3\%$ |
| **Gait** | High | Low-Med | Low | Medium | High | Low | $5\% - 15\%$ |
| **DNA** | 100% | 100% | 100% | Very Low | Very Low | Highest | Near 0% |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:**
A fingerprint verification system has genuine score distribution $\mathcal{N}(75, 10^2)$ and impostor score distribution $\mathcal{N}(40, 12^2)$. The threshold is set at $\tau = 60$. Calculate FAR, FRR, GAR, and the EER.

**Step 1: Compute FRR(60).**

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{75 - 60}{10 \sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{15}{14.14}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}(1.0607)
$$

From standard erfc tables, $\operatorname{erfc}(1.06) \approx 0.1407$. Thus:

$$
\text{FRR}(60) \approx 0.0704 = 7.04\%
$$

**Step 2: Compute FAR(60).**

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{60 - 40}{12 \sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{20}{16.97}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}(1.1785)
$$

$\operatorname{erfc}(1.18) \approx 0.0899$. Thus:

$$
\text{FAR}(60) \approx 0.0450 = 4.50\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute GAR.**

$$
\text{GAR} = 1 - 0.0704 = 0.9296 = 92.96\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute EER (the threshold $\tau^*$).**

$$
\tau^* = \frac{\sigma_I \mu_G + \sigma_G \mu_I}{\sigma_G + \sigma_I} = \frac{12 \cdot 75 + 10 \cdot 40}{10 + 12} = \frac{900 + 400}{22} = \frac{1300}{22} \approx 59.09
$$

**Step 5: Compute EER value at $\tau^* = 59.09$.**

$$
\text{EER} = \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{75 - 59.09}{10\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}\left(\frac{15.91}{14.14}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}(1.125)
$$

$\operatorname{erfc}(1.125) \approx 0.1143$. Thus:

$$
\text{EER} \approx 0.0572 = 5.72\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical takeaway:** This system has a *moderate* EER. For a high-security use case, you would tune $\tau$ to **lower the FAR** (accepting a higher FRR); for consumer use, you would do the **opposite**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Biometric Verification System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User claims Identity<br>UserID or Smart Card] --> B[Biometric Sensor<br>Fingerprint Iris Face Camera]
    B --> C[Preprocessing<br>Noise Removal Normalization]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction<br>Minutiae Eigenfaces MFCC]
    D --> E[Query Template Xq]
    E --> F{Matcher Module<br>Compute Score S Xq Xt}
    G[(Template Database<br>Stored Templates)] --> F
    F --> H[Score S]
    H --> I{Decision Module<br>S greater than or equal tau}
    I -->|Yes| J[ACCEPT Genuine User]
    I -->|No| K[REJECT Impostor]
    J --> L[Audit Log Timestamp]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functionality Mapping:**
> - **Sensor** — physical acquisition (electromechanical, optical).
> - **Preprocessing** — image enhancement, segmentation, alignment.
> - **Feature Extraction** — domain-specific algorithm (e.g., LoG filter for iris; Gabor filter for fingerprint).
> - **Matcher** — distance metric (Euclidean, Hamming, Cosine, Mahalanobis).
> - **Decision Module** — threshold-based hypothesis test.

---

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Complete Verification Lifecycle (Enrollment + Authentication)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ENROLLMENT[PHASE 1 ENROLLMENT Offline]
        E1[User presents biometric to sensor] --> E2[Capture raw sample]
        E2 --> E3[Quality Check SNR Contrast]
        E3 -->|Fail| E4[Request Recapture]
        E4 --> E1
        E3 -->|Pass| E5[Feature Extraction]
        E5 --> E6[Generate Template T]
        E6 --> E7[Encrypt and Store in DB]
    end
    subgraph AUTHENTICATION[PHASE 2 AUTHENTICATION Online]
        A1[User claims ID] --> A2[Retrieve Stored Template T]
        A3[User presents live biometric] --> A4[Capture Live Sample]
        A4 --> A5[Feature Extraction]
        A5 --> A6[Generate Query Template Xq]
        A2 --> A7{Matcher}
        A6 --> A7
        A7 --> A8[Compute Similarity Score S]
        A8 --> A9{S greater than or equal tau}
        A9 -->|Yes| A10[GRANT ACCESS Genuine]
        A9 -->|No| A11[DENY ACCESS Impostor]
    end
    E7 -.-> A2
```

---

### 4.3 Threshold Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW_THRESHOLD[LOW THRESHOLD Permissive]
        LT1[FAR HIGH] --> LT2[FRR LOW]
        LT2 --> LT3[Convenient but Insecure]
    end
    subgraph HIGH_THRESHOLD[HIGH THRESHOLD Strict]
        HT1[FAR LOW] --> HT2[FRR HIGH]
        HT2 --> HT3[Secure but Annoying]
    end
    subgraph EER_POINT[EER POINT Optimal]
        EP1[FAR equal FRR] --> EP2[Balanced Operating Point]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Verification vs. Identification — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[User presents biometric] --> Q1{Does user claim<br>an Identity?}
    Q1 -->|YES Claimed ID known| V[VERIFICATION 1 to 1<br>Compare against single template]
    Q1 -->|NO Identity unknown| I[IDENTIFICATION 1 to N<br>Search whole database]
    V --> V1[Match or No Match Binary Output]
    I --> I1[Ranked List of Candidates Top K]
    I --> I2{Is top score above threshold?}
    I2 -->|Yes| I3[Return Best Match ID]
    I2 -->|No| I4[No Match Output]
```

---

### 4.5 Biometric System Failure Mode Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Acquisition Failure] --> F1a[FTE Failure to Enroll]
    F1 --> F1b[FTC Failure to Capture]
    F2[Matching Failure] --> F2a[FRR False Reject Type I]
    F2b[FAR False Accept Type II] --> F2
    F3[Decision Failure] --> F3a[Suboptimal Threshold Selection]
    F3 --> F3b[Liveness Detection Bypass Spoofing]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define Biometrics. List and briefly explain the **two main categories** of biometric traits with **one example each**.

**Model Answer:**

> Biometrics is the automated recognition of individuals based on their unique physiological or behavioral characteristics.
>
> 1. **Physiological Biometrics:** Based on the *static physical structure* of the body. *Example:* Fingerprint, Iris pattern, Face geometry.
> 2. **Behavioral Biometrics:** Based on the *dynamic patterns* of human action. *Example:* Voice (speaker recognition), Gait, Keystroke dynamics.
>
> *(Word count: ~50 words — exactly 3-mark depth.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between **Verification** and **Identification** in biometric systems. State one real-world example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Verification | Identification |
|---|---|---|
| Matching Type | 1:1 | 1:N |
| Input | Identity + Biometric | Biometric only |
| Output | Yes/No | Ranked candidate list / ID |
| Example | ATM card + fingerprint | Forensics matching latent print to criminal DB |

> Example: **Verification** — Fingerprint login on a smartphone. **Identification** — Face recognition at an airport surveillance gate scanning all passengers.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q3.a (7 Marks)** Explain the **seven desirable characteristics** of a biometric trait. State why **no single biometric is universally "best"** and discuss the concept of **multimodal biometrics** as a solution.

**Model Answer:**

The seven characteristics (KTU framework, mapped to ISO/IEC 2382-37) are:

1. **Universality** — Every individual must possess the trait. *Counter-example:* Retina scanning is impossible for some blind patients.
2. **Uniqueness** — The trait must be distinguishable across individuals. Fingerprints and iris patterns are extremely unique.
3. **Permanence** — Invariance over time. *Counter-example:* Face changes with aging.
4. **Collectability** — Measurable by sensors. *Counter-example:* DNA requires invasive blood/saliva collection.
5. **Acceptability** — Public willingness. *Counter-example:* Retina is highly unacceptable (eye proximity).
6. **Performance** — Accuracy, speed, robustness.
7. **Circumvention** — Resistance to spoofing.

**Why no biometric is universally best:**

| Trait | Strong Point | Weak Point |
|---|---|---|
| Face | Universality, Acceptability | Vulnerable to photos |
| Iris | Uniqueness, Permanence | Cost of sensor |
| Voice | Remote capture | Vulnerable to recordings |
| Fingerprint | Established legal status | Affected by cuts, dirty fingers |
| DNA | Highest accuracy | Slow, invasive |

**Multimodal Biometrics as a Solution:**

Multimodal systems combine **two or more traits** (e.g., fingerprint + face, or fingerprint + iris). This approach:
- **Reduces FAR** (an impostor must fool both modalities).
- **Increases universality** (a worker with no fingerprint can still use iris).
- **Resilience:** Spoofing 2 traits simultaneously is exponentially harder.
- **Higher accuracy:** EER can drop from ~1% (unimodal) to ~0.01% (multimodal).

*Examples:* Aadhaar (fingerprint + iris + face), FBI's NGI (face + fingerprint + iris).

**(7 marks distributed as: 4 marks for the 7 characteristics, 2 marks for the "no best biometric" argument, 1 mark for multimodal solution.)**

---

**Q3.b (7 Marks)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **general structure of a biometric verification system**. Describe the functions of the **enrollment** and **authentication** modules.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram** (must be drawn in exam — *marks awarded*):

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ BIOMETRIC VERIFICATION SYSTEM                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                          │
│  [User ID Input]──┐                                     │
│                    ▼                                     │
│              [Template DB] ──► [Matcher] ◄── [Sensor]    │
│                                    │           │         │
│                                    ▼           ▼         │
│                          [Score S]    [Preprocessor]     │
│                                    │           │         │
│                                    ▼           ▼         │
│                          [Feature Extractor] [Quality    │
│                                    │         Check]      │
│                                    ▼                     │
│                          [Decision Module]               │
│                          (Threshold τ)                   │
│                                    │                     │
│                                    ▼                     │
│                          [Accept / Reject]               │
│                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Module Functions:**

**A. Enrollment Module (One-time, Offline):**
1. **Sensor** captures the raw biometric (e.g., 2D image of fingerprint).
2. **Preprocessing** performs segmentation, noise reduction, normalization.
3. **Feature Extractor** generates a compact template (e.g., minutiae points for fingerprint).
4. **Template DB** stores the encrypted template, indexed by user-ID.

**B. Authentication Module (Online, Repeated):**
1. User **claims** an ID (typed or via smart card).
2. System **retrieves** the enrolled template for that ID.
3. Live sample is captured, preprocessed, and feature-extracted.
4. **Matcher** computes a similarity/distance score $S(X_q, X_t)$.
5. **Decision Module** compares $S$ to threshold $\tau$:
   - $S \geq \tau \Rightarrow$ **ACCEPT** (genuine)
   - $S < \tau \Rightarrow$ **REJECT** (impostor)

**(7 marks: 3 marks diagram, 2 marks enrollment, 2 marks authentication.)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q4.a (7 Marks)** Define **FAR, FRR, GAR, and EER**. Sketch a **ROC curve** and mark the **EER point** on it. Explain the **trade-off** between FAR and FRR.

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions** (each 1.25 marks):

- **False Acceptance Rate (FAR):** Probability that an impostor is wrongly accepted. $\text{FAR} = N_{FA} / N_I$.
- **False Rejection Rate (FRR):** Probability that a genuine user is wrongly rejected. $\text{FRR} = N_{FR} / N_G$.
- **Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR):** $\text{GAR} = 1 - \text{FRR}$.
- **Equal Error Rate (EER):** Operating threshold where $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$.

**ROC Curve Sketch** (in exam):
```
  GAR
  1.0 ┤                              ●●●●●●●
      │                         ●●●●●
      │                      ●●●
 0.5 ┤- - - - - - - - - - ●- - - - - - - - - EER Point
      │                 ●●●
      │              ●●●
      │           ●●●
      │         ●●
  0  ┤●●●●●●●●●
      └──────────────────────────────────────► FAR
       0                              1.0
```

**Trade-off Explanation:**

- As threshold $\tau$ **increases** (stricter): FAR $\downarrow$ but FRR $\uparrow$.
- As threshold $\tau$ **decreases** (lenient): FAR $\uparrow$ but FRR $\downarrow$.
- The two curves intersect at EER — the **optimal balanced operating point**.
- For **high-security** systems (military, banking), choose $\tau > \tau^*$ to minimize FAR (accept fewer impostors, even if some genuine users are inconvenienced).
- For **consumer** systems (phone unlock), choose $\tau < \tau^*$ to minimize FRR (avoid annoying genuine users, even at slight security cost).

**(7 marks: 4 × 1.25 for definitions, 2 marks for ROC+EER, 1.25 marks for trade-off explanation.)**

---

**Q4.b (7 Marks)** Explain the **benefits and limitations of biometric systems** over traditional authentication methods. State **three real-world applications** where biometrics has replaced passwords.

**Model Answer:**

**Benefits** (4 marks):

1. **Non-Transferable:** Biometric traits are physically bound to the user — cannot be shared, lent, or stolen like a password or smart card.
2. **Cannot be Forgotten:** Users do not need to remember complex strings (or worse, write them down).
3. **Difficult to Forge:** Modern liveness detection (3D depth, IR reflectance, blood-flow detection) makes spoofing hard.
4. **Strong Non-Repudiation:** Provides a legally binding audit trail ("*the iris print belongs to user X*").
5. **Reduced TCO:** Eliminates password-reset helpdesk costs (estimated 20–50% reduction in enterprise IT costs).
6. **User Convenience:** Single-sign-on via biometric; faster than typing passwords repeatedly.

**Limitations** (2 marks):

1. **Cannot be Revoked:** If a fingerprint template is compromised, the user cannot "change" their fingerprint.
2. **Privacy Concerns:** Centralized biometric databases can be misused for surveillance.
3. **Noisy Data:** Biometric signals vary (lighting for face, dryness for fingerprint).
4. **Spoofing:** Replay attacks, presentation attacks (printed photo, fake fingerprint).
5. **Cost:** Specialized sensors (especially 3D face, iris) are expensive.

**Three Real-World Applications** (1 mark each):

1. **Aadhaar (India):** 1.3 billion residents authenticated via fingerprint + iris for government benefits, banking.
2. **Apple Face ID / Touch ID:** Unlocks iPhone and authorizes Apple Pay transactions.
3. **Border Control (e-Passport / e-Gates):** ICAO-compliant biometric passport verification at immigration.
4. **Windows Hello:** Biometric login to Windows devices.
5. **BNI Mobile / Banking Apps:** Fingerprint-based transaction signing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse FAR with FRR.** A frequent error: students write "FAR is when genuine users are rejected" — this is FRR.
> 2. **Verification is 1:1, Identification is 1:N.** Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 3. **Always state units** of EER (%). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 4. **In ROC curves**, label **both axes** (FAR on X, GAR on Y). Mark the EER explicitly.
> 5. **In block diagrams**, use **arrows** for data flow; do not forget to include the **threshold $\tau$** in the decision module.
> 6. **"No biometric is perfect"** is the *expected* answer — do not defend one trait as universally superior.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Rapid Revision Bulletins:**

- **Biometrics** = automated recognition of individuals via **physiological** (body structure) or **behavioral** (action patterns) traits.
- **7 desirable characteristics** (mnemonic **"7-U H-CAP-PA"**): Universality, Uniqueness, Permanence, Collectability, Acceptability, Performance, Circumvention.
- **Three authentication factors:** Something you **know** (password), **have** (card), **are** (biometric).
- **Verification = 1:1**, **Identification = 1:N**.
- **FAR** = Impostor accepted. **FRR** = Genuine rejected. **GAR = 1 − FRR**. **EER** = FAR = FRR.
- **Threshold $\tau$ trade-off:** Higher $\tau \Rightarrow$ Lower FAR, Higher FRR.
- **Multimodal biometrics** = combining 2+ traits → reduces FAR, increases universality.
- **Enrolment** is offline & one-time. **Authentication** is online & repeated.
- **Matcher** computes score $S$; **Decision** compares $S$ to $\tau$.
- **Soft biometrics** (height, age) are *auxiliary* — not used alone.
- **ROC curve** plots GAR (Y) vs FAR (X); **steeper ROC = better separability**.
- **EER formula** (Gaussian assumption): $\tau^* = (\sigma_I \mu_G + \sigma_G \mu_I) / (\sigma_G + \sigma_I)$.
- **No biometric is universally best** — each has strengths and weaknesses.
- **Real-world use cases:** Aadhaar (1:1B scale), Apple Face ID (EER 1/1M), e-Passport, FBI NGI.
- **Liveness detection** is critical to prevent **presentation attacks** (spoofing).
- **Standardization:** ISO/IEC 2382-37 (vocabulary), ISO/IEC 19794 (template format), ICAO 9303 (e-passport).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PECCT635 Biometric Security**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification and Biometric Matching, Performance Measures in Biometric Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Identification and Biometric Matching, Performance Measures in Biometric Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biometric Matching — The Formal Definition

In the context of biometric security, **matching** is the computational process of comparing a presented biometric sample (the *probe* or *query*) against one or more previously enrolled biometric templates (the *gallery* or *reference* database) to produce a **similarity score** (or **dissimilarity/distance score**), which is then evaluated against a decision threshold to render an accept/reject decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus Context):** *"Biometric matching is the process of comparing the extracted feature set of an input biometric sample with the stored template(s) to compute a match score, which is then classified against a decision threshold."*

Biometric matching is broadly classified into two operational modes:

1. **Verification (Authentication) — 1:1 Matching**
   Confirms whether a person **is who they claim to be**. The user presents both a biometric sample *and* an identity claim (e.g., User ID, smart card). The system retrieves the stored template for that specific claimed identity and performs a **one-to-one** comparison.

2. **Identification (Recognition) — 1:N Matching**
   Establishes the **identity of an unknown individual** by searching the entire enrolled database. The system performs a **one-to-many** search and returns either the top candidate (closed-set) or a "no match" decision (open-set).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** Verification answers the question *"Are you Mr. X?"* while Identification answers *"Who are you?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Scenario

Imagine walking into **Cochin International Airport (COK)**:

- **Verification mode (1:1):** You walk up to the immigration counter and hand over your **passport** (identity claim) along with your face/fingerprint. The officer compares *your* face against *that specific passport photo*. One document, one comparison.
- **Identification mode (1:N):** A CCTV camera silently scans a face in a crowded terminal and tries to match it against a database of **10 million flagged travellers**. No identity claim — the system must find a match or declare "unknown".

This analogy also illustrates the **computational complexity gap**: 1:1 is O(1) lookup, while 1:N is O(N) — identification is therefore inherently slower and error-prone as N grows.

## 1.3 Performance Measures — Why They Matter

A biometric system is **never 100% accurate**. The twin unavoidable errors are:
- **False Acceptance (FA):** Impostor accepted as genuine.
- **False Rejection (FR):** Genuine user rejected as impostor.

These trade off against each other via a **decision threshold**, and quantifying this trade-off is the essence of biometric performance evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The choice of threshold fundamentally changes the system's character — lowering it makes the system "more lenient" (higher FA, lower FR), and raising it makes it "more strict" (lower FA, higher FR). Performance metrics provide a **mathematical language** to describe this trade-off precisely.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Matching Score & Decision Threshold

Let the matching algorithm output a score $S$ for a comparison. Define:
- **Genuine Score Distribution:** $p_g(s)$ — scores when probe and gallery belong to the **same** individual.
- **Impostor Score Distribution:** $p_i(s)$ — scores when probe and gallery belong to **different** individuals.

For a similarity-based system (higher = more similar), the decision rule is:

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Genuine (Accept)} & \text{if } S \geq \tau \\ \text{Impostor (Reject)} & \text{if } S < \tau \end{cases}$$

For a distance-based system (lower = more similar), the inequality signs are reversed.

## 2.2 Core Performance Metrics — Definitions

### 2.2.1 False Acceptance Rate (FAR) / False Match Rate (FMR)
Probability that the system **incorrectly accepts an impostor**:

$$\text{FAR}(\tau) = P(S \geq \tau \mid \text{Impostor}) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} p_i(s)\, ds$$

### 2.2.2 False Rejection Rate (FRR) / False Non-Match Rate (FNMR)
Probability that the system **incorrectly rejects a genuine user**:

$$\text{FRR}(\tau) = P(S < \tau \mid \text{Genuine}) = \int_{-\infty}^{\tau} p_g(s)\, ds$$

### 2.2.3 Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR) / True Match Rate (TMR)
$$\text{GAR}(\tau) = 1 - \text{FRR}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} p_g(s)\, ds$$

### 2.2.4 Equal Error Rate (EER)
The operating threshold $\tau_{EER}$ where FAR equals FRR:

$$\text{EER} = \text{FAR}(\tau_{EER}) = \text{FRR}(\tau_{EER})$$

> [!NOTE]
> EER is the **single most cited scalar metric** for biometric accuracy in KTU exam questions and in industry. A *lower EER* implies a *better* biometric system.

### 2.2.5 Failure to Enroll Rate (FTER)
Fraction of users for whom the enrollment process fails to produce a usable template:

$$\text{FTER} = \frac{\text{Number of users who failed to enroll}}{\text{Total number of users attempting enrollment}}$$

### 2.2.6 Failure to Capture Rate (FTCR)
Fraction of attempts where the sensor fails to capture a usable sample (distinct from matching failure):

$$\text{FTCR} = \frac{\text{Number of failed capture attempts}}{\text{Total number of capture attempts}}$$

### 2.2.7 Identification-Specific Metrics (CMC Curve)

For 1:N identification, define **Cumulative Match Characteristic (CMC)** at rank $k$:

$$\text{CMC}(k) = P(\text{correct identity appears in top-}k \text{ candidates})$$

Special cases:
- **Rank-1 Accuracy (R1):** Probability the top match is correct.
- **Rank-N Accuracy:** Always 1.0 in closed-set identification (since the probe is in the gallery by definition).

## 2.3 The ROC and DET Curves — Visual Trade-off Tools

| Curve Type | Y-axis | X-axis | Use |
|------------|--------|--------|-----|
| **ROC** (Receiver Operating Characteristic) | GAR = 1 − FRR | FAR | Standard ML classification plot |
| **DET** (Detection Error Trade-off) | FRR (log scale) | FAR (log scale) | Preferred for biometric comparison; linearizes Gaussian distributions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **DET curve** is the de-facto standard in KTU-level biometric literature (Jain, Pankanti et al.) because on a log-log scale, the FRR vs FAR trade-off for Gaussian-distributed genuine/impostor scores plots as a near-straight line, making system comparisons geometrically intuitive.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value |
|--------|---------|-------|------------|
| FAR / FMR | $\int_{\tau}^{\infty} p_i(s)\, ds$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| FRR / FNMR | $\int_{-\infty}^{\tau} p_g(s)\, ds$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| GAR / TMR | $1 - \text{FRR}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| EER | $\text{FAR}(\tau_{EER}) = \text{FRR}(\tau_{EER})$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| FTER | Failures / Enrollment attempts | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| FTCR | Failed captures / Capture attempts | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| CMC(k) | $P(\text{correct in top-}k)$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| Throughput | Users processed per unit time | $\mathbb{R}^+$ | $\infty$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Border Control (e-Gates):** Operate at low FAR ($\approx 0.0001$) to prevent security breaches; high FRR is tolerated and resolved by manual fallback.
- **Mobile Phone Unlock:** Operates at low FRR ($\approx 0.01$) for user convenience; a small FAR is acceptable since the attacker has only one "guess" per device.
- **Aadhaar Authentication (India):** Uses a **fusion of fingerprint + iris + face** to push EER below 0.001% — a 1:N identification at population scale.
- **NIST FRVT/FVRT Evaluations:** Publish DET curves to enable apples-to-apples comparison of vendor algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EER in Terms of Score Distribution Parameters

Assume the genuine scores follow $\mathcal{N}(\mu_g, \sigma_g^2)$ and impostor scores follow $\mathcal{N}(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$, with $\mu_g > \mu_i$ (similarity-based system).

**Step 1 — Write FAR in closed form using the Q-function:**

For a similarity threshold $\tau$:

$$\text{FAR}(\tau) = P(S \geq \tau \mid \text{Impostor}) = Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right)$$

where $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2}\, dt$ is the standard Gaussian tail probability.

**Step 2 — Write FRR in closed form:**

$$\text{FRR}(\tau) = P(S < \tau \mid \text{Genuine}) = 1 - Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_g}{\sigma_g}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu_g - \tau}{\sigma_g}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Impose the EER condition $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$:**

$$Q\!\left(\frac{\tau_{EER} - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu_g - \tau_{EER}}{\sigma_g}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use the strict monotonicity of $Q$:**

Since $Q$ is strictly decreasing, the arguments must be equal:

$$\frac{\tau_{EER} - \mu_i}{\sigma_i} = \frac{\mu_g - \tau_{EER}}{\sigma_g}$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $\tau_{EER}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_g (\tau_{EER} - \mu_i) &= \sigma_i (\mu_g - \tau_{EER}) \\
\sigma_g \cdot \tau_{EER} - \sigma_g \mu_i &= \sigma_i \mu_g - \sigma_i \cdot \tau_{EER} \\
\tau_{EER} (\sigma_g + \sigma_i) &= \sigma_i \mu_g + \sigma_g \mu_i \\
\tau_{EER} &= \frac{\sigma_i \mu_g + \sigma_g \mu_i}{\sigma_g + \sigma_i}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the FAR expression to get the scalar EER value:**

$$\boxed{\text{EER} = Q\!\left(\frac{\tau_{EER} - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{\mu_g - \tau_{EER}}{\sigma_g}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Step 1 expresses FAR/FRR as integrals of Gaussian PDFs. Step 3 enforces the definition of EER as the *intersection point* of the FAR and FRR curves. Step 5 is a linear solve; the $\tau_{EER}$ is a **weighted mean** of the two means, weighted by the opposite standard deviation.

### 3.1.1 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $\mu_g = 0.85$, $\sigma_g = 0.08$, $\mu_i = 0.45$, $\sigma_i = 0.10$.

**Compute $\tau_{EER}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{EER} &= \frac{(0.10)(0.85) + (0.08)(0.45)}{0.08 + 0.10} \\
&= \frac{0.0850 + 0.0360}{0.18} \\
&= \frac{0.1210}{0.18} \\
&\approx 0.6722
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute EER:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EER} &= Q\!\left(\frac{0.6722 - 0.45}{0.10}\right) = Q(2.222) \\
&\approx 0.0131
\end{aligned}$$

So this hypothetical system has an **EER of ~1.31%**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Decision Error Trade-off (DET) Linearization

The DET curve plots $\text{FRR}$ on the y-axis vs $\text{FAR}$ on the x-axis, **both on a normal-deviate (probit) scale** $\Phi^{-1}(\cdot)$.

**Step 1 — Take the inverse standard normal of both rates:**

Let $x = \Phi^{-1}(\text{FAR})$ and $y = \Phi^{-1}(\text{FRR})$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian expressions:**

For impostor: $\text{FAR} = Q\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}\right)$.

So $\Phi^{-1}(\text{FAR}) = \frac{\tau - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$, giving:

$$x = \frac{\tau - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$$

For genuine: $\text{FRR} = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau - \mu_g}{\sigma_g}\right)$, so $\Phi^{-1}(\text{FRR}) = \frac{\mu_g - \tau}{\sigma_g}$, giving:

$$y = \frac{\mu_g - \tau}{\sigma_g}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the parameter $\tau$:**

From the first equation: $\tau = \sigma_i \cdot x + \mu_i$.

Substituting into the second:

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{\mu_g - (\sigma_i x + \mu_i)}{\sigma_g} \\
  &= \frac{(\mu_g - \mu_i) - \sigma_i x}{\sigma_g} \\
  &= \frac{\mu_g - \mu_i}{\sigma_g} - \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_g} x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the linear form $y = mx + c$:**

$$\boxed{y = -\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_g}\, x + \frac{\mu_g - \mu_i}{\sigma_g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The DERIVATION transforms the *non-linear* FRR-vs-FAR relationship (a curve in linear-linear space) into a **straight line** on probit-probit axes. The slope is $-\sigma_i/\sigma_g$ and the intercept encodes the separability of the two distributions.

This explains why DET curves are linear when underlying scores are Gaussian — and why deviations from linearity in real DET plots diagnostically indicate *non-Gaussian* score distributions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Metric

| Application Scenario | Primary Metric | Secondary Metric | Rationale |
|----------------------|---------------|------------------|-----------|
| Forensic/Criminal ID | Rank-1 / CMC | FTER | Must find the right person in huge databases |
| Smartphone unlock | FRR (low) | FAR | User convenience dominates |
| Airport e-Gate | FAR (low) | Throughput | Security dominates; throughput matters for queues |
| National ID (e.g., Aadhaar) | EER | FTER, FTCR | Must work for entire population including elderly/manual workers |
| Border surveillance watchlist | Rank-N, FAR | Latency | Catch suspect regardless of position in candidate list |
| Physical access (office) | FAR | FRR | Tailgating/breach consequences outweigh user inconvenience |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biometric System Functional Architecture (Verification vs Identification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Presents Biometric Sample] --> B[Sensor Acquisition]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Module]
    C --> D[Matching Score Generator]
    
    D --> E{Identity Claim Provided?}
    E -->|Yes - Verification 1:1| F[Retrieve Single Template by User ID]
    E -->|No - Identification 1:N| G[Search All N Templates in Gallery]
    
    F --> H[Compute Similarity Score S]
    G --> H1[Compute N Similarity Scores]
    
    H --> I{Compare S with threshold tau}
    I -->|S greater or equal tau| J[ACCEPT - Genuine]
    I -->|S less than tau| K[REJECT - Impostor]
    
    H1 --> L[Rank Candidates by Score]
    L --> M{Top Score greater or equal tau}
    M -->|Yes| N[Output Rank-1 Identity]
    M -->|No| O[Output NO MATCH]
```

## 4.2 Performance Metric Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ScoreA[Genuine Score Distribution p_g] --> FRRnode[FRR Computation]
    ScoreB[Impostor Score Distribution p_i] --> FARnode[FAR Computation]
    
    ScoreA --> GARnode[GAR equals 1 minus FRR]
    
    FRRnode --> EERnode{EER Intersection Point}
    FARnode --> EERnode
    
    FRRnode --> ROCcurve[ROC Curve: GAR vs FAR]
    FARnode --> ROCcurve
    ROCcurve --> DETcurve[DET Curve: Probit-Probit Linearization]
    
    FRRnode --> FTERnode[FTER - Enrollment Failure]
    FARnode --> FTCRnode[FTCR - Capture Failure]
    
    CMCdata[Identification Top-k Results] --> CMCcurve[CMC Curve]
    
    EERnode --> Summary[Single Number System Accuracy]
    ROCcurve --> Summary
    DETcurve --> Summary
    CMCcurve --> Summary
```

## 4.3 Threshold Trade-off Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Low_Threshold_High_FAR_Low_FRR
        T1[Threshold tau1 LOW] --> F1[FAR HIGH]
        T1 --> R1[FRR LOW]
    end
    
    subgraph EER_Optimum
        T2[Threshold tauEER] --> F2[FAR equals FRR]
        T2 --> R2[FAR equals FRR]
    end
    
    subgraph High_Threshold_Low_FAR_High_FRR
        T3[Threshold tau3 HIGH] --> F3[FAR LOW]
        T3 --> R3[FRR HIGH]
    end
    
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three blocks represent the **continuous trade-off** between FAR and FRR. The middle block is the **operational sweet spot (EER)**, where both error types are minimised jointly. Movement of $\tau$ in either direction always increases *one* error rate at the expense of the other — a fundamental law of threshold-based biometric systems.

## 4.4 Identification vs Verification Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[VERIFICATION 1:1] --> V1[Input: Biometric + User ID Claim]
    V1 --> V2[Output: ACCEPT or REJECT]
    V2 --> V3[Metrics: FAR, FRR, EER]
    V2 --> V4[Use Case: Phone Unlock, ATM, e-Gate]
    
    I[IDENTIFICATION 1:N] --> I1[Input: Biometric Only]
    I1 --> I2[Output: Identity or NO MATCH]
    I2 --> I3[Metrics: Rank-k Accuracy, CMC, FAR, FRR]
    I2 --> I4[Use Case: Forensics, Watchlist, Aadhaar]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Differentiate between **verification** and **identification** in biometric systems with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification (1:1) | Identification (1:N) |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| User claim | **Required** (User ID, smart card) | **Not required** |
| Comparison type | One-to-one | One-to-many |
| Output | Binary (Accept/Reject) | Identity (or NO MATCH) |
| Computational cost | O(1) | O(N) |
| Example | Fingerprint unlock of phone | Face recognition of suspect in CCTV |

Verification *authenticates* a claimed identity; identification *establishs* an unknown identity. **(3 marks — 1 for claim requirement, 1 for output difference, 1 for example)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Define **FAR**, **FRR**, and **EER**. State the relationship between FAR and FRR as the decision threshold varies.

**Model Answer:**
- **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Probability that an impostor is incorrectly accepted.
- **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Probability that a genuine user is incorrectly rejected.
- **EER (Equal Error Rate):** The common value of FAR and FRR at the threshold where they are equal.

**Relationship:** As threshold $\tau$ increases, FAR decreases monotonically and FRR increases monotonically. They are inversely related — a trade-off curve. **(3 marks — 1 each for definitions, 1 for relationship)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** 
(a) Explain the block diagram of a generic biometric recognition system. Discuss the functions of the **enrolment** and **recognition** modules. **(7 marks)**
(b) Define **FAR**, **FRR**, and **EER**. For a biometric system with genuine scores following $\mathcal{N}(0.8, 0.05^2)$ and impostor scores following $\mathcal{N}(0.3, 0.08^2)$, determine the EER. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A generic biometric recognition system consists of the following blocks:

1. **Sensor Module:** Captures the raw biometric sample (e.g., optical fingerprint scanner, CMOS camera for face).
2. **Pre-processing Module:** Enhances quality (noise reduction, normalisation, alignment).
3. **Feature Extraction Module:** Converts the sample into a compact feature vector (e.g., minutiae for fingerprint, Eigenfaces for face).
4. **Template Database:** Stores enrolled feature templates with associated user IDs.
5. **Matching Module:** Computes a similarity/distance score between query and stored template(s).
6. **Decision Module:** Compares the score against a threshold to output Accept/Reject or Identity.

**Enrolment Mode:** The user's biometric is captured, processed, and the extracted template is *stored* in the database — the system learns the user. **Recognition Mode:** A live sample is matched against templates to verify or identify the user. **(7 marks — 1 per block, 1 each for enrolment/recognition description, 1 for summary)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **FAR** $= P(\text{Accept} \mid \text{Impostor})$
- **FRR** $= P(\text{Reject} \mid \text{Genuine})$
- **EER** $= \text{FAR}(\tau^*) = \text{FRR}(\tau^*)$

**Step 1 — Compute the EER threshold (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{EER} &= \frac{\sigma_i \mu_g + \sigma_g \mu_i}{\sigma_g + \sigma_i} \\
&= \frac{(0.08)(0.8) + (0.05)(0.3)}{0.05 + 0.08} \\
&= \frac{0.064 + 0.015}{0.13} \\
&= \frac{0.079}{0.13} \\
&\approx 0.6077
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute EER (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{\tau_{EER} - \mu_i}{\sigma_i} = \frac{0.6077 - 0.3}{0.08} = 3.846 \\
\text{EER} &= Q(3.846) \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-5} = 0.006\%
\end{aligned}$$

This is an exceptionally accurate system (typical of high-quality iris recognisers). **(7 marks total)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
(a) Explain **ROC**, **DET**, and **CMC** curves. State why the DET curve is preferred over the ROC curve in biometric literature. **(7 marks)**
(b) A face recognition system has FTER = 2% and FTCR = 1%. For 10,000 enrolled users, find the expected number of users who can be **fully authenticated** at first attempt. If the FAR is 0.001% and the system processes 1,000,000 transactions per day, how many impostor acceptances are expected per day? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic):** Plots GAR (= 1 − FRR) on the y-axis against FAR on the x-axis as $\tau$ varies. Standard classification plot. **(2 marks)**

**DET (Detection Error Trade-off):** Plots FRR vs FAR on a **probit-probit (normal-deviate) scale**. For Gaussian score distributions, the trade-off becomes a straight line, making it easier to visualise system differences. **(2 marks)**

**CMC (Cumulative Match Characteristic):** Identification-specific curve plotting $\text{CMC}(k) = P(\text{correct ID in top-}k)$ vs rank $k$. Used in 1:N identification. **(2 marks)**

**Why DET is preferred:** Linearises Gaussian score distributions (slope $-\sigma_i/\sigma_g$), provides a wider dynamic range via log-scale axes, allows compact comparison of multiple algorithms on one chart, and visually represents EER at the (1,1) bisector. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Effective enrolled users after FTER (2 marks):**

$$\text{Successful enrollments} = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.02) = 9{,}800 \text{ users}$$

**Step 2 — Successful first-attempt captures after FTCR (2 marks):**

$$\text{Successful captures} = 9{,}800 \times (1 - 0.01) = 9{,}702 \text{ users}$$

**Step 3 — Expected impostor acceptances per day (3 marks):**

$$\text{False Accepts/day} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{0.001}{100} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 10^{-5} = 10 \text{ transactions/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - In Part (b), **do not apply FAR to the captured subset** — FAR applies to the *matching* stage, not the capture stage. Many students wrongly multiply $9{,}702$ by FAR instead of $1{,}000{,}000$. (Lose 2 marks)
> - FTER and FTCR are **independent** failure modes and must be **multiplied** in sequence, not added. (Lose 1 mark)
> - In Part A(b), forgetting to convert $0.001\%$ to a probability ($10^{-5}$) is a common arithmetic trap. (Lose 1 mark)
> - In ROC/DET questions, **never** confuse GAR with FAR on the y-axis — GAR is "True Positive Rate", not the false rate. (Lose 2 marks)
> - For EER derivation, students often write the wrong Gaussian tail formula ($P(X<\tau)$ vs $P(X\geq\tau)$) — use the **Q-function consistently** for similarity-based systems. (Lose 2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for last-night revision):**

- [x] **Verification = 1:1 (with claim), Identification = 1:N (no claim).**
- [x] Matching score $S$ is compared against threshold $\tau$ to make a decision.
- [x] **FAR + FRR trade-off** is governed by $\tau$; they move in **opposite directions**.
- [x] **EER** is the single number summarising a system's accuracy — find $\tau$ where FAR = FRR.
- [x] EER threshold formula (Gaussian): $\tau_{EER} = \dfrac{\sigma_i \mu_g + \sigma_g \mu_i}{\sigma_g + \sigma_i}$
- [x] **GAR = 1 − FRR**; always report GAR for genuine performance.
- [x] **ROC curve:** GAR (y) vs FAR (x) on linear axes.
- [x] **DET curve:** FRR (y) vs FAR (x) on probit-probit axes — linearises Gaussian distributions.
- [x] **CMC curve:** Identification-specific, plots Rank-$k$ accuracy vs $k$.
- [x] **FTER** (enrollment failure) and **FTCR** (capture failure) are **independent** multiplicative losses.
- [x] Low FAR ↔ high security (forensics, e-Gates). Low FRR ↔ high convenience (consumer devices).
- [x] **DET is preferred over ROC** in KTU/biometric literature because of linearization and wider dynamic range.
- [x] For 1:N identification, the **gallery size N** degrades performance; multi-modal fusion mitigates this.
- [x] The **Q-function** is used to compute Gaussian tail probabilities — keep formulas consistent for similarity-based systems.
- [x] EER is **not** the only metric — a system can have a low EER but terrible behaviour at extreme thresholds; always pair with ROC/DET.
- [x] **Rank-1 accuracy** is the gold standard for identification; Rank-N is trivially 1.0 in closed-set.
- [x] Score distributions are typically **not perfectly Gaussian** in practice — DET curves show curvature that diagnoses the deviation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FTE Rate, EER and ATV Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FTE Rate, EER and ATV Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Failure to Enroll (FTE) Rate

**Formal Definition:** The **Failure to Enroll (FTE) Rate** is the proportion (or percentage) of users in the target population who cannot be successfully enrolled into a biometric system after a reasonable number of attempts, due to unacceptable quality of the captured biometric samples, physiological limitations, or environmental conditions.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\text{FTE Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of users who fail to enroll}}{\text{Total number of users attempted for enrollment}} \times 100\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** FTE is a *pre-deployment* metric. It is measured **before** a user ever attempts verification. A high FTE rate implies the system is **inaccessible** to a significant portion of the population, which is a serious usability and inclusivity concern (e.g., fingerprint systems failing for manual laborers with worn fingerprints).

**Real-World Analogy — The Passport Office Counter:**
Imagine a passport office using iris recognition for new applicants. Out of **1,000** applicants, **30** cannot place their eye properly in the scanner (due to medical conditions, very dark irises, or bright sunlight glare). The system simply cannot generate a usable template for them. They must be routed to a manual counter. Those 30 represent the **FTE population**, and the FTE rate is **3%**. If this rate rises above an acceptable threshold (say 5%), the airport authority would reject the entire biometric kiosk project.

---

### 1.2 Equal Error Rate (EER)

**Formal Definition:** The **Equal Error Rate (EER)** is the specific operating point on a biometric system's Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve where the **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** equals the **False Rejection Rate (FRR)**. It is the single scalar value where:

$$
\text{FAR}(\tau) = \text{FRR}(\tau)
$$

at the threshold $\tau = \tau_{\text{EER}}$. A **lower EER indicates a more accurate and robust biometric system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Insight:** When asked "Which is the best system?", examiners expect the answer: *"The system with the **lowest EER** and the **smallest area under the ROC curve (AUC)**, while also maintaining a low FTE rate."*

**Real-World Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club:**
A bouncer (the biometric system) decides whom to let in. He must balance two errors:
- **False Acceptance (FA):** Letting an unauthorized person (impostor) inside.
- **False Rejection (FR):** Refusing a genuine VIP member.

If the bouncer is **too lenient** (low threshold) → many FA, low FR.
If the bouncer is **too strict** (high threshold) → low FA, many FR.
The **sweet spot** — where the rate of letting in impostors *equals* the rate of rejecting genuine members — is the **EER**. The bouncer with the **lowest EER** is the most balanced and accurate.

---

### 1.3 ATV Rate (Average Verification / Authentication Throughput Rate)

**Formal Definition (KTU Module 1 Context):** The **ATV Rate** denotes the **Average Throughput** of a biometric system — the number of authentication (verification) decisions the system can reliably produce per unit time, typically measured in **transactions per second (TPS)** or **users per minute**. It is a measure of the **operational speed and scalability** of the matcher subsystem, encompassing capture time, feature extraction, template comparison, and decision output.

$$
\text{ATV Rate} = \frac{N_{\text{successful verifications}}}{T_{\text{total observation window}}} \;\; [\text{users/sec}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation Note:** In the context of KTU Module 1, ATV Rate is treated as a **real-time performance benchmark**. A high EER is meaningless if the system takes 30 seconds per authentication — usability collapses. ATV Rate captures the **temporal dimension** of biometric performance.

**Real-World Analogy — The Toll Booth Lane:**
A toll booth using license-plate recognition:
- The **scanner** is the sensor.
- The **camera + OCR** is the feature extractor.
- The **database lookup** is the matcher.
- The number of cars processed per minute is the **ATV Rate**.

If the camera is slow, the throughput drops and a queue forms. Even if the recognition is 100% accurate (EER ≈ 0%), the lane is operationally useless. **ATV Rate quantifies this real-world usability.**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Biometric Performance Trinity

In a complete biometric system evaluation, three orthogonal metrics are required to characterize performance fully:

| **Dimension** | **What it measures** | **Metric** |
|---|---|---|
| Accessibility / Inclusivity | Can the user even enroll? | **FTE Rate** |
| Accuracy / Reliability | Once enrolled, how often is the decision correct? | **EER** (with FAR/FRR) |
| Speed / Throughput | How fast can the system decide? | **ATV Rate** |

### 2.2 Why FTE Matters — The "Garbage In" Problem

A biometric template is built from samples captured during enrollment. If samples are too noisy (dry finger, dark iris, poor lighting), the feature extractor produces a low-quality template. The matcher will then reject *every* genuine attempt by that user — a phenomenon known as a **"Piggy in the Python" failure mode** (a "Fred" who always fails). FTE is the **upfront filter** that prevents Freds from entering the active user population.

**Engineering implication:** Designers can lower FTE by:
1. Using **multi-modal biometrics** (combine face + fingerprint).
2. Using **better sensors** (e.g., multi-spectral fingerprint readers).
3. Allowing **template re-enrollment** with relaxed quality thresholds.
4. Providing **acoustic / visual user guidance** during capture.

### 2.3 The EER Mechanics — Threshold Sweep

A biometric matcher outputs a **similarity score** $S \in [0, 1]$ between the probe and the gallery template. A **threshold** $\tau$ converts the score into a binary decision:

- If $S \geq \tau$ → **Accept** (claim identity)
- If $S < \tau$ → **Reject**

As $\tau$ varies from $0$ to $1$:
- $\text{FAR}(\tau)$ is a **monotonically non-decreasing** function.
- $\text{FRR}(\tau)$ is a **monotonically non-increasing** function.

By the **Intermediate Value Theorem**, since FAR starts near 0 and ends near 1, and FRR starts near 1 and ends near 0, they **must intersect** at least once. That intersection (under standard unimodal assumptions) is the **EER**.

### 2.4 ATV Rate — The Engineering Reality Check

In a real deployment (e.g., Aadhaar authentication at a ration shop, or a stadium turnstile), the matcher throughput is bounded by:
- Sensor capture latency ($T_c$)
- Feature extraction time ($T_f$)
- Template comparison time ($T_m$)
- Communication / network round-trip ($T_n$)
- Decision display / actuator time ($T_d$)

$$
\text{ATV Rate}_{\max} = \frac{1}{T_c + T_f + T_m + T_n + T_d}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Multi-user Parallelism:** In batch or 1:N identification systems, ATV Rate is also influenced by the **gallery size** $N$ and the **indexing algorithm** (e.g., kd-tree, locality-sensitive hashing) used to prune comparisons. An efficient indexer keeps $T_m \ll O(N)$.

---

### 📋 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FTE Rate | $\text{FTE} = \dfrac{N_{\text{fail}}}{N_{\text{attempt}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measured at enrollment stage; target typically **< 1%** |
| 2 | FAR (FMR) | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \dfrac{\text{FP}}{N_{\text{impostor}}}$ | ratio / % | Impostor attempts accepted |
| 3 | FRR (FNMR) | $\text{FRR} = \dfrac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}} = \dfrac{\text{FN}}{N_{\text{genuine}}}$ | ratio / % | Genuine attempts rejected |
| 4 | GAR (TAR) | $\text{GAR} = 1 - \text{FRR}$ | ratio / % | True Acceptance Rate |
| 5 | EER condition | $\text{FAR}(\tau^{\ast}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{\ast})$ | **%** | $\tau^{\ast}$ is the EER threshold |
| 6 | EER numeric | $\text{EER} = \text{FAR}(\tau^{\ast})$ | **%** | Lower is better |
| 7 | FTE-to-FRR relation | $\text{FRR}_{\text{operational}} = \text{FRR}_{\text{enrolled}} \cdot (1 - \text{FTE})$ | ratio | Real-world FRR includes FTE loss |
| 8 | ATV Rate | $\text{ATV} = \dfrac{N_{\text{verifications}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | **users/sec** | Higher is better |
| 9 | Max ATV (latency bound) | $\text{ATV}_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{\sum T_i}$ | users/sec | Bottleneck = slowest stage |
| 10 | ROC AUC | $\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}\, d(\text{FPR})$ | unitless | Smaller AUC = better |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Many students confuse **FTE with FTC (Failure to Capture)**. FTE is at *enrollment*; FTC is at every *live capture* during verification. KTU examiners test this distinction frequently.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to EER

**Setup.** Suppose a biometric verification system is tested on a population. The matcher produces similarity scores. By varying the decision threshold $\tau$, we collect counts of TP, FP, TN, FN. Define:

$$
\text{FAR}(\tau) = \frac{\text{FP}(\tau)}{\text{FP}(\tau) + \text{TN}(\tau)}, \qquad \text{FRR}(\tau) = \frac{\text{FN}(\tau)}{\text{FN}(\tau) + \text{TP}(\tau)}
$$

**Step 1 — Monotonicity of FAR.**
As $\tau$ increases, fewer impostor scores exceed $\tau$, so $\text{FP}(\tau)$ is non-increasing. The impostor population $\text{FP} + \text{TN}$ is constant. Therefore, $\text{FAR}(\tau)$ is **monotonically non-increasing** in $\tau$.

**Step 2 — Monotonicity of FRR.**
Similarly, as $\tau$ increases, fewer genuine scores exceed $\tau$, so $\text{TP}(\tau)$ drops and $\text{FN}(\tau)$ rises. Hence $\text{FRR}(\tau)$ is **monotonically non-increasing** in $\tau$? No — wait, correct statement:

$$
\text{FRR}(\tau) = \frac{\text{FN}(\tau)}{\text{FN}(\tau) + \text{TP}(\tau)}
$$

As $\tau$ increases, $\text{TP}(\tau)$ decreases, $\text{FN}(\tau)$ increases, so $\text{FRR}(\tau)$ is **monotonically non-decreasing** in $\tau$.

**Step 3 — Endpoint values.**

$$
\text{FAR}(0) = 1, \quad \text{FAR}(1) = 0, \quad \text{FRR}(0) = 0, \quad \text{FRR}(1) = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Define the gap function.**

$$
g(\tau) = \text{FAR}(\tau) - \text{FRR}(\tau)
$$

Then:

$$
g(0) = 1 - 0 = +1, \qquad g(1) = 0 - 1 = -1
$$

By the **Intermediate Value Theorem**, since $g$ is continuous (assuming continuous score distributions) and changes sign, there exists $\tau^{\ast} \in (0,1)$ such that:

$$
g(\tau^{\ast}) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{FAR}(\tau^{\ast}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{\ast}) \;\;\triangleq\;\; \text{EER}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical computation (Discrete case).** In practice, scores are discretized. We sweep $\tau$ over all observed score values, compute FAR and FRR at each, and find the $\tau^{\ast}$ minimizing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau^{\ast} = \arg\min_{\tau} \;\big\vert \text{FAR}(\tau) - \text{FRR}(\tau) \big\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

The EER is then read off as either value at that threshold.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Finding the EER

A fingerprint matcher was tested with **1,000 genuine attempts** and **10,000 impostor attempts**. The following table was generated by sweeping the threshold $\tau$:

| **Threshold $\tau$** | **TP** | **FN** | **FP** | **TN** | **FAR (%)** | **FRR (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | 950 | 50 | 8,500 | 1,500 | 85.00 | 5.00 |
| 0.40 | 880 | 120 | 3,200 | 6,800 | 32.00 | 12.00 |
| 0.55 | 800 | 200 | 850 | 9,150 | 8.50 | 20.00 |
| 0.65 | 700 | 300 | 220 | 9,780 | 2.20 | 30.00 |
| 0.80 | 400 | 600 | 15 | 9,985 | 0.15 | 60.00 |

**Solution.**

FAR and FRR cross between $\tau = 0.40$ (FAR=32, FRR=12) and $\tau = 0.55$ (FAR=8.5, FRR=20). Linear interpolation gives the EER threshold and value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau^{\ast} &= 0.40 + (0.55 - 0.40) \cdot \frac{32.00 - 12.00}{(32.00 - 12.00) + (20.00 - 8.50)} \\
&= 0.40 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{20.00}{20.00 + 11.50} \\
&= 0.40 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{20.00}{31.50} \\
&= 0.40 + 0.15 \cdot 0.6349 \\
&= 0.40 + 0.0952 \\
&= 0.4952
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EER} \approx 16.83\,\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Note:** A 14-mark EER question in KTU 2024 typically allocates **3 marks** for setting up the FAR/FRR formula, **6 marks** for the threshold sweep table, and **5 marks** for the interpolation and final numeric answer.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Metric vs. System Stage

| **System Stage** | **Relevant Metric** | **Failure Consequence** | **Typical Target (KTU 2024)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Enrollment** | FTE Rate | User is permanently excluded | $< 1\%$ to $< 5\%$ |
| **Live Capture** | FTC Rate | Verification aborts, retry needed | $< 0.5\%$ |
| **Matching** | FAR, FRR, EER | Security breach / usability loss | EER $< 1\%$ (high-security) |
| **Decision Output** | ATV Rate | User-perceived latency, queueing | $\geq 1$ user/sec (turnstile) |
| **Long-term Drift** | Template Aging | Gradual FRR rise | Re-enroll every 1–3 years |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROC Curve with EER Operating Point

The following Mermaid block renders the conceptual ROC curve showing the trade-off between FAR and FRR, with the EER intersection highlighted.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Low Threshold tau] --> B[High FAR, Low FRR]
    C[Medium Threshold tau] --> D[EER Point: FAR = FRR]
    E[High Threshold tau] --> F[Low FAR, High FRR]
    D --> G[System Accuracy Scalar]
    G --> H[Compare Multiple Systems]
    H --> I[Select Lowest EER Winner]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Biometric Performance Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User Population] --> B[Enrollment Module]
    B --> C{Quality OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Counted as FTE]
    C -- Yes --> E[Template Database]
    E --> F[Verification Module]
    F --> G[Live Capture]
    G --> H[Feature Extraction]
    H --> I[Matcher Score]
    I --> J{Threshold tau}
    J -- Accept --> K[Genuine Acceptance]
    J -- Reject --> L[False Rejection FRR]
    P[Impostor Attempt] --> F
    P --> Q{Threshold tau}
    Q -- Accept --> R[False Acceptance FAR]
    Q -- Reject --> S[True Rejection]
    T[Stopwatch] --> F
    F --> U[Compute ATV Rate users per sec]
```

### 4.3 Decision Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Threshold Increase] --> Y[FAR Decreases]
    X --> Z[FRR Increases]
    Y --> AA[Security Up]
    Z --> AB[Usability Down]
    AA --> AC[Find EER Sweet Spot]
    AB --> AC
    AC --> AD[Optimal Operating Point]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The trade-off between security (low FAR) and usability (low FRR) is the central engineering challenge in biometric threshold selection. The EER is the **scalar summary** of this trade-off, while the FTE is the *accessibility gate* that must be passed *before* this trade-off even becomes relevant. The ATV Rate ensures the entire pipeline is operationally viable in real-time.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Failure to Enroll (FTE) Rate" in a biometric system. Mention two practical causes of a high FTE rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Failure to Enroll (FTE) Rate is the percentage of users in the target population who, after a specified number of attempts, cannot be successfully enrolled into the biometric system due to inability to provide an acceptable-quality biometric sample.

**Causes (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Poor physiological condition of the user** — e.g., worn fingerprints of manual laborers, cataract-affected irises, very dark eyes resisting IR illumination, or amputees lacking the required biometric trait.
2. **Environmental / sensor limitations** — e.g., dirty scanner platen, low ambient lighting for face recognition, or cold/dry fingers producing low-contrast ridge patterns.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by Equal Error Rate (EER)? Why is a system with EER = 0.5% preferred over a system with EER = 4%?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Equal Error Rate (EER) is the operating point on the ROC curve of a biometric system at which the False Acceptance Rate (FAR) equals the False Rejection Rate (FRR). It is computed by varying the decision threshold $\tau$ until the equality $\text{FAR}(\tau^{\ast}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{\ast})$ is satisfied. The EER serves as a single scalar index of the system's overall accuracy.

**Justification (1 mark):** A system with EER = 0.5% makes both types of errors (false acceptances and false rejections) far less frequently than a system with EER = 4%. Hence, the 0.5% EER system is more accurate, more secure, and more usable, making it the preferred choice in almost all practical deployments (assuming comparable FTE and ATV).

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Q1]**

**(a)** Explain the FTE Rate metric in detail. Discuss how FTE impacts the operational False Rejection Rate of a deployed system. Derive the relation between operational FRR and the enrolled-population FRR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multimodal biometric system uses face + fingerprint. The face subsystem has an FTE rate of 4%, and the fingerprint subsystem has an FTE rate of 6%. Assuming independence, calculate the combined FTE rate if a user is considered enrolled only if **both** modalities succeed. Compare this with a parallel "OR" fusion strategy where enrollment succeeds if **either** modality succeeds. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FTE and its impact on operational FRR (7 marks):**

FTE is measured at the **enrollment stage** and represents the fraction of the population that *cannot even enter* the system. Users in this FTE group will never reach the matching stage — they are filtered out before any verification.

Let:
- $N$ = total target population.
- $N_{\text{fail}}$ = number who fail to enroll → FTE = $N_{\text{fail}}/N$.
- $N_{\text{enrolled}} = N - N_{\text{fail}}$.
- Among enrolled users, the matcher produces an FRR of $f$ (e.g., 0.02 = 2%).
