The **operational FRR** $F_{\text{op}}$ is computed over the *entire* population including FTE failures (who contribute a "rejection" by default):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{op}} &= \frac{N_{\text{fail}} + N_{\text{enrolled}} \cdot f}{N} \\
&= \text{FTE} + (1 - \text{FTE}) \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation expanded:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{op}} &= \frac{N_{\text{fail}}}{N} + \frac{N_{\text{enrolled}}}{N} \cdot \frac{N_{\text{enrolled,rejected}}}{N_{\text{enrolled}}} \\
&= \text{FTE} + (1 - \text{FTE}) \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, if FTE = 2% and enrolled-population FRR $f = 3\%$:

$$
F_{\text{op}} = 0.02 + (1 - 0.02)(0.03) = 0.02 + 0.0294 = 0.0494 \approx 4.94\%
$$

Thus, FTE **amplifies the apparent FRR** of a deployed system. Designers must report FRR *only over the enrolled cohort* to enable fair system comparison.

**(b) Multimodal FTE calculation (7 marks):**

**AND-fusion (both must enroll):**
Probability a single user fails on face: $P(F_{\text{face}}) = 0.04$.
Probability a single user fails on fingerprint: $P(F_{\text{fp}}) = 0.06$.
Assuming independence, probability of failing *at least one*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{FTE}_{\text{AND}}) &= 1 - P(\text{both succeed}) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.04)(1 - 0.06) \\
&= 1 - (0.96)(0.94) \\
&= 1 - 0.9024 \\
&= 0.0976 = 9.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**OR-fusion (either suffices):**
A user is considered enrolled if face **OR** fingerprint succeeds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Enrolled}_{\text{OR}}) &= 1 - P(\text{both fail}) \\
&= 1 - (0.04)(0.06) \\
&= 1 - 0.0024 \\
&= 0.9976
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the OR-fusion FTE rate is:

$$
\text{FTE}_{\text{OR}} = 1 - 0.9976 = 0.0024 = 0.24\%
$$

**Comparison:** OR-fusion is dramatically more inclusive (FTE = 0.24% vs 9.76%) but requires both sensors and processing pipelines at every verification, increasing cost and potentially reducing ATV Rate. KTU examiners look for this **trade-off discussion** in the answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Q1 Alt]**

**(a)** Define and derive the Equal Error Rate (EER) from the ROC curve. Show that there always exists at least one threshold $\tau^{\ast}$ at which $\text{FAR}(\tau^{\ast}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{\ast})$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A voice biometric system was tested with **2,000 genuine** and **20,000 impostor** attempts. The threshold sweep yielded:

| $\tau$ | FAR (%) | FRR (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.30 | 40.0 | 5.0 |
| 0.45 | 18.0 | 9.0 |
| 0.55 | 9.5 | 15.0 |
| 0.65 | 4.0 | 22.0 |

Estimate the EER and the corresponding threshold $\tau^{\ast}$ using linear interpolation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EER derivation (7 marks):**

*(As derived in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

The gap function $g(\tau) = \text{FAR}(\tau) - \text{FRR}(\tau)$ is continuous in $\tau$ over $[0,1]$, with $g(0) = +1$ and $g(1) = -1$. By the Intermediate Value Theorem, $\exists\, \tau^{\ast} \in (0,1)$ such that $g(\tau^{\ast}) = 0$, i.e., $\text{FAR}(\tau^{\ast}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{\ast})$. The common value is the EER. (Full step-by-step: 7 marks.)

**(b) Numerical EER (7 marks):**

FAR and FRR cross between $\tau = 0.45$ (FAR=18, FRR=9) and $\tau = 0.55$ (FAR=9.5, FRR=15). Linear interpolation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau^{\ast} &= 0.45 + (0.55 - 0.45) \cdot \frac{18.0 - 9.0}{(18.0 - 9.0) + (15.0 - 9.5)} \\
&= 0.45 + 0.10 \cdot \frac{9.0}{9.0 + 5.5} \\
&= 0.45 + 0.10 \cdot \frac{9.0}{14.5} \\
&= 0.45 + 0.10 \cdot 0.6207 \\
&= 0.45 + 0.0621 \\
&= 0.5121
\end{aligned}
$$

The EER value (interpolated FAR or FRR at $\tau^{\ast}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EER} &= 18.0 + (9.5 - 18.0) \cdot 0.6207 \\
&= 18.0 - 8.5 \cdot 0.6207 \\
&= 18.0 - 5.276 \\
&= 12.72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau^{\ast} \approx 0.512, \qquad \text{EER} \approx 12.72\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing FAR and FRR endpoints.** FAR(0) = 1, FRR(0) = 0 (NOT the reverse). Many students swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting the 100× factor** when reporting EER as a percentage vs. ratio.
> 3. **Using the wrong interpolation denominator** in numerical EER problems. The denominator must be the **sum** of the differences, not just the FAR difference.
> 4. **Failing to mention units** in ATV Rate (must say "users per second" or "TPS").
> 5. **Mixing FTE with FTC** in definitions — FTE = enrollment, FTC = live capture.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **FTE Rate** = Pre-deployment inclusivity metric. Measured at *enrollment*. Caused by sensor/physiology mismatch. Affects operational FRR via $F_{\text{op}} = \text{FTE} + (1-\text{FTE}) \cdot f$.
- ✅ **EER** = Scalar accuracy metric. Found at $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ on the ROC curve. **Lower EER = better system**. Exists by the Intermediate Value Theorem.
- ✅ **ATV Rate** = Throughput metric (users/sec). Bounded by the slowest pipeline stage. High EER is meaningless if ATV is too low for real-time use.
- ✅ **Multimodal FTE:** AND-fusion *increases* FTE; OR-fusion *decreases* FTE. Trade-off: cost vs. inclusivity.
- ✅ **FAR + TPR relationship:** $\text{GAR} = 1 - \text{FRR}$. ROC plots typically show TPR (= GAR) vs. FAR.
- ✅ **FTE vs FTC:** FTE = enrollment failure; FTC = live capture failure during verification. KTU tests this distinction.
- ✅ **Operational FRR** must be reported *with* FTE included for honest field-deployment claims.
- ✅ **ROC AUC** is another scalar accuracy summary; the system with **minimum AUC and minimum EER** is preferred.
- ✅ **ATV Rate bottleneck** identification is a common 3-mark sub-question in KTU 2024 papers.
- ✅ Always specify the **threshold value** $\tau^{\ast}$ when reporting an EER — examiners award a separate mark for it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Biometric Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Biometric Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biometric Systems** are automated pattern-recognition systems that identify or verify the identity of a living person based on their physiological (fingerprint, face, iris) or behavioral (gait, keystroke dynamics, voice) characteristics. An **Application of a Biometric System** refers to any real-world deployment scenario where such a system is integrated into a workflow to provide a security, identification, or transactional function. These applications are broadly classified into three operational categories: **Verification (1:1 matching)**, **Identification (1:N matching)**, and **Watch-list Screening (negative identification)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Under the 2024 scheme, "Applications of Biometric Systems" is the foundational descriptive topic that contextualizes *why* every subsequent module (sensor design, feature extraction, matching algorithms) exists. The examiner expects students to clearly map a biometric **trait** to its **operational use case**, justifying the choice based on universality, distinctiveness, permanence, and circumvention resistance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a biometric system as a **highly advanced version of a personal name-tag that you cannot lose, lend, or forge**.

- **Traditional systems** (like passwords, ID cards, PINs) are like giving a key to a building — anyone holding the key can enter.
- **Biometric systems**, in contrast, are like a door that recognizes *who you are by your face* — no key to copy, no card to steal, no PIN to forget.

> **Real-World Analogy — The Modern Airport:**
> When you walk through e-gates at Dubai, Singapore, or Delhi International Airports, you walk past a camera that reads your face and matches it against a database of **millions of pre-registered travelers** in under **2 seconds**. The system does not "ask" who you are — it **recognizes** you. This is an application of biometric identification (1:N mode). Conversely, when you unlock your phone using a fingerprint to approve a UPI payment, the system compares your fingerprint to the **single template stored on your device** — this is verification (1:1 mode).

### 1.3 Standard Biometric Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **FRR (False Rejection Rate)** — also called FNMR
- **FAR (False Acceptance Rate)** — also called FMR
- **EER (Equal Error Rate)** — the operating point where FAR = FRR
- **FTE (Failure to Enroll) Rate**
- **FTA (Failure to Acquire) Rate**
- **CER (Crossover Error Rate)** — industry synonym for EER

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition for Exam:**
> **Verification (1:1)** answers the question: *"Is this person who they claim to be?"* — e.g., phone unlock, Aadhaar OTP.
> **Identification (1:N)** answers: *"Who is this person?"* — e.g., criminal AFIS lookup, missing person search.
> **Screening (1:N negative)** answers: *"Is this person on a watch-list?"* — e.g., border-crossing watch-list matching.

### 1.4 The Two Fundamental Operating Modes

| Mode | Match Ratio | Question Answered | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Verification | 1 : 1 | "Is this the claimed user?" | Phone unlock, ATM, KYC |
| Identification | 1 : N | "Who is this person?" | Forensics, National ID |
| Screening | 1 : N (negative) | "Is this person a threat?" | Airport watch-lists |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" of Biometric Applications

**Step 1 — Establish the Need (Why):**
Conventional authentication (passwords, tokens) suffers from three inherent weaknesses:
1. *Can be shared* (non-repudiation failure)
2. *Can be forgotten or lost* (usability failure)
3. *Can be guessed, phished, or replayed* (security failure)

Biometrics solve all three because the credential is **intrinsically bound** to the human body.

**Step 2 — Choose the Modality (How):**
Each application imposes different constraints. The choice of trait is driven by:
- *Throughput requirements* (passport control needs speed → face/iris)
- *Accuracy requirements* (forensic match needs certainty → fingerprint/iris)
- *Environmental constraints* (noisy factory floor → finger vein, not voice)
- *User cooperation level* (cooperative vs. non-cooperative vs. covert)

**Step 3 — Select the Operating Mode (How):**
- Cooperative + small user base → **Verification (1:1)**
- Cooperative + large public database → **Identification (1:N)**
- Adversarial / non-cooperative + critical security → **Screening + Surveillance**

**Step 4 — Deploy the Architecture (How):**
- *Standalone* — embedded (e.g., phone fingerprint sensor)
- *LAN-tethered* — office access control
- *Cloud-based* — national ID, banking KYC
- *Mobile/wearable* — on-device authentication

**Step 5 — Evaluate Performance (Why it matters for exam):**
All applications are graded on the **FMR / FNMR trade-off curve** (ROC). The single most-cited number is the **EER**, where False Accepts equal False Rejects.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner may test for this topic. Memorize the symbol conventions — they appear verbatim in board question papers.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Interpretation | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| $\text{FMR}$ | $\frac{\text{Number of Impostor Accepts}}{\text{Total Impostor Attempts}}$ | Probability of a false match | Expressed as **%** |
| $\text{FNMR}$ | $\frac{\text{Number of Genuine Rejects}}{\text{Total Genuine Attempts}}$ | Probability of a false non-match | Expressed as **%** |
| $\text{FAR}$ | Same as FMR, used in verification contexts | False acceptance — wrong person accepted | **%** |
| $\text{FRR}$ | Same as FNMR, used in verification contexts | False rejection — right person rejected | **%** |
| $\text{EER}$ | Operating point where $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ | Equal Error Rate | **%** (lower is better) |
| $\text{CER}$ | Synonym for EER (industry term) | Crossover Error Rate | **%** |
| $\text{FTE}$ | $\frac{\text{Users failing to enroll}}{\text{Total users}}$ | Failure to Enroll | **%** (ideal $<$ **2%**) |
| $\text{FTA}$ | $\frac{\text{Attempts failing at acquisition}}{\text{Total attempts}}$ | Failure to Acquire | **%** |
| $\text{GAR}$ | $1 - \text{FRR}$ | Genuine Acceptance Rate | **%** (higher is better) |
| $\text{Accuracy}$ | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Standard classifier accuracy | **%** |
| $\text{Throughput}$ | $\frac{N_{\text{users processed}}}{T_{\text{interval}}}$ | Users handled per unit time | **users/min** |
| $N_{\text{comparisons}}$ | $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ for identification (1:N) | Pairwise comparisons in 1:N search | **Count** |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In deployment planning, the FAR and FRR values are used to compute the **expected number of impostor entries per day** in a high-traffic system. For instance, a metro station scanning 1 million passengers/day with FAR = 0.0001% would still let through **1 impostor per day**, making watch-list screening a non-trivial matching problem.

### 2.3 The 12 Major Application Domains (KTU High-Yield List)

> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 KTU syllabus specifically expects students to enumerate application domains with a one-line justification for the choice of modality. Master the following 12.

1. **Aadhaar (UIDAI) — National Identity** — Iris + fingerprint + face for 1.4 billion residents (1:N identification)
2. **Passport & Visa Issuance** — Face + fingerprint enrolment, 1:1 verification at border
3. **Voter Registration & De-duplication** — Fingerprint to prevent duplicate entries in electoral rolls
4. **Driver's License** — Fingerprint + face, linked to RTO database
5. **Border Control / Immigration (e-Gates)** — Face + iris for fast-track lanes
6. **Criminal Identification & Forensics (AFIS / IAFIS)** — Latent fingerprint matching 1:N against criminal database
7. **Banking & Financial KYC** — Fingerprint / face for account opening, transaction signing
8. **Healthcare & Patient ID** — Palm vein / iris to eliminate duplicate medical records
9. **Mobile Device Unlocking** — Fingerprint / face / iris on consumer smartphones
10. **Enterprise / Corporate Access Control** — Fingerprint or face for office entry, logical IT login
11. **Time & Attendance** — Fingerprint to prevent "buddy punching"
12. **Surveillance & Public Safety** — Face recognition CCTV for crowd monitoring, missing child recovery

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Impostor Pass-Through in a Biometric Application

**Context:** A biometric application (e.g., airport e-gate using face recognition) processes $N$ travellers per day. Given a False Acceptance Rate (FAR) of $f$, derive the expected number of impostors admitted per day.

**Step 1 — Define the binomial random variable.**
Let $X$ be the number of impostors (people not enrolled in the system but presenting a sample) admitted through the gate in a single day.

**Step 2 — Identify the parameters.**
Each traveller presenting to the gate is an independent Bernoulli trial. The probability of being wrongly accepted (i.e., being an impostor and being accepted) is $f = \text{FAR}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Binomial distribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &\sim \text{Binomial}(N, f) \\
\text{Expected impostor admissions: } E[X] &= N \cdot f \\
\text{Variance: } \text{Var}(X) &= N \cdot f \cdot (1 - f)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (the kind KTU loves).**
Suppose an airport gate serves $N = 50{,}000$ passengers per day, and the deployed face-recognition system has $\text{FAR} = 0.0001$ (i.e., $1$ in $10^6$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= 50{,}000 \times 0.0001 \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 5 \text{ impostors per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic explained:** The FAR is given in fractional form, not percentage. To convert a percentage, divide by **100**. The expected impostor count is the product of the volume and the per-trial error probability, which is precisely the mean of the binomial distribution.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Identification Complexity for 1:N Search

**Context:** In identification mode (1:N), the system compares a probe against every template in the database. Show how the number of comparisons grows with $N$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate pairwise comparisons.**
For a database of $N$ enrolled templates, a single probe is compared to all $N$ templates:

$$
C_{\text{one probe}} = N
$$

**Step 2 — Total comparisons over the day.**
If the application processes $M$ probes in a day (each from a different unknown person):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= M \cdot N \\
&= M \cdot N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Closed-form (alternative) for offline enrollment de-duplication.**
When the application is to de-duplicate a fresh database of $N$ new enrollments (e.g., voter ID deduplication), the total number of pairwise comparisons is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{dedup}} &= \binom{N}{2} \\
&= \frac{N(N-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration.**
If $N = 1{,}000$ new voter applicants are to be checked for duplicates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{dedup}} &= \frac{1000 \times 999}{2} \\
&= \frac{999{,}000}{2} \\
&= 499{,}500 \text{ comparisons}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why it matters for engineering:** Aadhaar's de-duplication engine performs **~10^12 comparisons per day**. The choice of indexing structure (e.g., kd-tree, BK-tree, LSH) is justified solely by this scaling.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Modality vs. Application Suitability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most exam-relevant table** for the topic. Memorize the three "best" and three "worst" pairings — they form the backbone of any 14-mark answer.

| Application | Best Modality | Why this modality works | Weakest Modality | Why it fails |
|-------------|---------------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------|
| National ID (1:N on 10^9 scale) | **Iris** | High uniqueness, small template, stable over decades | Face | Ageing, twins confound |
| Phone unlock | **Fingerprint** (capacitive) | Fast, small sensor, low FAR | DNA | Far too slow (days) |
| Airport e-Gate | **Face** (visible-light + IR) | Non-intrusive, passive, quick | Scent | No sensor, no standards |
| Latent crime-scene match | **Fingerprint** (powder-raised) | Forensic-grade, court-admissible | Gait | Court-uncertain |
| Banking KYC | **Fingerprint + Face** | Dual-factor, regulatory compliance | Keystroke | Behavioural drift |
| Healthcare patient ID | **Palm vein** | Internal pattern, sterile, no fake risk | Voice | Patient may be intubated |
| Surveillance / missing child | **Face** (CCTV-grade) | Covert, non-cooperative, long range | Iris | Requires cooperation |
| Time & attendance | **Fingerprint** | Cheap sensor, anti-buddy-punching | Iris | Costly sensor |
| Border watch-list | **Face + Iris** | Covert acquisition possible | Signature | Easily forged |
| Driver's license | **Fingerprint + Photo** | Legacy compatibility, ISO 19794 | Gait | Irrelevant to driving |
| Cloud KYC (video) | **Face (liveness)** | Remote-friendly, liveness via blink | DNA | Lab-bound |
| Laptop logical access | **Fingerprint** | Embedded, USB-ready | Palm vein | Bulky sensor |

### 3.4 The Universality–Distinctiveness–Permanence–Collectability (UDPC) Filter

Every biometric application passes through this 4-criterion test:

$$
\text{Application Suitability Score} = w_1 \cdot U + w_2 \cdot D + w_3 \cdot P + w_4 \cdot C
$$

Where:

- $U$ = Universality (does every user have it?)
- $D$ = Distinctiveness (does it separate users uniquely?)
- $P$ = Permanence (does it remain stable over time?)
- $C$ = Collectability (can a sensor capture it?)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are **application-defined weights** (sum to 1)

> **Example:** For *forensic criminal ID*, $w_2$ (distinctiveness) is set highest because court conviction requires near-certain identification. For *time & attendance*, $w_4$ (collectability) dominates because the sensor must handle 100+ dirty fingers per hour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Biometric Application

> The diagram below maps the **complete flow** of a real-world biometric application, from end-user enrollment to operational matching and audit. It consolidates every Module 1 sub-concept into a single visualizable chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    enrollA[User Presents Trait] --> captureA[Sensor Captures Raw Biometric]
    captureA --> qualityA{Quality OK?}
    qualityA -- No --> rejectA[Reject: Retry or FTE Log]
    qualityA -- Yes --> extractA[Feature Extractor]
    extractA --> storeA[(Template Database)]
    storeA --> ready[System Ready for Matching]

    probeA[Probe Subject Presents Trait] --> captureB[Sensor Captures Sample]
    captureB --> qualityB{Quality OK?}
    qualityB -- No --> rejectB[FTA - Failure to Acquire]
    qualityB -- Yes --> extractB[Feature Extractor]
    extractB --> matcher[Matcher]

    ready --> matcher
    matcher --> mode{Mode Selected?}
    mode -- 1 is to 1 --> verify[Verification 1 to 1]
    mode -- 1 is to N --> ident[Identification 1 to N]
    mode -- Watch --> screen[Screening 1 to N Negative]

    verify --> thr{Threshold}
    ident --> thr
    screen --> thr

    thr -- Match --> accept[Accept - Identity Established]
    thr -- No Match --> deny[Deny - Access Refused]
    accept --> audit[Audit Log FMR FNMR EER]
    deny --> audit
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every biometric application, regardless of the trait used, follows this exact 3-phase architecture — *Enrollment* (top arm), *Matching* (bottom arm), and *Decision & Audit* (right arm). The dotted line from the template database back to the matcher is the critical **secure reference**; in real deployments, templates are encrypted and never leave the secure enclave.

### 4.2 Application-Domain Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Biometric System Applications]
    root --> gov[Government and Civil ID]
    root --> sec[Security and Defense]
    root --> fin[Financial and Commercial]
    root --> health[Healthcare]
    root --> cons[Consumer Electronics]
    root --> acad[Forensic and Research]

    gov --> aadhaar[Aadhaar National ID]
    gov --> passport[Passport and Visa]
    gov --> voter[Voter ID De-duplication]
    gov --> dlicense[Driver License]

    sec --> border[Border Control e-Gate]
    sec --> afis[AFIS Forensics]
    sec --> surv[Surveillance CCTV]
    sec --> mil[Military Base Access]

    fin --> kyc[Banking KYC]
    fin --> upi[UPI Mobile Payment]
    fin --> atm[ATM Cardless Withdrawal]
    fin --> attend[Time and Attendance]

    health --> patient[Patient ID]
    health --> ehr[Electronic Health Record Access]
    health --> newborn[Newborn Identification]

    cons --> phone[Phone Unlock]
    cons --> laptop[Laptop Login]
    cons --> iot[Smart Home IoT]

    acad --> afis2[Criminal AFIS Lookup]
    acad --> dna[DNA Paternity]
    acad --> research[Behavioural Biometrics Research]
```

### 4.3 The 1:1 vs 1:N Operational Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VER[Verification 1 is to 1]
        V1[Claimed ID: user123] --> V2[Fetch user123 Template]
        V3[Probe Sample] --> V2
        V2 --> V4[Single Comparison]
        V4 --> V5[Match or No Match]
    end

    subgraph IDN[Identification 1 is to N]
        I1[No ID Claimed] --> I2[Probe Sample]
        I2 --> I3[Compare Against All N Templates]
        I3 --> I4[Rank by Similarity Score]
        I4 --> I5[Top Match = Identity]
    end
```

> **Engineering insight:** The *verification* block runs **O(1)** comparisons and is therefore fast enough for real-time embedded use (phone unlock, payment signing). The *identification* block is **O(N)** and is computationally expensive — Aadhaar's 1.4-billion-strong database uses a distributed parallel architecture with **sharding by PIN code** to keep response time under 2 seconds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a Biometric System. Differentiate between Verification and Identification modes with one real-world example each.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Biometric System Definition:** An automated pattern-recognition system that recognizes a person based on their physiological or behavioural characteristics.
- **Verification (1:1):** Compares the probe against a *single* claimed template. *Example:* Fingerprint unlock on a smartphone.
- **Identification (1:N):** Compares the probe against *all* templates in a database to find the best match. *Example:* AFIS matching a latent print from a crime scene against the national criminal database.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "identification = 1:1" or "verification = 1:N" out of confusion. The mnemonic is: **V**erification is **V**ery specific (**1:1**), **I**dentification is **I**ndiscriminate (**1:N**).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*List any three major applications of biometric systems and identify the most suitable biometric trait for each.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. **National ID (Aadhaar)** → Iris + Fingerprint (large-scale 1:N, requires uniqueness)
2. **Smartphone Unlock** → Fingerprint (fast, low-cost, embedded sensor)
3. **Surveillance (CCTV crowd monitoring)** → Face (non-intrusive, long-range, covert acquisition)
4. (Optional fourth) **Patient Identification in Hospital** → Palm vein (hygienic, internal pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only the *trait name* without justifying *why* it suits the application leads to a **1-mark deduction**. Always append a 1-line justification (e.g., "Face is chosen because it allows non-cooperative acquisition at distance").

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Choice Option A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **commercial and government applications** of biometric systems. For each application, justify the choice of biometric trait.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **general architecture of a biometric system** and explain the role of each block in the **enrolment and matching phases**.

##### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Government Applications**
   - **National ID (Aadhaar)**: Uses *iris + fingerprint + face* for de-duplication of 1.4 billion residents. Iris is chosen for *distinctiveness* and *permanence*; fingerprint for *universality* and *collectability*.
   - **Passport / Visa**: *Face* is the ICAO-mandated primary biometric; fingerprint is the secondary biometric.
   - **Voter ID De-duplication**: *Fingerprint* is used to ensure one person, one vote, by detecting duplicate enrolments.
   - **Driver's License**: *Fingerprint* links the license to the holder, preventing impersonation.

2. **Commercial Applications**
   - **Banking KYC**: *Fingerprint + face* ensures non-repudiation of account opening.
   - **Mobile Payments (UPI)**: *Fingerprint* authorizes high-value transactions; the device stores the template in a secure hardware enclave.
   - **Time & Attendance**: *Fingerprint* prevents "buddy punching" — a major source of payroll fraud.

3. **Security & Defence**
   - **Border Control**: *Face + iris* at e-Gates; watch-list screening against 1:N criminal or terrorist databases.
   - **AFIS (Forensics)**: *Latent fingerprint* matching against criminal records; *DNA* for cold cases.

4. **Healthcare**
   - **Patient ID**: *Palm vein* or *iris* prevents medical record mix-ups; hygiene-friendly (no contact required with palm vein).

5. **Consumer Electronics**
   - **Phone Unlock**: *Fingerprint* (capacitive/ultrasonic) or *face* (IR-based structured light).
   - **Laptop Login**: *Fingerprint* via embedded sensor.

> **Justification Rule Used:** Every modality choice is justified by the *UDPC* (Universality, Distinctiveness, Permanence, Collectability) criteria, weighted per the application's needs.

##### Model Solution for (b):

The **general architecture of a biometric system** consists of five sequential blocks:

1. **Sensor Module**: Captures the raw biometric (e.g., optical fingerprint scanner, CMOS camera for face). It includes illumination (IR LEDs for iris) and anti-spoofing elements.
2. **Pre-processing & Quality Check**: Enhances the image (normalization, binarization for fingerprint) and rejects low-quality samples (FTE / FTA logic).
3. **Feature Extractor**: Converts the pre-processed image into a compact *feature template* (e.g., minutiae points for fingerprint, eigenface coefficients for face, IrisCode for iris).
4. **Template Database**: Stores the encrypted reference templates. In *enrolment mode*, templates are *written*; in *matching mode*, templates are *read*.
5. **Matcher**: Compares the live feature vector with the stored template, producing a **similarity score**. The decision (accept/reject) is made by comparing this score against a **threshold**.

> **Enrolment flow:** Sensor → Pre-process → Feature Extract → **Save to Database**
> **Matching flow:** Sensor → Pre-process → Feature Extract → **Compare to Database** → Score → Decision

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. Students often forget to *label the enrolment and matching arrows separately* on the diagram — loss of **1 mark**.
> 2. Writing "feature extraction" without naming **what features** (e.g., minutiae, IrisCode) loses **1 mark**.
> 3. Omitting the **threshold / decision block** loses **1 mark** — it is the operational heart of the system.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Choice Option B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of biometric systems in **forensic identification** and **border security**. Compare the operational modes used in each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **FAR, FRR, and EER**. Explain how these metrics influence the choice of operating threshold in a real-world application (e.g., airport e-gate).

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Forensic Identification (1:N Identification mode):**
- Law-enforcement agencies collect *latent prints* (partial, smudged, lifted from crime scenes).
- These are matched against the **national criminal database (IAFIS in the USA, IDENT in India)** which contains millions of ten-print records.
- The operational mode is **1:N identification** — the system returns a *ranked candidate list*, and a human latent examiner makes the final court-admissible decision.
- **Trait chosen:** *Fingerprint* (universality 100%, permanence lifetime, distinctiveness 10^–50, court-accepted).

**Border Security (1:1 Verification + 1:N Screening):**
- **e-Gates** use **1:1 verification**: the traveller presents a chip-enabled passport (claimed ID), and the live face/iris is compared to the chip-stored template.
- **Watch-list screening** uses **1:N negative identification**: the live sample is compared against a list of known persons of interest; a *match* triggers secondary inspection.
- **Trait chosen:** *Face + Iris* (fast acquisition, ICAO 9303 compliant, can be acquired at a distance).

> **Comparison Table (memorize this):**

| Aspect | Forensic ID | Border Security |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| Mode | 1:N Identification | 1:1 + 1:N Screening |
| Primary trait | Fingerprint | Face + Iris |
| Operator | Human expert + system | Traveller + automated gate |
| Throughput | Low (10s/day) | High (1000s/day) |
| FAR tolerance | Very low ($\leq 10^{-6}$) | Moderate ($10^{-4}$) |

##### Model Solution for (b):

- **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Probability that the system *incorrectly accepts* an impostor. Also called FMR.
- **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Probability that the system *incorrectly rejects* a genuine user. Also called FNMR.
- **EER (Equal Error Rate):** The operating point on the ROC curve where **FAR = FRR**. A *lower EER* indicates a more accurate system.

**Influence on operating threshold:**
- The **threshold** $T$ is a tunable scalar applied to the matcher's similarity score.
- *Lowering T* → system accepts more easily → FAR increases, FRR decreases (more impostors get in, fewer genuine users are rejected).
- *Raising T* → system accepts only high-confidence matches → FAR decreases, FRR increases (more genuine users are inconvenienced, fewer impostors get in).

**Airport e-Gate example:**
- A **high-security mode** sets a *high threshold*, tolerating a higher FRR (some genuine passengers wait for manual inspection) to keep FAR very low (almost no impostor gets through).
- A **convenience mode** sets a *low threshold*, tolerating a higher FAR (some impostors slip through) to keep FRR low (passengers flow without delay).
- The **EER is the engineering sweet spot** used during *system evaluation* — it is rarely the deployed threshold because the deployment must favour either security (lower FAR) or convenience (lower FRR).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse FAR with FRR. **Mnemonic: "FAR = False Accept = Wrong person Accepted (security breach). FRR = False Reject = Right person Rejected (inconvenience)."** A security-critical application tunes to **minimise FAR**, not FRR.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Applications of Biometric Systems**

- **Definition:** A biometric system identifies/verifies a person using physiological or behavioural traits.
- **Three Operating Modes:** Verification (1:1), Identification (1:N), Screening (1:N negative).
- **Mnemonic for FAR vs FRR:** *FAR = False Accept = Wrong person in (security bad)*. *FRR = False Reject = Right person out (convenience bad)*.
- **EER is the most-cited single number** for system performance — lower is better; occurs where FAR = FRR.
- **1:N complexity scales as O(N)**; de-duplication is $\binom{N}{2}$.
- **Top 12 Applications to Memorize (with modality):**
  1. Aadhaar → Iris + Fingerprint
  2. Passport → Face (ICAO 9303)
  3. Voter ID → Fingerprint (de-duplication)
  4. Driver's License → Fingerprint + Photo
  5. Border e-Gate → Face + Iris
  6. AFIS Forensics → Latent Fingerprint
  7. Banking KYC → Fingerprint + Face
  8. Healthcare Patient ID → Palm Vein / Iris
  9. Phone Unlock → Fingerprint / Face
  10. Time & Attendance → Fingerprint
  11. Surveillance / Missing Child → Face
  12. UPI / ATM → Fingerprint
- **UDPC Filter:** Every application is justified via *Universality, Distinctiveness, Permanence, Collectability*.
- **Aadhaar scale fact:** 1.4 billion enrolments → sharding + parallel matching is mandatory.
- **Threshold Tuning Rule:** High-security → Raise threshold → Lower FAR, Higher FRR. Convenience → Lower threshold → Higher FAR, Lower FRR.
- **Block diagram must include:** Sensor → Pre-process → Feature Extract → Template DB → Matcher → Threshold → Decision.
- **Cooperative vs Non-cooperative:** Cooperative = enrolment, verification, e-Gates. Non-cooperative = surveillance, face-in-crowd.
- **Expected impostor count** in a deployment: $E[X] = N \cdot \text{FAR}$ (binomial mean) — always quote this in numerical problems.
- **Forensic trait of choice:** Fingerprint (court-admissible, >100 years of legal precedent).
- **Hygienic modality (healthcare):** Palm vein / Iris — contactless, no skin contact, no COVID transmission risk.

> **Final Examiner's Note:** In 14-mark answers, always begin with the **definition**, follow with a **block diagram**, then enumerate the **applications with modality justification**, and conclude with a **comparison table or threshold-tuning example**. This 4-part structure is the KTU valuation key's expected template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security and Privacy Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security and Privacy Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Biometric Security** refers to the automated recognition of individuals based on their behavioral and biological characteristics. However, the deployment of such systems introduces critical **Security and Privacy Issues** — a set of technical, operational, legal, and ethical challenges concerning the protection of biometric data, prevention of unauthorized access, resistance to spoofing, and preservation of the subject's personal identity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Security issues* in biometrics pertain to the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of biometric templates and the system infrastructure against adversarial attacks. *Privacy issues* pertain to the unauthorized collection, storage, sharing, or misuse of personally identifiable physiological/behavioral information, which is **non-revocable** (unlike passwords).

The foundational triad governing these issues is the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** extended with **Non-Repudiation** and **Privacy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Master Key Problem"**
>
> Imagine your **fingerprint** is a master key. Unlike a password (which you can change if leaked), you **cannot grow a new fingerprint**. If a hacker steals this master key from a poorly designed lock, they can:
> - Open your house (account access),
> - Clone the key for others (template reuse),
> - Track which houses you have keys for (cross-database linkage/cross-matching).
>
> **Security issues** = the lock is broken (system hacked, template stolen, sensor spoofed).
> **Privacy issues** = someone knows your key's shape and is tracking your movements (identity linkage, function creep).

## 1.3 Key Terms (KTU Board High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition (Exam-Ready) |
|---|---|
| **Biometric Template** | A digital reference record (e.g., minutiae map, eigenface vector) extracted from a captured sample. |
| **Identity Theft** | Unauthorized use of another's biometric traits to gain access or impersonate. |
| **Function Creep** | Use of biometric data beyond the originally stated purpose. |
| **Cross-Matching** | Linking records across different databases using biometric data to profile individuals. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Property ensuring a subject cannot deny having performed a biometric transaction. |
| **Cancellable Biometrics** | Intentional, repeatable distortion of biometric templates allowing revocation and re-issuance. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Security Analysis (KTU Board Mandatory)

| Metric | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **False Acceptance Rate** | **FAR / FMR** | Probability that an imposter is accepted (Security metric) |
| **False Rejection Rate** | **FRR / FNMR** | Probability that a genuine user is rejected (Usability metric) |
| **Equal Error Rate** | **EER** | Operating point where FAR = FRR |
| **Failure To Enrol Rate** | **FTE** | % of users who cannot enrol |
| **Failure To Capture Rate** | **FTC** | % of attempts that fail to produce a usable sample |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security ↔ Usability Trade-off:** Lowering FAR (for security) always raises FRR (hurting usability). The **EER** is the unique point balancing both — frequently cited in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Security Issues in Biometric Systems

The security issues are classified along the **biometric processing pipeline** (enrolment → storage → matching → decision). An attacker can target ANY stage.

### A. Attacks on the **Sensor** (Presentation / Spoofing Attacks)
- **Replay Attack:** Re-injecting a previously recorded raw signal.
- **Spoofing (Artefact Attack):** Presenting a fake artefact (gummy finger, face mask, printed iris photo) to the sensor.
- **Synthetic Biometric Generation:** DeepFakes, GAN-generated fingerprints.
- **BruteForce / Hill-Climbing:** Iteratively modifying input to push the match score higher.

### B. Attacks on the **Feature Extractor / Matcher**
- **Feature Manipulation:** Modifying extracted features to cause misclassification.
- **Trojan / Backdoor Injection:** Subverting the matching module.
- **Matcher Re-tuning:** Forcing a lower threshold to increase FAR.

### C. Attacks on the **Template Database** (Most Critical)
- **Template Reconstruction / Inversion:** Recovering raw biometric data from a stolen template.
- **Template Tampering:** Modifying stored templates to allow unauthorised access.
- **Masquerade:** Replacing a legitimate template with the attacker's own.

### D. Attacks on the **Channel / Transmission**
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Intercepting the template/score during transit.
- **Replay across modules:** E.g., replaying an old match score.

### E. Attacks on the **Decision / Application**
- **Coercion:** Forcing an individual to authenticate against their will.
- **Replay of decision output.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Memorise the **8 attack points** of a generic biometric system as per Jain, Ross & Nandakumar's framework — a frequently asked 7-mark question.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Privacy Issues

| Privacy Issue | Description | KTU Board Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Untraceability** | Subject cannot be tracked across databases. | National ID cards linked via fingerprint. |
| **Unlinkability** | Records from two systems must not be correlatable. | Same fingerprint used in bank + hospital. |
| **Revocability** | Ability to invalidate and re-issue a compromised template. | Password reset equivalent. |
| **Renewability / Diversity** | One trait should yield multiple secure templates. | Multiple fingers / fingers with salts. |
| **Information Leakage** | Hidden demographic data (gender, ethnicity, age) inferred from traits. | Face recognition leaking medical conditions. |
| **Function Creep** | Scope expansion beyond the original consent. | Aadhaar used beyond welfare schemes. |
| **Surveillance / Tracking** | Continuous identification without consent. | CCTV face recognition in public spaces. |
| **Data Sovereignty** | Cross-border storage and jurisdictional conflicts. | Cloud storage of EU citizens' data in the US. |

## 2.3 The "Three Pillars" of Biometric Privacy Frameworks

1. **Consent & Notice:** Explicit, informed, purpose-bound consent.
2. **Purpose Limitation:** Data used only for the stated objective.
3. **Data Minimisation & Retention Limits:** Store only what is necessary, for only as long as needed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **High-Yield Equations for Security and Privacy Analysis**

| Equation / Rule | LaTeX | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Equal Error Rate Condition | $\text{FAR}(\tau^{*}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{*}) = \text{EER}$ | Defines optimal threshold $\tau^{*}$. |
| Total Error Rate | $\text{TER} = \text{FAR} + \text{FRR}$ | Sum of both error types at threshold $\tau$. |
| Genuine Acceptance Rate | $\text{GAR} = 1 - \text{FRR}$ | Complement of False Rejection. |
| ZeroFAR Threshold | $\text{FRR}_{\text{zero-FAR}}$ | FRR when FAR = 0 (high-security mode). |
| ZeroFRR Threshold | $\text{FAR}_{\text{zero-FRR}}$ | FAR when FRR = 0 (convenience mode). |
| Trade-off Curve | $\text{FAR}(\tau) \text{ is monotonically decreasing in } \tau$ | Increasing threshold $\downarrow$ FAR, $\uparrow$ FRR. |
| Unlinkability (ISO/IEC 24745) | $H(T_1, T_2) \approx H(T_1) \approx H(T_2)$ where $T_1, T_2$ are templates | Templates must be mutually uninformative. |
| Revocability Condition | $\text{MatchScore}(T_i, T_j) \leq \text{FAR} \; \forall \, i \neq j$ | Two transformed versions must not falsely match. |
| Diversity Condition | $\vert \{ T_i \mid T_i = f(B, k_i) \} \vert$ is large | Many independent keys $k_i$ yield distinct templates. |
| Entropy of Biometric Key | $H(B) \geq L$ where $L$ is the security bits | Biometric must provide at least L bits of entropy. |

> **Engineering Utility:** The Unlinkability + Revocability + Diversity triplet (the **URD properties**) forms the design specification for **Cancellable Biometrics** and **Biometric Cryptosystems** — high-frequency KTU topics in Module 2 and 3.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Banking:** Aadhaar-enabled Payment System (AEPS) uses biometric hash + tokenization to prevent privacy leakage.
- **Border Control:** e-Gates must defend against morphed-face attacks at the **sensor layer**.
- **Healthcare:** Genomic data in EHRs are quasi-biometric — privacy frameworks like **HIPAA** apply.
- **Mobile Unlocking:** Secure Enclave / TEE stores the template encrypted; raw image is never exposed.
- **GDPR (EU, 2018):** Treats biometric data as a "special category" — explicit consent + DPIA mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Threat Matrices

> Since "Security and Privacy Issues" is a conceptual domain, the analytical work consists of **threat-modelling derivations**, **error-rate computations**, and **URD property proofs** rather than algebraic derivations. Each step is shown exhaustively.

## 3.1 Derivation: The Security-Usability Trade-off Curve

**Goal:** Show why lowering FAR inherently increases FRR.

**Step 1 — Definitions.**
Let the **match score** $s \in [0, 1]$ be the output of a biometric matcher. Let $\tau \in [0, 1]$ be the **decision threshold** such that:
- If $s \geq \tau$ → ACCEPT (claim "Genuine")
- If $s < \tau$  → REJECT (claim "Impostor")

**Step 2 — Distribution of Scores.**
Let $p_G(s)$ be the probability density function of scores from **genuine users** and $p_I(s)$ be that from **impostors**. The two distributions overlap (otherwise biometrics would be perfect).

**Step 3 — Compute FAR and FRR as functions of $\tau$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FAR}(\tau) &= \int_{\tau}^{1} p_I(s) \, ds \;=\; P(S_{\text{impostor}} \geq \tau) \\
\text{FRR}(\tau) &= \int_{0}^{\tau} p_G(s) \, ds \;=\; P(S_{\text{genuine}} < \tau) \\
\text{GAR}(\tau) &= 1 - \text{FRR}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{1} p_G(s) \, ds
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Monotonicity of the Trade-off.**
- As $\tau$ **increases** (stricter rule): fewer impostors are accepted ⇒ $\text{FAR}(\tau) \downarrow$.
- But also fewer genuines are accepted ⇒ $\text{FRR}(\tau) \uparrow$ and $\text{GAR}(\tau) \downarrow$.

**Step 5 — Derivation of the Equal Error Rate (EER).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EER} &= \text{FAR}(\tau^{*}) = \text{FRR}(\tau^{*}) \\
\text{where } \tau^{*} \text{ solves: } \quad \frac{d}{d\tau}\bigl[\text{FAR}(\tau) - \text{FRR}(\tau)\bigr] &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow \; p_I(\tau^{*}) &= p_G(\tau^{*})
\end{aligned}
$$

So at the EER threshold, the **two score distributions cross** — the densities are equal.

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision.**
A high-security deployment (e.g., nuclear facility) sets $\tau > \tau^{*}$, accepting very low FAR at the cost of higher FRR. A consumer device (smartphone unlock) sets $\tau < \tau^{*}$, accepting higher FAR for usability.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat-Model Mapping (Jain-Ross-Nandakumar Framework)

> Use this 8-point matrix in any "Discuss security issues" question for full marks.

| # | Attack Point | Asset Targeted | Attack Type | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensor** | Live biometric sample | Spoofing / Replay | Liveness detection, multi-spectral imaging |
| 2 | **Feature Extractor** | Feature vector | Trojan / Manipulation | Trusted execution, code signing |
| 3 | **Matcher** | Match score | Hill-climbing, retuning | Score quantization, output encryption |
| 4 | **Template DB** | Stored template | Theft, inversion, tampering | Template protection (hashing, fuzzy vault) |
| 5 | **Channel (Template)** | Transmitted template | MITM | TLS / mutual authentication |
| 6 | **Channel (Score)** | Score in transit | Replay | Timestamped, signed tokens |
| 7 | **Decision Module** | Accept/Reject | Override, logic bombs | Hardware security modules (HSM) |
| 8 | **Application / Audit** | Logs, policies | Insider threat, function creep | Role-based access, audit, DPIA |

## 3.3 Proof Sketch: Unlinkability Property

**Claim.** A cancellable scheme $T = f(B, k)$ is unlinkable iff for two different keys $k_1 \neq k_2$ and the same biometric $B$:

$$
P\bigl(\text{Match}(f(B, k_1), f(B, k_2)) = \text{accept}\bigr) \;\leq\; \text{FAR}_{\text{system}}
$$

**Step 1.** By definition of FAR, the probability that two unrelated templates falsely match is bounded by the system FAR.
**Step 2.** For the cancellable scheme, $f(B, k_1)$ and $f(B, k_2)$ are **statistically independent** when $k_1, k_2$ are independent random keys — this is enforced by design (e.g., BioHash uses random orthonormal projections).
**Step 3.** Therefore the cross-match probability equals the chance of any random impostor match, satisfying the unlinkability bound. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU sub-question**: *"Prove that a BioHash-based cancellable system provides unlinkability."*

## 3.4 Comparison: Password Security vs. Biometric Security

| Property | Password | Biometric |
|---|---|---|
| **Revocability** | Easy (change password) | Hard — requires cancellable scheme |
| **Length / Entropy** | User-controlled | Fixed by physiology |
| **Sharing** | Can be shared | Inherently non-shareable |
| **Uniqueness** | User-chosen | Population-unique (theoretically) |
| **Loss** | Re-issue possible | Permanent compromise |
| **Storage** | Hash (one-way) | Cannot be one-way-hashed (fuzzy data) |
| **Privacy** | Low | High — quasi-identifier |

> **Engineering Insight:** The fact that biometric features are **noisy and fuzzy** (unlike exact-match passwords) is the **root cause** of the FAR/FRR trade-off and the need for specialised template protection schemes (Fuzzy Vault, Fuzzy Extractor, Secure Sketch).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biometric System Security Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User / Subject] -->|Presents Trait| B(Sensor)
    B -->|Raw Data| C[Feature Extractor]
    C -->|Feature Vector| D[Quality Check]
    D -->|Template T| E{Template Protection Module}
    E -->|Encrypted / Transformed| F[(Template Database)]
    F -->|Stored T'| G[M Matcher]
    C -->|Query Features| G
    G -->|Match Score S| H[Decision Policy tau]
    H -->|Accept / Reject| I[Application]

    P1[Liveness Detection] -.guards.-> B
    P2[Crypto Module] -.guards.-> E
    P3[Audit Logs] -.monitors.-> F
    P4[DPIA / Consent Mgmt] -.governs.-> I

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style E fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#831843
    style F fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style G fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#14532d
    style H fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#4c1d95
    style I fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal pipeline is the standard biometric processing chain.
- Dashed arrows show **defence layers** (liveness, crypto, audit, governance) added specifically to address the security/privacy issues of Module 1.

## 4.2 Threat Model: 8 Attack Surfaces of a Biometric System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Attack Point 1: Sensor Spoofing] -->|exposes| Trait[Raw Biometric]
    S2[Attack Point 2: Feature Extractor Trojan] -->|exposes| FVec[Feature Vector]
    S3[Attack Point 3: Matcher Manipulation] -->|exposes| Score[Match Score]
    S4[Attack Point 4: Template DB Theft] -->|exposes| Tmpl[Stored Template]
    S5[Attack Point 5: Channel MITM Template] -->|exposes| TChan[Transit Template]
    S6[Attack Point 6: Channel Replay Score] -->|exposes| SChan[Transit Score]
    S7[Attack Point 7: Decision Logic Override] -->|exposes| Dec[Accept Reject]
    S8[Attack Point 8: Application / Audit Insider] -->|exposes| Logs[Audit Logs]

    Trait --> CM1[Liveness Detection, Multi-spectral Sensor]
    FVec --> CM2[TEE, Signed Code, FHE]
    Score --> CM3[Score Quantization, Output Encryption]
    Tmpl --> CM4[Fuzzy Vault, BioHash, Homomorphic Encryption]
    TChan --> CM5[TLS 1.3, Mutual Auth]
    SChan --> CM6[Timestamped Signed Tokens]
    Dec --> CM7[HSM, Policy Engine]
    Logs --> CM8[RBAC, Immutable Audit, DPIA]

    style S1 fill:#fee2e2
    style S2 fill:#fee2e2
    style S3 fill:#fee2e2
    style S4 fill:#fee2e2
    style S5 fill:#fee2e2
    style S6 fill:#fee2e2
    style S7 fill:#fee2e2
    style S8 fill:#fee2e2
    style CM1 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM2 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM3 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM4 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM5 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM6 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM7 fill:#dcfce7
    style CM8 fill:#dcfce7
```

## 4.3 Privacy Framework: URD Properties + Consent Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Enrolment
        E1[Informed Consent] --> E2[Purpose Limitation]
        E2 --> E3[Biometric Capture]
        E3 --> E4[Template Protection Scheme]
    end
    E4 --> P1[Unlinkability Test]
    E4 --> P2[Revocability Test]
    E4 --> P3[Diversity Test]
    P1 --> Op[Operational Use]
    P2 --> Op
    P3 --> Op
    Op --> M1{Misuse Detected}
    M1 -->|Yes| R1[Revoke Old Template]
    R1 --> E4
    M1 -->|No| M2{Function Creep}
    M2 -->|Yes| A1[Audit and DPIA]
    M2 -->|No| OK[Continue Operation]

    style E1 fill:#fef3c7
    style P1 fill:#dcfce7
    style P2 fill:#dcfce7
    style P3 fill:#dcfce7
    style A1 fill:#fee2e2
    style OK fill:#dbeafe
```

> This diagram precisely captures the **continuous privacy lifecycle** — consent is not a one-time event, and the system must support re-issuance when a template is compromised or scope is violated.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between FAR and FRR. Why is the trade-off between them important in biometric security?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Probability that an impostor is incorrectly accepted as genuine. It is a **security** metric — a high FAR means unauthorised entry.
- **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Probability that a genuine user is incorrectly rejected. It is a **usability** metric — a high FRR annoys legitimate users.
- **Trade-off:** A single decision threshold $\tau$ governs both. Increasing $\tau$ lowers FAR but raises FRR and vice-versa. The **EER** is the unique operating point where $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$. The choice depends on application context (banking → low FAR; mobile unlock → balanced EER).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three privacy issues associated with biometric systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Unlinkability** — Records from two systems should not be correlatable using the same biometric.
2. **Function Creep** — Biometric data originally collected for one purpose (e.g., welfare) being reused for another (e.g., surveillance).
3. **Information Leakage** — Hidden demographic attributes (gender, ethnicity, health) inferred from raw biometrics without consent.
   *(Any three of: unlinkability, revocability, function creep, cross-matching, surveillance, data sovereignty, information leakage.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Coverage

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **8 attack points** of a generic biometric system as proposed by Jain, Ross and Nandakumar. For each attack point, suggest **one suitable countermeasure**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **threats to privacy** in biometric systems. Explain the **URD properties** (Unlinkability, Revocability, Diversity) that a privacy-preserving biometric template must satisfy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Block Diagram (re-drawn for answer sheet):**

> Draw a horizontal pipeline: **Sensor → Feature Extractor → Matcher → Template Database → Decision Module → Application**, with the transmission channel between Matcher and Decision. Add a separate audit / application block.

**The 8 Attack Points (1 mark each for naming + 0.5 mark for countermeasure, totalling 7 marks when balanced):**

| # | Attack Point | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Type-1 Attack (Sensor / Spoof):** Fake finger, face mask, iris print presented to sensor. | **Liveness detection** (perspiration, blood flow, 3-D depth). |
| 2 | **Type-2 Attack (Replay / Re-injection):** Recorded raw signal replayed. | Challenge-response, time-stamped tokens. |
| 3 | **Type-3 Attack (Feature Extractor Override):** Trojan in feature module. | Code signing, TEE / secure enclave. |
| 4 | **Type-4 Attack (Matcher Override):** Hill-climbing or threshold reset. | Score quantization, encrypted output. |
| 5 | **Type-5 Attack (Template DB Theft):** Stealing stored templates. | Fuzzy Vault, BioHash, AES-at-rest. |
| 6 | **Type-6 Attack (Channel MITM):** Intercepting template in transit. | TLS / mutual authentication. |
| 7 | **Type-7 Attack (Channel Replay):** Replaying an old score. | Time-bound signed tokens. |
| 8 | **Type-8 Attack (Decision / Application Override):** Logic bomb, insider override. | Hardware Security Module (HSM), RBAC. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Drawing only **5 attack points** instead of the standard **8** (deducted 2 marks in past papers).
> - Naming the attack but **not providing a countermeasure** (lose 0.5 mark per point).
> - Confusing **Type-2 (replay)** with **Type-1 (spoof)** — they are distinct.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**1. Privacy Threats (4 marks):**
- **Identity Theft / Masquerade** — Stolen template impersonates subject permanently.
- **Cross-Matching** — Linking the same subject across unrelated databases.
- **Function Creep** — Scope expansion beyond original consent.
- **Surveillance / Tracking** — Covert continuous identification.
- **Information Leakage** — Soft biometrics / demographic inference.
- **Coercion** — Forced authentication under duress.
- **Data Sovereignty Issues** — Cross-border storage conflicting with local laws.

**2. URD Properties (3 marks):**

- **Unlinkability:** Given two protected templates $T_1 = f(B, k_1)$ and $T_2 = f(B, k_2)$ derived from the same biometric $B$ but different keys, the cross-match probability must satisfy:
$$
P\bigl(\text{Match}(T_1, T_2) = \text{accept}\bigr) \leq \text{FAR}_{\text{system}}
$$

- **Revocability:** If $T_1$ is compromised, a new template $T_3 = f(B, k_3)$ must be issuable and the old one invalidated. The matcher must reject $T_1$ henceforth.

- **Diversity:** For the same biometric $B$, the system must support an unlimited number of distinct keys $k_i$, producing non-correlatable templates. Formally, the **diversity measure**:
$$
D = \frac{1}{C(n,2)} \sum_{i<j} \bigl[ \text{Match}(T_i, T_j) = \text{reject} \bigr]
$$
should be $\approx 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Writing the URD properties as mere words without the **probabilistic / formal condition** (lose up to 2 marks).
> - Confusing **Revocability** with **Re-enrolment** — the latter is for genuine new users, not template revocation.
> - Omitting **examples** (e.g., BioHash for cancellable schemes) — examiners reward concrete illustrations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** and **Non-Repudiation** in the context of biometric security. How does each apply to the protection of biometric templates? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **liveness detection** techniques used to counter sensor-level spoofing attacks. Classify them into **hardware-based** and **software-based** methods, with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**1. CIA Triad in Biometric Context (5 marks):**

- **Confidentiality:** Biometric templates must be unreadable to anyone except the legitimate matcher. Achieved by:
  - **Encryption-at-rest** (AES-256 of templates in DB).
  - **Template transformation** (BioHash, Fuzzy Vault — even if DB is stolen, raw trait cannot be recovered).
  - **Access control** — only the matcher process can decrypt.

- **Integrity:** Stored templates and live match scores must not be modified by attackers. Achieved by:
  - **Cryptographic hashing** of templates at enrolment.
  - **Digital signatures** on score messages during transit.
  - **Write-once storage** (append-only logs).

- **Availability:** The biometric system must serve authentication requests on demand. Achieved by:
  - **Redundant servers** and **failover clusters**.
  - **DDoS mitigation** at the API layer.
  - **Multi-modal biometrics** as backup when one trait fails.

**2. Non-Repudiation (2 marks):**
Non-repudiation ensures that a subject **cannot later deny** having performed a biometric transaction. It is achieved by:
- **Signed, time-stamped audit logs** binding the user ID, timestamp, and match score.
- **Hash-linked ledgers** (blockchain-style) preventing log tampering.
- **Continuous authentication** with liveness evidence.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Liveness Detection Techniques (7 marks):**

**A. Hardware-Based Methods (3.5 marks):**

| Method | Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **3-D Depth Sensing** | Detects planar artefacts (photos, masks). | iPhone FaceID uses IR dot projector. |
| **Multi-Spectral Imaging** | Penetrates skin layers; artefacts lack blood flow. | Lumidigm sensors. |
| **Pulsation / Blood-Oxymetry** | Detects heartbeat-induced micro-changes. | Fingerprint pulse sensors. |
| **Temperature Sensors** | Real skin ≈ 33°C; silicone is cooler. | Embedded IR thermopiles. |
| **Capacitive / Electric Field** | Detects dielectric properties of living tissue. | Touch ID sensor. |

**B. Software-Based Methods (3.5 marks):**

| Method | Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Texture Analysis (LBP, BSIF)** | Real skin has fine texture; prints are smooth. | Fingerprint spoof detection. |
| **Micro-Expression / Blinking** | Static photos do not blink or move. | Face liveness. |
| **Challenge-Response** | System asks user to turn head, smile, or read digits. | Banking KYC video. |
| **Frequency / Fourier Analysis** | Recapture artefacts leave high-frequency aliasing. | Iris print detection. |
| **Deep-Learning Anti-Spoof** | CNN trained to distinguish real vs fake. | Face anti-spoofing models. |

**Conclusion:** Best practice = combine hardware + software for **multi-factor liveness**, raising the cost of forgery above any plausible gain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Listing liveness methods without categorising them as **hardware vs software** (lose 1 mark).
> - Confusing **liveness detection** (anti-spoof) with **presentation attack detection** terminology — they are synonymous, but the KTU board expects the term "presentation attack" to appear at least once.
> - Omitting examples (e.g., FaceID) — examiners reward named, real systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **CIA + Non-Repudiation** = the four security pillars for biometric systems.
- [ ] **FAR/FRR trade-off** is governed by threshold $\tau$; **EER** is the balance point.
- [ ] **8 attack points** of Jain-Ross-Nandakumar framework (sensor, replay, extractor, matcher, DB, channel-template, channel-score, decision/application) — must be memorised verbatim.
- [ ] **URD properties** = Unlinkability, Revocability, Diversity — design goals for cancellable biometrics.
- [ ] **Biometric traits are non-revocable** — therefore *templates* (not raw data) must be protected.
- [ ] **Liveness detection** = primary defence against sensor spoofing; combine hardware + software.
- [ ] **Privacy issues** include function creep, cross-matching, information leakage, surveillance, coercion, data sovereignty.
- [ ] **Regulatory frameworks**: GDPR (EU), IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules (India), HIPAA (USA healthcare), BIPA (Illinois).
- [ ] **Cancellable biometrics** (BioHash, Bloom-filter) and **Biometric Cryptosystems** (Fuzzy Vault, Fuzzy Extractor) are the two main *template protection* families — privacy solutions.
- [ ] **Zero-FAR mode** (security-critical) and **Zero-FRR mode** (convenience-critical) — define the operational extremes.
- [ ] **Match score** $s$ and **threshold** $\tau$ drive every security/privacy trade-off equation.
- [ ] **Golden rule:** *"Biometrics authenticate identity; they do not **secrete** it."* — protect the template, not just the channel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physiological Biometrics and Behavioural Biometrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Physiological Biometrics and Behavioural Biometrics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Biometrics – The Foundational Definition **Biometrics** refers to the automated recognition of individuals based on their unique physiological and/or behavioural characteristics. In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus (PECCT635), biometrics is formally defined as the science and technology of measuring and statistically analysing biological data. A **biometric system** is therefore a pattern-recognition system that establishes the authenticity of a specific physiological or behavioural characteristic possessed by a user. The two broad taxonomies that the KTU Module 1 framework mandates are: | # | Branch | Definition | |---|--------|------------| | 1 | **Physiological Biometrics** | Authentication based on **what a person IS** – the static, physical, anatomical traits of the body. | | 2 | **Behavioural Biometrics** | Authentication based on **what a person DOES** – the dynamic, action-based patterns produced by the user. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** The single most important distinction that examiners test is that **physiological traits are time-invariant (or near-invariant)** while **behavioural traits are time-variant and influenced by psychological state, fatigue, and learning.** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview **Real-World Analogy – The College Office Scenario:** Imagine the KTU exam cell verifying a student. There are two security desks: - **Desk A (Physiological):** The clerk checks your *face against the photo on your hall ticket*, your *fingerprint against the database*, and your *iris against the Aadhaar record*. You do **nothing** except stand there. - **Desk B (Behavioural):** The clerk asks you to *sign on the digital pad in your unique style*, *speak your name into the microphone*, or *type a phrase on the keyboard while measuring your key-press rhythm*. Here, the system evaluates **how you perform the action**, not just what you are. Desk A is a physiological system; Desk B is a behavioural system. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECCT635 Module 1):** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects the student to differentiate between the two biometric classes, list five examples of each, identify their working principles, and discuss performance parameters such as **FAR, FRR, and EER**. ## 1.3 Sub-Classification – The Master Taxonomy Physiological and behavioural traits each contain a family of measurable modalities. The exhaustive list per KTU Module 1 is: **A. Physiological Modalities:** - Fingerprint (dermatoglyphics) - Face recognition (2D / 3D) - Iris recognition - Retina scan - Hand geometry - Palm print - DNA matching - Vascular patterns (vein) - Ear shape - Finger geometry - Skin spectroscopy / odour **B. Behavioural Modalities:** - Voice / Speaker verification - Signature dynamics (Dynamic Signature Verification – DSV) - Keystroke dynamics - Gait analysis - Mouse-movement dynamics - Touch-screen gestures - Lip movement - Cognitive biometrics (response to stimuli) ## 1.4 Generic Architecture of a Biometric System Regardless of whether the trait is physiological or behavioural, every KTU-defined biometric system contains four universal functional blocks: 1. **Sensor Module** – captures the raw biometric (camera for face, microphone for voice, scanner for fingerprint). 2. **Feature Extraction Module** – processes raw data into a mathematical template (e.g., minutiae points, MFCC coefficients, eigenfaces). 3. **Matcher / Comparison Module** – compares the live template against the stored **enrolment template**. 4. **Decision Module** – outputs **ACCEPT** or **REJECT** based on a similarity score *S* crossed against a threshold *T*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definitions worth memorising verbatim for the 2-mark KTU questions:** > - **Enrolment** – the one-time process of capturing a biometric and storing it as a reference template. > - **Verification (1:1 matching)** – "Am I who I claim to be?" > - **Identification (1:N matching)** – "Who am I?" > - **Template** – the compact, mathematical representation of the biometric feature set. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physiological Biometrics – Operational Deep-Dive ### 2.1.1 Fingerprint Recognition (The KTU Flagship Example) The **fingerprint** is the ridge–valley pattern formed on the distal phalanx of a finger. It is the oldest and most deployed physiological biometric. Its uniqueness is established by the *Galton-Henry classification*: - **Minutiae features**: ridge endings, bifurcations, dots, islands, ridge crossings. - **Singular points**: core and delta. - A typical fingerprint template stores **40–80 minutiae points** as a coordinate map. **Operational Flow:** 1. Image acquisition → enhancement (Gabor filters, FFT). 2. Binarisation → thinning (skeletonisation). 3. Minutiae extraction → matching (Hamming distance, minutiae-based similarity). 4. Match score → threshold comparison. ### 2.1.2 Face Recognition - **2D Face**: Uses geometric features (eyes, nose, mouth distances) and/or appearance features (Eigenfaces via PCA, Fisherfaces via LDA, Local Binary Patterns). - **3D Face**: Uses depth maps to overcome lighting and pose variations. - **Modern KTU-expected methods**: CNN-based deep feature embeddings, FaceNet, ArcFace. ### 2.1.3 Iris Recognition - The iris is the **coloured annular region** between pupil and sclera. - **Daugman's algorithm** (the gold standard) uses the Integro-Differential Operator: $$ \max_{(r,x_{0},y_{0})} \left\vert G_{\sigma}(r) * \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \oint_{r,x_{0},y_{0}} \frac{I(x,y)}{2\pi r} ds \right\vert $$ - Produces the **IrisCode** (2048-bit binary template). - Universally considered the **most accurate** physiological trait (error rate ~ 1 in $1.2 \times 10^{7}$). ### 2.1.4 Retina Scan - Captures the **blood-vessel pattern of the retina** using low-intensity infrared light. - Extremely secure but intrusive → used in high-security military and nuclear facilities. ### 2.1.5 Hand Geometry, Palm Print, Vascular Patterns - **Hand geometry** measures finger lengths, widths, curvatures. - **Palm print** is essentially a large-scale fingerprint with principal lines, wrinkles, and minutiae. - **Vascular (vein) patterns**: finger-vein, palm-vein, wrist-vein – captured using near-infrared light that is absorbed by haemoglobin. ## 2.2 Behavioural Biometrics – Operational Deep-Dive Behavioural traits are produced by **motor control, cognition, and learned motor patterns** of the central nervous system. They are inherently noisier than physiological traits. ### 2.2.1 Voice / Speaker Verification - The user speaks a fixed or prompted phrase. - **Feature extraction**: Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (**MFCC**), Linear Predictive Coding (LPC), i-vectors, x-vectors. - **Matching**: Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), GMM-UBM (Universal Background Model), or modern DNN embeddings. ### 2.2.2 Signature Dynamics (Dynamic Signature Verification – DSV) - Beyond the static visual appearance, DSV captures: - Pen pressure (x, y, z) - Velocity, acceleration - Stroke order, time, pen-up/pen-down events - Azimuth and altitude angles - Two error classes: **genuine forgeries** (random) and **skilled forgeries** (mimicry). ### 2.2.3 Keystroke Dynamics - Measures the **dwell time** (key pressed) and **flight time** (between key releases and presses). - Latency vector: $\mathbf{L} = (t_{dwell}, t_{flight}, \dots)$ - Applications: continuous authentication on login sessions. ### 2.2.4 Gait Analysis - Recognises a person by their **walking style** – step length, cadence, body sway. - Captured via accelerometer (smartphone), floor sensors, or video. ## 2.3 Why and How – The Underlying Logic | Step | Why is it needed? | How is it implemented? | |------|-------------------|------------------------| | Sensing | Captures raw analogue trait | Camera / scanner / mic | | Pre-processing | Removes noise | Filters, normalisation | | Feature Extraction | Compresses data, retains identity | Mathematical transform | | Template Storage | Allows future comparison | Encrypted DB | | Matching | Computes similarity | Distance metric / classifier | | Decision | Authorises access | Threshold logic | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU Board Exam numerical problems in Module 1.** | # | Metric / Formula | Symbol & Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **False Accept Rate** | $FAR = \dfrac{FA}{N_{Impostor}}$ – probability that an impostor is wrongly accepted. | | 2 | **False Reject Rate** | $FRR = \dfrac{FR}{N_{Genuine}}$ – probability that a genuine user is wrongly rejected. | | 3 | **Genuine Accept Rate** | $GAR = 1 - FRR$ | | 4 | **True Positive Rate** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | | 5 | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | | 6 | **Equal Error Rate** | $EER$ – operating point where $FAR = FRR$. | | 7 | **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** | Plot of $GAR$ vs $FAR$. | | 8 | **Hamming Distance (IrisCode)** | $HD = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \oplus B_i$ – for two bit-strings $A, B$ of length $N$. | | 9 | **Minutiae Similarity Score** | $S = \dfrac{M_{matched}}{\max(M_{template}, M_{input})}$ | | 10 | **Crossover Accuracy / F1** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | | 11 | **Threshold Decision Rule** | If $S \geq T$ → ACCEPT, else REJECT. | | 12 | **Dwell Time** | $t_{dwell} = t_{release} - t_{press}$ | | 13 | **Flight Time** | $t_{flight} = t_{press_{n+1}} - t_{release_n}$ | | 14 | **MFCC** (behavioural feature) | Derived from mel-scale filter bank energies of speech FFT. | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are industry standards in NIST evaluations, Aadhaar (UIDAI) performance audits, and ICAO passport verification. KTU frequently asks numerical problems on FAR, FRR, EER, and thresholding. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Daugman's Integro-Differential Operator for Iris Localisation The iris must first be localised as two non-concentric circles (pupil boundary and iris-sclera boundary) before an IrisCode can be produced. Daugman uses the following derivation. ### Step 1: The Raw Iris Integral Given an input image $I(x,y)$ and a circular contour parameterised by centre $(x_0, y_0)$ and radius $r$, the normalised contour integral is: $$ \oint_{r,x_0,y_0} \frac{I(x,y)}{2\pi r} \, ds $$ This computes the **circular average** of pixel intensity along a candidate circle. ### Step 2: The Radial Derivative To detect the edge (i.e., the maximum intensity transition), differentiate with respect to $r$: $$ \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \oint_{r,x_0,y_0} \frac{I(x,y)}{2\pi r} \, ds $$ ### Step 3: Gaussian Smoothing To suppress high-frequency sensor noise, convolve with a Gaussian of scale $\sigma$: $$ G_{\sigma}(r) * \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \oint \frac{I(x,y)}{2\pi r} \, ds $$ ### Step 4: Final Optimisation Form (the Daugman Operator) Maximise the blurred partial derivative over the search space: $$ \begin{aligned} \max_{(r,x_0,y_0)} \Bigg\vert \, G_{\sigma}(r) \,*\, \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \oint_{r,x_0,y_0} \frac{I(x,y)}{2\pi r} \, ds \, \Bigg\vert \end{aligned} $$ > **Logical interpretation:** The operator searches the entire image for the $(r, x_0, y_0)$ that produces the strongest circular edge response. The pupil boundary is dark-to-light edge, and the iris-sclera boundary is light-to-dark edge – both are detected in the same framework. ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Hamming Distance for Iris Matching ### Step 1: Represent Two IrisCodes as Binary Vectors Let the enrolment template be $A = (A_1, A_2, \dots, A_N)$ and the live template be $B = (B_1, B_2, \dots, B_N)$, where $A_i, B_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and typically $N = 2048$ bits. ### Step 2: Compute Bitwise XOR The XOR operation $A_i \oplus B_i$ yields $1$ if bits differ and $0$ if identical. ### Step 3: Sum the Mismatches The total number of differing bit positions is: $$ d(A,B) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \oplus B_i $$ ### Step 4: Normalise by Template Length The fractional Hamming Distance is: $$ \begin{aligned} HD(A,B) &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \oplus B_i \\ &= \frac{d(A,B)}{N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Decision Rule If $HD \leq T_{iris}$ (typically $0.32$ for normalised Hamming distance, or $\approx 0.30$ for genuine accept), then the system returns **ACCEPT**; otherwise **REJECT**. > [!NOTE] > **Why HD and not Euclidean?** IrisCodes are binary; HD is the natural binary-equivalent of L1 distance on $\{0,1\}^N$ and is fast to compute via bitwise XOR + popcount. ## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: ROC Curve and EER Computation ### Step 1: Generate Genuine and Impostor Score Distributions For a database of $N$ users, perform $N(N-1)/2$ genuine matches and $N(N-1)$ impostor matches, recording similarity scores $S$. ### Step 2: Compute FAR(threshold) and FRR(threshold) for Each Threshold $$ \begin{aligned} FAR(T) &= \frac{\#\{S_{impostor} \geq T\}}{N_{impostor}} \\ FRR(T) &= \frac{\#\{S_{genuine} < T\}}{N_{genuine}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Vary $T$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ to Trace the ROC The ROC plots $GAR(T) = 1 - FRR(T)$ on the y-axis and $FAR(T)$ on the x-axis. ### Step 4: Locate the EER The EER is the unique threshold $T^{\ast}$ where: $$ FAR(T^{\ast}) = FRR(T^{\ast}) $$ Numerically, this is found by: - Tabulating both rates at discrete thresholds. - Plotting both curves. - Locating their intersection (or using linear interpolation between the bracketing points). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A lower EER means a more accurate biometric system.
