| **Fingerprint (minutiae)** | Set of $(x, y, \theta)$ points | Hausdorff / Minutiae-cylinder-code (MCC) | 0.1 – 2 % | 10K matches/sec |
| **Iris (iris-code)** | 2048-bit binary code | Normalized Hamming with bit-shifting | 0.0001 – 0.1 % | 100K matches/sec |
| **Face (deep features)** | 512-d float (FaceNet) | Cosine / Euclidean | 0.1 – 3 % | 5K matches/sec |
| **Voice (i-vector / x-vector)** | 400-d float | PLDA scoring | 1 – 5 % | 1K matches/sec |
| **Palmprint** | Orientation / texture code | Hamming on competitive code | 0.01 – 0.5 % | 20K matches/sec |
| **Finger-vein** | Binary vein pattern | Hamming with mask | 0.01 – 0.1 % | 15K matches/sec |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Biometric Matching Pipeline

The diagram below maps every stage of matching, from probe input to final accept/reject decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biometric Sensor Capture] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> C[Feature Extractor]
    C --> D[Probe Feature Vector Xp]
    D --> E{Mode Selector}
    E -->|1:1 Verification| F[Fetch Claimed Template XT]
    E -->|1:N Identification| G[Fetch All N Templates from Database]
    F --> H[Score Computation Engine]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Score Normalization]
    I --> J[Threshold Comparator]
    J --> K{Decision}
    K -->|Score passes threshold| L[ACCEPT - Genuine User]
    K -->|Score fails threshold| M[REJECT - Impostor]
    L --> N[Audit Log]
    M --> N
    H --> O[ROC Curve Generator]
    O --> P[EER Computation Module]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The score computation engine and the threshold comparator are the *only* components that change when you switch from a fingerprint matcher to a face matcher — the rest of the pipeline is modality-agnostic.

## 4.2 Score Distribution & Decision Threshold Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Genuine["GENUINE SCORE DISTRIBUTION (Higher Mean)"]
        G1[Impostor-tail region]
        G2[Genuine core region]
    end
    subgraph Impostor["IMPOSTOR SCORE DISTRIBUTION (Lower Mean)"]
        I1[Impostor core region]
        I2[Genuine-tail region]
    end
    T[Threshold Tau] --> FAR[Area = FAR]
    T --> FRR[Area = FRR]
    FAR --> ERR1[False Accepts]
    FRR --> ERR2[False Rejects]
```

## 4.3 Verification vs Identification — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[User Presents Biometric] --> ID[User Claims Identity - e.g., User ID]
    ID --> Retrieve[Retrieve Template Tclaimed from DB]
    Retrieve --> Match1[Match Q vs Tclaimed]
    Match1 --> Decision{Score vs Threshold Tau}
    Decision -->|Accept| Grant[Grant Access]
    Decision -->|Reject| Deny[Denial - Try Again]
    Start2[Unknown User Probe] --> Search[Probe matched against ALL N templates]
    Search --> TopK[Generate Ranked List R1, R2, ... RN]
    TopK --> Top1{Rank-1 score vs Tau}
    Top1 -->|Above Tau| IDout[Identified as R1]
    Top1 -->|Below Tau for all| Unknown[No Match - Subject Unknown]
```

## 4.4 Matcher Internal Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Probe[Probe Vector Xp] --> Dist[Distance or Similarity Function]
    Tmpl[Template Vector XT] --> Dist
    Dist --> RawScore[Raw Scalar Score]
    RawScore --> NormBlock{Score Normalization Enabled?}
    NormBlock -->|Yes| ZNorm[Z-Score or Tanh Norm]
    NormBlock -->|No| Direct[Pass Through]
    ZNorm --> Comp[Compare to Threshold Tau]
    Direct --> Comp
    Comp --> Bool[Boolean Decision 0 or 1]
    Comp --> Logging[Logging Subsystem]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**Distinguish between Verification and Identification in biometric matching. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification (1:1) | Identification (1:N) |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Definition** | Confirms whether the claimed identity is true | Determines the identity of an unknown user |
| **Comparison Ratio** | One probe vs. one claimed template | One probe vs. all stored templates |
| **Decision Output** | YES / NO (Accept / Reject) | Ranked candidate list or "Unknown" |
| **Real-world Example** | Aadhaar fingerprint authentication at a POS terminal | Forensic AFIS search by latent fingerprint at a crime scene |
| **Computational Cost** | Low — single comparison | High — N comparisons required |

*(Each cell carries partial credit; one sentence per row is sufficient for full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define False Acceptance Rate (FAR) and False Rejection Rate (FRR). How are they related to the matching threshold $\tau$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **FAR** is the proportion of *impostor* attempts that are incorrectly accepted by the system. Formally, $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$, where $FP$ = false positives and $TN$ = true negatives.
- **FRR** is the proportion of *genuine* attempts that are incorrectly rejected. Formally, $FRR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$, where $FN$ = false negatives and $TP$ = true positives.
- **Relationship to $\tau$:** As $\tau$ is *raised* (stricter acceptance), **FAR decreases** and **FRR increases**, and vice-versa. This inverse relationship is the fundamental **trade-off curve** captured by the ROC.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 carries a single Part-B question of 14 marks with an internal choice (a or b). Each sub-part is 7 marks. We provide TWO complete alternative questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **distance and similarity metrics** used in biometric matching, with their mathematical formulations. Discuss which metric is most suitable for **(i) iris-code matching** and **(ii) face recognition using deep features**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **Equal Error Rate (EER)** and **d-prime ($d'$)** separability index. A fingerprint matcher's genuine and impostor similarity scores are modeled as Gaussian distributions: $\mathcal{N}(0.80, 0.06^2)$ and $\mathcal{N}(0.35, 0.12^2)$ respectively. Calculate the EER threshold, the EER value, and the d-prime index. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Step 1 — List the metrics (2 marks):**

1. **Euclidean Distance** ($L_2$ norm):
   $$D_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(x_k - y_k)^2}$$
2. **Manhattan Distance** ($L_1$ norm):
   $$D_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{k=1}^{d}\vert x_k - y_k \vert$$
3. **Cosine Similarity / Distance:**
   $$S_C = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert\mathbf{x}\Vert_2 \, \Vert\mathbf{y}\Vert_2}, \quad D_C = 1 - S_C$$
4. **Hamming Distance** (binary codes):
   $$HD = \sum_{k=1}^{d} (x_k \oplus y_k), \quad HD_{\text{norm}} = \frac{HD}{d}$$
5. **Mahalanobis Distance** (correlated features):
   $$D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$$

**Step 2 — Discuss properties (2 marks):**

- Euclidean and Manhattan are **distance** metrics (smaller = better); Cosine is a **similarity** metric (larger = better); Hamming is for binary iris/palm codes.
- Euclidean is sensitive to *magnitude*; Cosine is invariant to magnitude (only direction matters).
- Mahalanobis accounts for **feature correlation** and scaling, useful when features are not independent.

**Step 3 — Apply to iris and face (3 marks):**

- **(i) Iris-code matching:** Iris-codes are **2048-bit binary vectors**; the canonical matcher is **Normalized Hamming Distance** with bit-shifting (Daugman, 1993). The threshold is empirically tuned to $\approx 0.32$, achieving theoretical FAR of $10^{-6}$.
- **(ii) Face recognition using deep features:** Modern face recognition systems (FaceNet, ArcFace, DeepFace) produce 512-dimensional *unit-normalized embeddings*. The recommended matcher is **Cosine Similarity** (or equivalently Euclidean on $L_2$-normalized vectors). Threshold typically $\tau \approx 0.6$ for ArcFace.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Define EER and d-prime (2 marks):**

- **EER** is the operating point on the ROC where $FAR(\tau^*) = FRR(\tau^*)$. It summarizes matcher performance in a single scalar (lower is better).
- **d-prime** measures the *separability* of genuine and impostor distributions:
  $$d' = \frac{\vert \mu_g - \mu_i \vert}{\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_g^2 + \sigma_i^2}{2}}}$$
  Higher $d'$ means easier to separate genuine from impostor.

**Step 2 — Compute the EER threshold (2 marks):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$\tau^* = \frac{\sigma_i \mu_g + \sigma_g \mu_i}{\sigma_i + \sigma_g} = \frac{(0.12)(0.80) + (0.06)(0.35)}{0.12 + 0.06}$$

$$\tau^* = \frac{0.0960 + 0.0210}{0.18} = \frac{0.1170}{0.18} = 0.6500$$

**Step 3 — Compute the EER (1.5 marks):**

$$EER = FRR(\tau^*) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau^* - \mu_g}{\sigma_g}\right) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{0.6500 - 0.80}{0.06}\right) = \Phi(-2.50) \approx 0.00621$$

So the **EER $\approx 0.62\%$** (an acceptable fingerprint matcher).

**Step 4 — Compute d-prime (1.5 marks):**

$$d' = \frac{\vert 0.80 - 0.35 \vert}{\sqrt{\frac{0.06^2 + 0.12^2}{2}}} = \frac{0.45}{\sqrt{\frac{0.0036 + 0.0144}{2}}} = \frac{0.45}{\sqrt{0.0090}} = \frac{0.45}{0.0949} \approx 4.74$$

The d-prime of **4.74** indicates strong, but not perfect, separation between genuine and impostor distributions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete **biometric matching pipeline** from probe capture to final decision. Describe the role of **score normalization** in multi-modal or score-level fusion systems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the construction and interpretation of a **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve**. Explain how the choice of threshold $\tau$ influences the operating point on the ROC. What is the **ZeroFAR** operating point and why is it important in security-critical applications? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (3 marks):**

The student is expected to draw the pipeline as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes, clearly labelling:

- Sensor → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Probe Vector
- Database / Template Retrieval
- Score Computation Engine
- Normalization block
- Threshold Comparator → Decision (Accept / Reject)
- Audit Log

**Step 2 — Explanation of each stage (2 marks):**

Each stage must be explained in 1–2 lines: sensor digitizes the trait; preprocessing removes noise and aligns the sample; feature extractor maps the image to a compact vector; the matcher computes pairwise scores; the threshold comparator issues a Boolean decision.

**Step 3 — Score normalization in fusion (2 marks):**

- In **multi-modal** or **multi-algorithm** systems, raw scores from different matchers lie in incompatible ranges (e.g., Hamming distance ∈ [0,1] vs. Cosine similarity ∈ [−1,1]).
- Score normalization maps them to a common scale using **min-max**, **z-score**, or **tanh** normalization.
- After normalization, scores can be **fused** (sum-rule, weighted-sum, max-rule) to produce a single decision score, which is then thresholded.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 — ROC construction (2.5 marks):**

- The ROC plots **GAR (= 1 − FRR)** on the y-axis against **FAR** on the x-axis, as the threshold $\tau$ is swept from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.
- Each point on the curve is an operating point. The **diagonal line** ($GAR = FAR$) represents a random (chance) matcher.
- The closer the ROC is to the **top-left corner**, the better the matcher.

**Step 2 — Threshold influence (2 marks):**

- **Low $\tau$** (lenient) → high GAR, high FAR → user-friendly but insecure.
- **High $\tau$** (strict) → low FAR, low GAR → secure but frustrating for users.
- The **EER point** is the unique intersection of the ROC with the line $GAR = 1 - FAR$ (or equivalently $FAR = FRR$).

**Step 3 — ZeroFAR operating point (2.5 marks):**

- The **ZeroFAR** point is the GAR value achieved when $\tau$ is set so high that $FAR = 0$.
- This means *no impostor is ever accepted*, regardless of how many attack trials are made.
- **Importance in security-critical applications:** Border control, military base access, and nuclear-facility entry require ZeroFAR because the cost of a false acceptance (a terrorist entering) is catastrophic, far outweighing the inconvenience of a few false rejections.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Matching Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing FAR with FRR** — Students often swap the two. Remember: FAR is when an *impostor* is wrongly **accepted**; FRR is when a *genuine* user is wrongly **rejected**.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization** — When asked for normalized Hamming distance, students frequently forget to divide by the bit-length $d$, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign of the threshold rule** — A similarity-based matcher uses `Accept if score ≥ τ`; a distance-based matcher uses `Accept if score ≤ τ`. Mixing these up reverses the entire decision.
> 4. **Not specifying the modality in metric choice** — Simply stating "Euclidean distance" without saying *for which feature type* loses 1–2 marks. Always tie the metric to the modality.
> 5. **Skipping the units in d-prime** — Although d-prime is dimensionless, you must show the formula and the substitution step clearly.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Matching** compares a probe template $Q$ against one (verification) or many (identification) gallery templates $T_i$.
- ✅ The matcher outputs a **score**; the threshold $\tau$ converts this score into a **binary decision** (Accept / Reject).
- ✅ **Verification = 1:1**; **Identification = 1:N** — the *N* changes the computational complexity and database design.
- ✅ **Euclidean** and **Cosine** distances dominate for *real-valued* feature vectors (face, voice).
- ✅ **Normalized Hamming Distance** dominates for *binary* codes (iris, palmprint, finger-vein).
- ✅ **FAR ↓ as $\tau$ ↑** (for distance-based matchers), and **FRR ↑ as $\tau$ ↑** — the classic trade-off.
- ✅ **EER** is the single-number summary; **lower is better**.
- ✅ **ROC** plots GAR vs FAR; the area under the ROC (AUC) and the EER are the two most-cited performance indices.
- ✅ **d-prime** quantifies the *separability* of the two score distributions; **higher is better**.
- ✅ **Score normalization** (min-max / z-score / tanh) is **mandatory** before fusing scores from different matchers in multi-modal systems.
- ✅ **ZeroFAR** is the security-critical operating point where $FAR = 0$; the corresponding GAR is the *worst-case genuine acceptance rate under zero-impostor-acceptance*.
- ✅ Daugman's iris threshold is **~0.32**; FaceNet/ArcFace threshold is **~0.6** (cosine similarity on normalized embeddings).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heterogeneous Face Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heterogeneous Face Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heterogeneous Face Recognition (HFR)** is a specialized sub-domain of face biometrics that addresses the problem of matching face images acquired from **different sensing modalities, capture devices, or imaging conditions**, where the probe (query) and gallery (reference) images belong to visually distinct domains.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, HFR is formally defined as:

> "The process of establishing the identity of a subject by comparing facial images captured in two or more heterogeneous domains such that the inter-domain variation (called the *modality gap*) is significantly larger than the intra-class variation (variation between images of the same person)."

Common heterogeneous pairings studied in biometric security include:

- **Near-Infrared (NIR) to Visible Light (VIS)** matching
- **Sketch to Photograph** matching (for forensic/legal use)
- **Thermal (THM) to Visible** matching
- **Low-Resolution to High-Resolution (LR–HR)** matching
- **3D Depth to 2D Photograph** matching
- **Cross-Spectral** matching (e.g., UV, multispectral)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a detective trying to identify a suspect. You have a **black-and-white artist's sketch** drawn from a witness's description, but the police database only contains **colored digital photographs** taken by CCTV. To a human, recognizing the suspect from sketch-to-photo is possible because we focus on *structural cues* (face shape, nose width, eye spacing) rather than superficial details (skin tone, lighting, photo texture).

HFR systems aim to **teach a machine the same trick**: ignore the domain-specific "texture" and focus on the *identity-preserving geometry and structure* of the face. The "modality gap" is analogous to the language barrier between two people who speak different languages — the goal is to find a common "interpreter" (feature space) so the underlying identity (the message) can be correctly understood on both sides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight):** Homogeneous Face Recognition (HFR is NOT the same as HFR — HFR stands for Heterogeneous, not Homogeneous) deals with images of the **same modality**, e.g., two visible-light photographs. Heterogeneous Face Recognition (HFR) deals with **cross-modal** images.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in HFR

The following performance metrics are evaluated against the KTU Board standard:

- **Rank-1 Accuracy:** $\mathbf{R1}$ — probability that the top match is the correct identity.
- **Verification Accuracy (VA):** correct accept / reject rate at a fixed False Accept Rate (FAR).
- **Equal Error Rate (EER):** operating point where FAR = False Reject Rate (FRR).
- **Cumulative Match Characteristic (CMC):** curve of recognition rate vs. rank $k$.
- **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC):** Genuine Acceptance Rate (GAR) vs. FAR.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU theory papers, you are expected to know that **lower EER and higher Rank-1** indicate a superior HFR algorithm. EER is expressed as a **percentage (%)**, while similarity scores between probe and gallery embeddings are dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Is HFR Hard? — The Modality Gap

The central challenge is the **modality gap** — a non-linear, complex distribution shift between the source domain $D_s$ and target domain $D_t$. For any two face images $x_i \in D_s$ and $x_j \in D_t$ of the **same identity**, the Euclidean distance in pixel space satisfies:

$$d(x_i, x_j) \gg d(x_i, x_i') \quad \text{where } x_i' \in D_s$$

This violates the **homogeneous biometric assumption** that inter-class distance > intra-class distance.

### 2.2 Three Major Approaches to HFR

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes HFR solutions into three broad families:

#### 2.2.1 Synthesis-Based Methods
- Convert the probe image from one modality to the other (e.g., sketch → photo).
- Once synthesized, a standard face matcher is used.
- **Examples:** Markov Random Field (MRF) based sketch synthesis, CycleGAN-based photo synthesis.

#### 2.2.2 Common Subspace / Feature-Based Methods
- Project both modalities into a **shared latent subspace** where the modality gap is minimized.
- **Examples:** Partial Least Squares (PLS), Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA), Coupled Subspace Learning, Transfer Subspace Learning.

#### 2.2.3 Deep Learning / Invariant Feature Methods
- Train CNNs to learn **modality-invariant embeddings**.
- **Examples:** Siamese networks, Triplet networks, ArcFace with domain adversarial training, TransFace, IDiff-Face.

### 2.3 Theoretical Foundation — Coupled Subspace Learning

Given paired data $\{(x_i^s, x_i^t)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from source and target modalities, the goal is to find projection matrices $P_s, P_t$ such that:

$$y_i^s = P_s^T x_i^s \quad \text{and} \quad y_i^t = P_t^T x_i^t$$

are aligned in a common subspace (i.e., $y_i^s \approx y_i^t$ for matching pairs).

The objective is to maximize correlation (CCA) or minimize reconstruction error with shared structure (Coupled Mappings).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation / Definition** | **Purpose / Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Modality Gap | $\Delta_{mod} = \mathbb{E}[d(x_i^s, x_i^t)] - \mathbb{E}[d(x_i^s, x_j^s)]$ for same identity $i$, different $j$ | Quantifies the difficulty of HFR |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(a, b) = \dfrac{a \cdot b}{\Vert a \Vert \cdot \Vert b \Vert}$ | Standard matching score in deep HFR |
| CCA Objective | $\max_{P_s, P_t} \dfrac{P_s^T C_{st} P_t}{\sqrt{P_s^T C_{ss} P_s \cdot P_t^T C_{tt} P_t}}$ | Finds maximally correlated projections |
| Cross-Modality Distance | $d_{cm}(x^s, x^t) = \Vert f_\theta(x^s) - f_\theta(x^t) \Vert_2$ | Used by Siamese / Triplet networks |
| Triplet Loss | $L = \max\bigl(d_{cm}(a, p) - d_{cm}(a, n) + \alpha, \; 0\bigr)$ | Anchor $a$, positive $p$, negative $n$, margin $\alpha$ |
| EER | EER $=$ FAR $=$ FRR | Standard operating point metric |
| Rank-k Recognition | $R(k) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\text{correct ID in top-}k]$ | Used in CMC curves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Triplet Loss is a high-frequency 14-mark topic. Always define anchor, positive, negative, and the margin $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 0.2$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forensic Identification:** Match a suspect's sketch to mugshot databases.
- **Law Enforcement under Low-Light:** Match NIR surveillance footage to daylight photo databases.
- **Border Control:** Match face images from e-passport chips (often low-res, NIR) to live visible-light captures.
- **Anti-Spoofing:** Detect printed photo attacks by cross-checking thermal vs. visible images.
- **Cross-Camera Tracking:** Match a face across CCTV cameras of varying make, resolution, and IR capability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) for HFR

CCA is a foundational KTU theory topic. We derive the projection vectors that maximize correlation between two modalities.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Let $X_s \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ be the source modality feature matrix and $X_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times q}$ be the target modality matrix, each with $n$ paired samples. We want to find vectors $w_s \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ and $w_t \in \mathbb{R}^{q}$ such that the correlation between projected variables is maximized.

**Step 2 — Correlation Coefficient Between Projections:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{w_s^T \, X_s^T X_t \, w_t}{\sqrt{(w_s^T X_s^T X_s w_s)(w_t^T X_t^T X_t w_t)}}$$

**Step 3 — Use Sample Covariance Notation:**

$$C_{ss} = \tfrac{1}{n} X_s^T X_s, \quad C_{tt} = \tfrac{1}{n} X_t^T X_t, \quad C_{st} = \tfrac{1}{n} X_s^T X_t$$

So the objective becomes:

$$\rho = \dfrac{w_s^T C_{st} w_t}{\sqrt{(w_s^T C_{ss} w_s)(w_t^T C_{tt} w_t)}}$$

**Step 4 — Constrained Optimization:**
Because $\rho$ is scale-invariant, we fix the denominators to 1, i.e.:

$$w_s^T C_{ss} w_s = 1, \quad w_t^T C_{tt} w_t = 1$$

The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L} = w_s^T C_{st} w_t - \tfrac{\lambda_1}{2}(w_s^T C_{ss} w_s - 1) - \tfrac{\lambda_2}{2}(w_t^T C_{tt} w_t - 1)$$

**Step 5 — Partial Derivatives and Stationarity:**

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_s} = C_{st} w_t - \lambda_1 C_{ss} w_s = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{st} w_t = \lambda_1 C_{ss} w_s$$

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_t} = C_{st}^T w_s - \lambda_2 C_{tt} w_t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{st}^T w_s = \lambda_2 C_{tt} w_t$$

**Step 6 — Combining to Form Generalized Eigenvalue Problem:**

From the second equation, $w_t = \tfrac{1}{\lambda_2} C_{tt}^{-1} C_{st}^T w_s$. Substituting into the first:

$$C_{st} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\lambda_2} C_{tt}^{-1} C_{st}^T w_s = \lambda_1 C_{ss} w_s$$

Rearranging:

$$C_{ss}^{-1} C_{st} C_{tt}^{-1} C_{st}^T w_s = \lambda^2 w_s, \quad \text{where } \lambda = \lambda_1 = \lambda_2$$

**Step 7 — Solution:**
The projection vector $w_s$ is the eigenvector of $C_{ss}^{-1} C_{st} C_{tt}^{-1} C_{st}^T$ corresponding to its largest eigenvalue $\lambda^2$, and $w_t$ is recovered analogously. The top $k$ eigenvectors yield a $k$-dimensional common subspace.

> [!NOTE]
> This generalized eigenvalue formulation is the bedrock of **Coupled Subspace Learning** for HFR and frequently appears in 14-mark KTU questions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of HFR Methods

| **Method Family** | **Core Idea** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Synthesis-Based | Translate probe to gallery modality | Reuses existing matchers; interpretable | Synthesis artifacts hurt accuracy | Sketch-photo, NIR-VIS |
| Common Subspace (CCA, PLS) | Project both modalities into shared space | Mathematically tractable; closed-form | Linear assumption; requires paired data | Small-scale academic setups |
| Siamese / Triplet CNN | Learn distance metric directly | Captures non-linearity; scalable | Needs many triplets; sensitive to mining | Large-scale modern HFR |
| GAN-based Synthesis (CycleGAN) | Unpaired image-to-image translation | No paired data required | Training instability; mode collapse | Heterogeneous face datasets |
| Domain-Invariant Features (ArcFace + DANN) | Adversarial domain confusion | State-of-the-art accuracy | Complex training pipeline | Modern cross-spectral systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic HFR System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Probe Image Xs] --> B[Preprocessing]
    C[Gallery Image Xt] --> D[Preprocessing]
    B --> E[Modality-Specific Feature Extractor]
    D --> F[Modality-Specific Feature Extractor]
    E --> G[Coupled Subspace Projection Ps]
    F --> H[Coupled Subspace Projection Pt]
    G --> I[Common Embedding Space]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Similarity Computation]
    J --> K[Match or Non-Match Decision]
    J --> L[CMC / EER Evaluation]

    style A fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#cfc,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#fc9,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- `Probe Image Xs` (top-left, pink) and `Gallery Image Xt` (top-right, blue) are inputs from two heterogeneous modalities (e.g., sketch and photo).
- Each is independently preprocessed (alignment, normalization, face detection).
- Modality-specific CNNs extract initial features.
- Coupled projection matrices $P_s$ and $P_t$ (learned via CCA, PLS, or neural projection heads) embed both features into a **common identity space** (green).
- The matching module computes similarity (cosine or Euclidean), producing a binary decision and CMC/EER statistics.

### 4.2 HFR Algorithm Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Heterogeneous Face Recognition HFR]
    R --> S1[Synthesis Based]
    R --> S2[Common Subspace Based]
    R --> S3[Invariant Feature Based]

    S1 --> S1a[MRF Sketch Synthesis]
    S1 --> S1b[CycleGAN Photo Synthesis]
    S1 --> S1c[Pix2Pix Translator]

    S2 --> S2a[CCA / KCCA]
    S2 --> S2b[PLS Regression]
    S2 --> S2c[Coupled Dictionary Learning]
    S2 --> S2d[Transfer Subspace Learning]

    S3 --> S3a[Siamese CNN]
    S3 --> S3b[Triplet Loss Network]
    S3 --> S3c[Domain Adversarial NN DANN]
    S3 --> S3d[ArcFace with Cross Modal Loss]

    style R fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style S1 fill:#fdd,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#dfd,stroke:#333
    style S3 fill:#ddf,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The tri-fork of **Synthesis / Common Subspace / Invariant Feature** is the standard taxonomy expected in KTU 2024 Scheme exam answers. Memorize the three branches and at least one representative algorithm per branch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
Define Heterogeneous Face Recognition. List any two real-world scenarios where HFR is required.

**Model Answer:**
Heterogeneous Face Recognition (HFR) is the process of matching facial images captured in two or more different sensing modalities, capture devices, or imaging domains, where the inter-domain variation (modality gap) exceeds the intra-class variation.
Two real-world scenarios:

1. **Forensic Sketch Matching:** Matching a hand-drawn suspect sketch (artistic modality) to a digital mugshot database.
2. **Cross-Spectral Surveillance:** Matching a low-light NIR surveillance frame to a high-resolution visible-light photo in the gallery.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for scenarios with one-line justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the "modality gap" in HFR? Why does it make recognition difficult?

**Model Answer:**
The **modality gap** is the distributional difference between two image domains of the same identity (e.g., sketch vs. photo). It is quantified as the expected distance between paired cross-modal samples minus the expected distance between same-modality samples.
It makes recognition difficult because conventional similarity metrics (Euclidean, cosine) computed in raw pixel space are dominated by *modality-specific* texture, color, and illumination rather than by *identity-preserving* facial structure. As a result, an image of the same person in a different modality may be farther from its true match than from images of unrelated persons.

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for reasoning.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **three main approaches** to solving Heterogeneous Face Recognition. For each, give one representative algorithm and a one-line description of how it handles the modality gap. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Approach | Algorithm | How It Bridges the Modality Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Synthesis-Based** | CycleGAN | Translates probe image into the gallery modality so a pre-trained homogeneous matcher can be used. |
| 2 | **Common Subspace** | CCA (Canonical Correlation Analysis) | Projects both modalities into a shared low-dimensional subspace where their correlation is maximized. |
| 3 | **Invariant Feature** | Siamese CNN with Triplet Loss | Learns a metric embedding such that same-identity cross-modal pairs are closer than different-identity pairs. |

*(2 marks for naming, 3 marks for explanation, 1 mark for diagram mention, 1 mark for summary.)*

**(b)** Derive the **Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA)** objective for two modalities and show that the optimal projection direction $w_s$ is the leading eigenvector of the matrix $C_{ss}^{-1} C_{st} C_{tt}^{-1} C_{st}^T$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

Key checkpoints expected by the examiner:
- Statement of correlation objective (1 mark).
- Lagrangian formulation with two constraints (2 marks).
- Partial derivative w.r.t. $w_s$ and $w_t$ (2 marks).
- Reduction to generalized eigenvalue problem and final conclusion (2 marks).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **Triplet Loss** formulation used in Siamese networks for HFR. Clearly define anchor, positive, negative, and the margin hyperparameter $\alpha$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Triplet Loss** trains an embedding network $f_\theta(\cdot)$ such that images of the same identity — possibly from different modalities — are pulled closer than images of different identities.

For a triplet $(a, p, n)$ where:
- $a$ = anchor image (any modality),
- $p$ = positive image (same identity, possibly different modality),
- $n$ = negative image (different identity),

the loss is:

$$L(a, p, n) = \max\bigl(\Vert f_\theta(a) - f_\theta(p) \Vert_2^2 - \Vert f_\theta(a) - f_\theta(n) \Vert_2^2 + \alpha, \; 0\bigr)$$

Here $\alpha > 0$ is the **margin** — a safety buffer ensuring the negative sample is at least $\alpha$ farther than the positive in the embedding space. If the constraint is already satisfied, the loss is zero (no update).

In HFR specifically:
- The anchor and positive are typically chosen from *different modalities* (e.g., sketch anchor, photo positive) to enforce modality invariance.
- The negative can be from either modality.

*(1 mark for triplet definition, 2 marks for the loss equation, 2 marks for margin role, 1 mark for HFR-specific usage, 1 mark for example.)*

**(b)** Compare **synthesis-based** and **common-subspace-based** HFR methods in terms of (i) training data requirement, (ii) accuracy on sketch-photo matching, and (iii) computational complexity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Synthesis-Based | Common-Subspace-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Paired Training Data** | Required (for MRF, Pix2Pix); CycleGAN works unpaired | Paired data is essential for CCA/PLS; otherwise unsupervised variants needed |
| **(ii) Accuracy on Sketch-Photo** | Limited by synthesis artifacts; hallucinates texture | Strong when modalities are linearly related; degrades for complex sketches |
| **(iii) Computational Complexity** | High — needs a generative model (GAN) inference per probe | Low — closed-form solution via generalized eigendecomposition |

*(3 marks for paired data, 2 marks for accuracy analysis, 2 marks for complexity.)*

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are lost:**
> 1. **Conflating "Homogeneous" and "Heterogeneous"** in the definition (–2 marks). Always explicitly mention that the two images come from *different modalities*.
> 2. **Writing the Triplet Loss without the margin $\alpha$ or the $\max(\cdot, 0)$ hinge** (–1.5 marks). The hinge is what makes it a *loss* and not just a distance difference.
> 3. **In CCA derivations, students often forget the constraint** $w_s^T C_{ss} w_s = 1$. Without it, the Lagrangian is unsolvable (–2 marks).
> 4. **Confusing Rank-1 Accuracy with EER** (–1 mark). Rank-1 is a *closed-set identification* metric, EER is a *verification* metric. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the *modality pair* explicitly** (e.g., saying "HFR" without saying "NIR-to-VIS") loses the application context (–0.5 marks).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ HFR matches faces across **different imaging modalities** (sketch-photo, NIR-VIS, thermal-VIS, LR-HR).
- ✅ The **modality gap** is the core challenge — it makes pixel-space distance unreliable.
- ✅ Three solution families: **Synthesis-based**, **Common Subspace**, **Invariant Feature Learning**.
- ✅ **CCA** finds projections $w_s, w_t$ that maximize cross-modal correlation via a generalized eigenvalue problem on $C_{ss}^{-1} C_{st} C_{tt}^{-1} C_{st}^T$.
- ✅ **Triplet Loss** = $\max(d_{ap} - d_{an} + \alpha, 0)$ with margin $\alpha$ enforces same-identity proximity in embedding space.
- ✅ **Rank-1 Accuracy** = identification metric; **EER** = verification metric (lower is better).
- ✅ Real-world uses: **forensic sketch matching**, **cross-spectral surveillance**, **border control**, **anti-spoofing**.
- ✅ Modern SOTA uses **deep metric learning** (Siamese, Triplet, ArcFace with domain adversarial training).
- ✅ Always state the **modality pair** explicitly in exam answers.
- ✅ CycleGAN enables **unpaired synthesis**; Pix2Pix/MRF require **paired data**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signature Scan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signature Scan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Signature Scan** is a **behavioral biometric modality** that authenticates an individual based on the unique physiological and behavioral characteristics exhibited during the act of signing. Unlike physiological biometrics (e.g., fingerprint, iris), signature verification relies on **what a person does** (a learned motor skill) combined with subtle anatomical traits, making it a **hybrid behavioral-physiological biometric**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, signature recognition is classified into two fundamental paradigms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition**
> **Static (Offline) Signature Verification:** Verification performed after the signing process is complete, using only the 2D image of the signature. The verifier analyzes the *shape*, *geometry*, and *spatial distribution* of pixels.
>
> **Dynamic (Online) Signature Verification:** Verification performed *during* the signing process by capturing time-varying signals such as pen-tip **pressure**, **velocity**, **acceleration**, **azimuth angle**, **altitude angle**, and **stroke sequencing** using specialized electronic digitizers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a friend you have known for years. Even if a stranger perfectly traced your friend's signature on paper, you could *probably* still tell it's a forgery by asking them to sign in front of you. Why?

- A **forger** can copy the *shape* (the static look), but they cannot easily mimic the **rhythm**, the **pen lift pauses**, the **hesitation strokes**, the **pressure variation**, and the **pen tilt** that have been subconsciously trained into your friend's hand over decades.

**Signature Scan works on exactly this principle:**
- **Offline mode** = the *forensic handwriting expert* studying the ink on paper.
- **Online mode** = the *keen observer* watching the hand move, noting the speed, pressure, and pen lifts in real time.

The underlying motor pattern is governed by the **Central Nervous System (CNS)** and proprioceptive feedback, making it highly individualistic. It is measured against two statistical error benchmarks: **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** and **False Rejection Rate (FRR)**, ideally converging at the **Equal Error Rate (EER)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Signature is a "Behavioral" Biometric**
> A signature is a *learned act*, not an innate trait. Illness, intoxication, aging, injury, or emotional state can temporarily alter a person's signing pattern, leading to higher intra-class variability compared to physiological traits like fingerprints.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & System Vocabulary

The following terminology forms the core vocabulary of Signature Scan systems and frequently appears in KTU theory questions:

- **Enrollment Template ($\mathbf{T_i}$):** The set of reference signature samples stored during registration (typically 3–10 genuine samples).
- **Genuine Score Distribution ($\mathbf{S_G}$):** Distribution of similarity scores when comparing samples from the same individual.
- **Impostor Score Distribution ($\mathbf{S_I}$):** Distribution of similarity scores when comparing samples from different individuals.
- **Threshold ($\mathbf{\tau}$):** Decision boundary above which a sample is accepted.
- **Skilled Forgery:** A forgery produced by a forger who has practiced the victim's signature.
- **Random Forgery:** A forgery produced without any knowledge of the victim's signature (e.g., the forger's own signature used as a probe).
- **Simple Forgery:** A forgery based on visual familiarity with the victim's signature style.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Signature Scan System

A signature recognition system, whether offline or online, operates through a standardized **pipeline**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate each stage with technical precision.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)

- **Offline:** High-resolution optical scanner (typically **300–600 DPI**), camera, or flatbed scanner. Output is a grayscale or binary image.
- **Online:** Specialized digitizing tablets (e.g., Wacom Intuos), PDAs with capacitive touchscreens, or stylus-based smart pens (e.g., Apple Pencil, Livescribe). Sampling rate is usually **100–200 Hz**.

### Stage 2 — Pre-processing

Raw signature data is rarely usable directly. Preprocessing standardizes inputs:

- **Image Pre-processing (Offline):**
  1. *Binarization* using Otsu's thresholding
  2. *Skew correction* via Hough transform
  3. *Noise removal* using morphological operations (erosion/dilation)
  4. *Bounding box normalization* to a fixed canvas (e.g., 128×256 pixels)
  5. *Thinning/skeletonization* for stroke topology analysis
- **Signal Pre-processing (Online):**
  1. *Low-pass filtering* to remove high-frequency sensor noise
  2. *Resampling* to normalize the number of points across signatures
  3. *Smoothing* using moving average or Savitzky-Golay filters
  4. *Baseline removal* and *size normalization*

### Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (The Core Stage)

This is where KTU examiners frequently pose 7 or 14 mark questions. Features divide into three categories:

#### A. Static (Geometric) Features
- **Image-level:** Aspect ratio, bounding box dimensions, pixel density, slant angle, center of mass, connected components, number of loops.
- **Geometric moments:** The **$(p+q)^{th}$ order geometric moment** of a binary signature image is defined as:

$$M_{p,q} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} x^p \cdot y^q \cdot f(x,y)$$

where $f(x,y) \in \{0,1\}$ is the pixel intensity. From these, **Hu's seven invariant moments** are derived — these are invariant to translation, rotation, and scale.

#### B. Dynamic (Functional) Features
- **Time-domain:** Stroke duration, pen-up/pen-down timings, number of strokes, velocity profile $v(t)$.
- **Pressure profile:** $p(t)$ — typically normalized to [0, 1].
- **Spatial trajectory:** $x(t), y(t)$ at timestamp $t$.
- **Derivative signals:** Instantaneous speed $v(t) = \sqrt{\dot{x}(t)^2 + \dot{y}(t)^2}$ and acceleration $a(t) = \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$.
- **Angular features:** Azimuth (yaw) $\theta_a(t)$ and altitude (pitch) $\theta_p(t)$ of the pen.

#### C. Statistical / Transform-Domain Features
- **Wavelet coefficients** (e.g., Haar, Daubechies db4) for multi-resolution analysis.
- **Fourier descriptors** for closed-contour shape representation.
- **Directional histograms** capturing local stroke orientation.

### Stage 4 — Matching / Classification

A test signature $\mathbf{X}$ is compared to the enrollment template $\mathbf{T_i}$ to produce a similarity score $S(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{T_i})$. Matching algorithms include:

| Algorithm Family | Examples | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Distance-based** | Euclidean, Manhattan, Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) | Online signals, time series |
| **Statistical** | Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), Hidden Markov Models (HMM) | Online sequential data |
| **Kernel-based** | Support Vector Machines (SVM) with RBF kernel | Static features |
| **Neural** | Deep CNNs, LSTMs, Siamese Networks | End-to-end feature learning |
| **Template-based** | Correlation, Hausdorff distance | Offline images |

### Stage 5 — Decision

- If $S(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{T_i}) \geq \tau$, **accept** the claimant (Genuine).
- If $S(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{T_i}) < \tau$, **reject** (Impostor).
- The threshold $\tau$ is tuned to balance **security** (low FAR) against **usability** (low FRR).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $M_{p,q} = \sum \sum x^p y^q f(x,y)$ | Geometric moment of order $(p,q)$ | Shape descriptor |
| $v(t) = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$ | Instantaneous pen-tip speed | Online dynamic feature |
| $a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt}$ | Pen-tip acceleration | Online dynamic feature |
| $\text{FAR}(\tau) = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Acceptance Rate | Security metric |
| $\text{FRR}(\tau) = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | False Rejection Rate | Usability metric |
| $\text{EER}$ | $\tau$ where $\text{FAR}(\tau) = \text{FRR}(\tau)$ | System accuracy benchmark |
| $\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \frac{FP + FN}{\text{Total}}$ | Overall classification accuracy | General evaluation |
| $\text{DTW}(X,Y) = \min \sum \Vert x_i - y_j \Vert$ | Dynamic Time Warping distance | Online signature alignment |
| $\theta_a(t) = \arctan(\dot{y}/\dot{x})$ | Pen azimuth angle | Online dynamic feature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Signature Scan**
> Signature verification is the **only behavioral biometric with legal standing in many jurisdictions** for contracts, banking instruments, and legal documents. It is used in: bank cheque processing, credit card transaction authentication, e-passport verification, secure document signing, forensic handwriting analysis, and high-value legal document workflows. The global biometric signature market is valued in billions of dollars, with banking as the largest vertical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Geometric Moments for Offline Signature Representation

A complete derivation of how moments characterize a signature image is a frequent KTU 14-mark question. The geometric moment of order $(p,q)$ over a discrete 2D binary signature image is:

$$M_{p,q} = \sum_{x=0}^{W-1} \sum_{y=0}^{H-1} x^p \cdot y^q \cdot I(x,y)$$

where $I(x,y) \in \{0,1\}$ is the pixel intensity (1 for ink, 0 for background) and the image dimensions are $W \times H$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Zero-Order Moment

Setting $p=0$ and $q=0$ gives the total ink area (number of foreground pixels), often denoted $m_{00}$:

$$M_{0,0} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} I(x,y) = N_{\text{ink}}$$

This represents the **total ink density** of the signature.

### Step 2 — Compute First-Order Moments (Centroid)

The first-order moments $M_{1,0}$ and $M_{0,1}$ are used to find the **centroid** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ of the signature:

$$M_{1,0} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} x \cdot I(x,y)$$

$$M_{0,1} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} y \cdot I(x,y)$$

The centroid is then:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{M_{1,0}}{M_{0,0}}, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{M_{0,1}}{M_{0,0}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Central Moments (Translation Invariance)

To make features invariant to where the signature was placed on paper, define **central moments**:

$$\mu_{p,q} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} (x - \bar{x})^p (y - \bar{y})^q I(x,y)$$

### Step 4 — Normalize to Achieve Scale Invariance

Normalized central moments $\eta_{p,q}$ make features independent of signature size:

$$\eta_{p,q} = \frac{\mu_{p,q}}{(\mu_{0,0})^{\gamma}}, \quad \text{where } \gamma = \frac{p+q}{2} + 1$$

### Step 5 — Derive Hu's Seven Invariant Moments

Hu (1962) combined normalized central moments into **seven algebraic invariants** that are invariant to translation, scale, and rotation. The first three are shown below — they are the most commonly used in KTU questions:

$$I_1 = \eta_{2,0} + \eta_{0,2}$$

$$I_2 = (\eta_{2,0} - \eta_{0,2})^2 + 4\eta_{1,1}^2$$

$$I_3 = (\eta_{3,0} - 3\eta_{1,2})^2 + (3\eta_{1,2} - \eta_{0,3})^2$$

**Why this works:** Because the moments are computed about the centroid (translation-invariant), normalized by the zero-order moment (scale-invariant), and combined through algebraic operations that cancel rotational effects, the resulting $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_7$ are stable descriptors of signature shape regardless of where, how large, or how rotated the signature appears.

### Step 6 — Distance Computation Between Two Signatures

Given test signature moments $I_k^{\text{test}}$ and enrolled signature moments $I_k^{\text{ref}}$ for $k=1,\ldots,7$, the matching distance is:

$$D = \sum_{k=1}^{7} \left\vert \log \vert I_k^{\text{test}} \vert - \log \vert I_k^{\text{ref}} \vert \right\vert$$

The decision rule:

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Genuine} & \text{if } D < \tau \\ \text{Impostor} & \text{if } D \geq \tau \end{cases}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Time Warping for Online Signature Alignment

Online signatures are time series, and two genuine signatures of the same person almost never have identical point counts. **Dynamic Time Warping (DTW)** is the gold-standard alignment algorithm.

Given two sequences $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$ and $Y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_M)$:

**Step 1:** Construct an $N \times M$ cost matrix $C$ where $C(i,j) = \Vert x_i - y_j \Vert_2$.

**Step 2:** Define a cumulative cost matrix $\gamma(i,j)$:

$$\gamma(i,j) = C(i,j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} \gamma(i-1,j) \\ \gamma(i-1,j-1) \\ \gamma(i,j-1) \end{Bmatrix}$$

with boundary conditions $\gamma(1,1) = C(1,1)$, $\gamma(i,1) = \sum_{k=1}^{i} C(k,1)$, and $\gamma(1,j) = \sum_{k=1}^{j} C(1,k)$.

**Step 3:** The DTW distance is:

$$\text{DTW}(X,Y) = \gamma(N,M)$$

**Intuition:** DTW allows the algorithm to "stretch" or "compress" segments of one signature to align with the other. This is critical because a person's signature is rarely produced at the same speed twice — the overall duration varies, but the **shape of the velocity profile is preserved**.

## 3.3 Performance Metrics Derivation

The KTU 2024 ESE pattern demands clear definitions. The **confusion matrix** has four cells:

| | **Actual Genuine** | **Actual Impostor** |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Genuine** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Predicted Impostor** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

**False Acceptance Rate (FAR)** — security-critical metric:

$$\text{FAR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

**False Rejection Rate (FRR)** — usability-critical metric:

$$\text{FRR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$$

**Equal Error Rate (EER)** — the operating point where security and usability are balanced:

$$\text{EER} = \text{FAR}(\tau^*) = \text{FRR}(\tau^*) \quad \text{where } \tau^* = \arg\min_{\tau} \left\vert \text{FAR}(\tau) - \text{FRR}(\tau) \right\vert$$

**Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Signature Scan System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional pipeline of an online signature verification system, from acquisition to final decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Presents Signature on Digitizing Tablet] --> B[Sampling at 100-200 Hz]
    B --> C[Raw Time Series: x_t, y_t, p_t, theta_a, theta_p]
    C --> D[Pre-processing: Filtering and Resampling]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction Module]
    E --> F1[Static Features: Shape and Geometry]
    E --> F2[Dynamic Features: Pressure, Velocity, Acceleration]
    E --> F3[Angular Features: Azimuth and Altitude]
    E --> F4[Transform Features: Wavelet and Fourier]
    F1 --> G[Feature Vector Construction]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{Matcher: Distance or Classifier}
    H --> I[Reference Template Database]
    H --> J[Similarity Score S]
    J --> K{Score vs Threshold tau}
    K -->|S greater than or equal to tau| L[Accept: Genuine User]
    K -->|S less than tau| M[Reject: Possible Impostor]
    L --> N[Audit Log and Decision Output]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Offline vs Online Signature Verification — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OFFLINE[Offline Static Verification]
        O1[Optical Scanner or Camera] --> O2[Image Pre-processing: Binarize, Skew Correct, Normalize]
        O2 --> O3[Feature Extraction: Hu Moments, Geometric Descriptors]
        O3 --> O4[Template Matching: Correlation or Distance]
        O4 --> O5[Accept or Reject Decision]
    end
    subgraph ONLINE[Online Dynamic Verification]
        P1[Pen on Digitizing Tablet] --> P2[Time-Series Capture: x t, y t, p t, angles]
        P2 --> P3[Signal Pre-processing: Filter and Smooth]
        P3 --> P4[Feature Extraction: Velocity, Acceleration, Pressure Profile]
        P4 --> P5[Temporal Alignment: DTW or HMM]
        P5 --> P6[Accept or Reject Decision]
    end
```

## 4.3 Forgery Threat Model Hierarchy

Signature forgery exists on a spectrum of difficulty. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to recognize this hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forgery Types] --> B[Random Forgery: Forger uses own signature]
    A --> C[Simple Forgery: Forger has seen signature once or twice]
    A --> D[Skilled Forgery: Forger has practiced the signature]
    A --> E[Simulated Forgery: Forger traces over authentic signature]
    B --> F[Lowest Threat: Easily Detected]
    C --> G[Moderate Threat]
    D --> H[High Threat: Most Challenging]
    E --> I[Critical Threat: Often requires human expert]
    F --> J[Static Features May Suffice]
    G --> K[Requires Dynamic Feature Analysis]
    H --> L[Requires Multi-modal Feature Fusion]
    I --> M[Requires Forensic Examiner Intervention]
```

## 4.4 Decision Threshold and Error Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Threshold tau Selection] --> B{Application Priority}
    B -->|High Security: Bank Vault| C[Set tau High]
    C --> D[Result: Low FAR, High FRR: Strict but Frustrating]
    B -->|High Usability: Daily Login| E[Set tau Low]
    E --> F[Result: High FAR, Low FRR: Convenient but Risky]
    B -->|Balanced: General Use| G[Set tau at EER]
    G --> H[Result: FAR equals FRR at Operating Point]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define signature verification. Differentiate between static and dynamic signature verification with two points.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

Signature verification is the process of authenticating an individual by analyzing the characteristics of their handwritten signature. It is a behavioral biometric that leverages the unique motor pattern developed over years of repetitive signing.

| Aspect | Static (Offline) Verification | Dynamic (Online) Verification |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | 2D image of the completed signature | Time-varying signals captured during signing |
| **Features Used** | Shape, geometry, spatial pixel distribution | Pressure, velocity, acceleration, pen tilt, stroke order |
| **Device** | Scanner, camera | Digitizing tablet, smart pen, capacitive screen |
| **Forgery Resistance** | Vulnerable to skilled static forgery | Significantly harder to forge due to dynamic features |

### Question 2: List any six features used in online signature verification systems.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional II]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pen-tip coordinates** $x(t), y(t)$ — spatial trajectory.
2. **Pressure** $p(t)$ — applied force on the writing surface, normalized to [0, 1].
3. **Velocity** $v(t) = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$ — first derivative of position.
4. **Acceleration** $a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt}$ — second derivative of position.
5. **Azimuth angle** $\theta_a(t)$ — pen yaw in the plane of the paper.
6. **Altitude angle** $\theta_p(t)$ — pen pitch, perpendicular to the paper.
7. **Stroke timing** — pen-up and pen-down events with associated durations.
8. **Total duration** of the signing process.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students may choose between two alternative questions.

### Question A: Comprehensive Analysis of Online Signature Verification

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the complete architecture of an online signature verification system. Describe in detail the **data acquisition** and **pre-processing** stages.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **dynamic features** extracted during online signature verification. Explain how **Dynamic Time Warping (DTW)** is used for matching two online signature samples.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The online signature verification system architecture consists of five sequential stages:

**1. Data Acquisition Layer:**
The user signs on a digitizing tablet (e.g., Wacom, Synaptics) equipped with an electromagnetic resonance stylus. The tablet samples the pen-tip position at **100–200 Hz**, simultaneously recording:
- Cartesian coordinates $x(t), y(t)$ of the pen-tip
- Axial pressure $p(t)$ on the writing surface
- Azimuth angle $\theta_a(t)$ and altitude angle $\theta_p(t)$ of the stylus

The resulting raw data is a multi-channel time series $\mathbf{S}(t) = [x(t), y(t), p(t), \theta_a(t), \theta_p(t)]^T$.

**2. Pre-processing Stage:**
- **Filtering:** A low-pass Butterworth filter (cutoff $\approx 20$ Hz) removes high-frequency sensor noise and tremor artifacts.
- **Resampling:** Different signatures have different durations. All samples are resampled to a fixed length (e.g., 256 points) using cubic spline interpolation to enable vector-level comparison.
- **Smoothing:** A Savitzky-Golay filter of window size 7 and polynomial order 2 reduces noise while preserving the underlying signal shape.
- **Size normalization:** Each $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ is mapped to a canonical $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ canvas.
- **Baseline correction:** Drift in the digitizer is removed by subtracting the median $y$ value.

**3. Feature Extraction:** (Detailed in part b).

**4. Matching:** DTW or HMM computes a similarity score.

**5. Decision:** Score is compared against threshold $\tau$.

**Block Diagram (Textual Reconstruction):**

```
Pen Input -> Digitizer -> Low-Pass Filter -> Resampler -> Smoother -> Normalizer
   -> Feature Extractor -> Matcher <-> Template DB -> Threshold Comparator -> Decision
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Dynamic Features in Online Signature Verification:**

Dynamic features capture the *time-varying motor pattern* of the signer. The most important features are:

1. **Velocity profile** $v(t)$: Speed of pen-tip movement. Genuine signers show consistent velocity curves despite duration variation.

2. **Acceleration profile** $a(t)$: Captures the jerk and fluidity of strokes. Skilled forgers struggle to replicate the exact acceleration rhythm.

3. **Pressure profile** $p(t)$: Force variation. A forger cannot easily mimic the natural pressure buildup and release of a genuine signer.

4. **Pen-up / pen-down events:** The number of strokes, their durations, and the timing of pen lifts are highly individualistic.

5. **Angular features:** $\theta_a(t)$ and $\theta_p(t)$ capture how the pen is held and rotated during signing.

**Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) for Matching:**

DTW aligns two time series of different lengths optimally. For two signature samples $X = (x_1, \ldots, x_N)$ and $Y = (y_1, \ldots, y_M)$:

**Step 1:** Compute the pointwise distance matrix $C(i,j) = \Vert x_i - y_j \Vert_2$.

**Step 2:** Build the cumulative cost matrix recursively:

$$\gamma(i,j) = C(i,j) + \min\{\gamma(i-1,j), \gamma(i-1,j-1), \gamma(i,j-1)\}$$

**Step 3:** The DTW distance is $\gamma(N,M)$.

**Step 4:** Compare to a learned threshold $\tau$. If $\gamma(N,M) < \tau$, the signature is accepted.

**Why DTW for Signatures:** Two genuine signatures of the same person rarely have the same duration, but the *shape* of their velocity and pressure profiles is consistent. DTW allows non-linear time alignment, making it ideal for matching such signals. It is robust to small temporal shifts, hesitations, and pauses.

### Question B: Forgery Analysis and Performance Evaluation

**(a)** [7 Marks] Classify the different **types of forgeries** encountered in signature verification systems. Which type is the most difficult to detect, and why?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Define **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)**, **False Rejection Rate (FRR)**, and **Equal Error Rate (EER)**. Sketch the typical **DET (Detection Error Tradeoff) curve** and explain its significance in choosing the operating threshold.

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification of Forgeries in Signature Verification:**

Forgeries are classified into four categories based on the forger's knowledge and preparation:

1. **Random Forgery:** The forger uses their own signature in place of the victim's. The forger has *no knowledge* of the genuine signature. Easiest to detect since shape and rhythm differ entirely.

2. **Simple Forgery:** The forger has casually observed the genuine signature once or twice and attempts to write it from memory. Detection requires basic static feature analysis.

3. **Skilled Forgery:** The forger has *practiced* the victim's signature extensively, often using a copy as reference. This is the most challenging automated scenario since the static shape closely matches. Detection requires **dynamic feature analysis** (pressure, velocity, stroke order).

4. **Simulated (Traced) Forgery:** The forger traces over an authentic signature, sometimes using a light table. This is the **most difficult to detect** because the trace follows the genuine path, but subtle pen dynamics and tremor patterns still betray the forgery in expert hands.

**The most difficult to detect is the Skilled Forgery or Simulated Forgery** for the following reasons:
- The static image features (Hu moments, geometry) closely match the genuine template.
- The forger can replicate the visual appearance but struggles to reproduce the **neuromuscular rhythm** of the original signer.
- Modern systems counter this by emphasizing dynamic features (pressure, velocity) and using **writer-specific feature weighting**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition of Performance Metrics:**

- **False Acceptance Rate (FAR):** The probability that an impostor is incorrectly accepted as a genuine user. Computed as:

$$\text{FAR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

- **False Rejection Rate (FRR):** The probability that a genuine user is incorrectly rejected. Computed as:

$$\text{FRR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$$

- **Equal Error Rate (EER):** The operating point where FAR equals FRR. A lower EER indicates better system performance. Computed as:

$$\text{EER} = \text{FAR}(\tau^*) = \text{FRR}(\tau^*) \quad \text{where } \tau^* \text{ minimizes } \vert \text{FAR} - \text{FRR} \vert$$

**DET (Detection Error Tradeoff) Curve — Verbal Sketch:**

```
FRR (%)
  | \
  |   \
  |     \  <-- FRR decreases as tau increases
  |       \
  |         \___
  |             \  <-- EER Point (Intersection)
  |               \___
  |                   \  <-- FAR increases as tau increases
  |                     \____
  +----------------------------------> tau
   Low                          High
```

**Significance of the DET Curve:**

- The DET curve plots FRR against FAR as a function of the decision threshold $\tau$.
- The **intersection point** of the two curves is the **EER**, representing the best-balanced operating point.
- **High-security applications** (e.g., bank vault access) operate at *low FAR* (high $\tau$), accepting the trade-off of higher FRR.
- **High-usability applications** (e.g., daily phone unlock) operate at *low FRR* (low $\tau$), accepting the trade-off of higher FAR.
- The **steepness** of the DET curve indicates system separability: a steeper curve implies a more decisive system with a clearly defined EER.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing FAR and FRR:** Many students swap FAR and FRR. Remember: **FAR is about impostors being accepted** (security risk); **FRR is about genuine users being rejected** (usability problem).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention sampling rate:** When asked about online signature acquisition, students often skip the sampling rate (**100–200 Hz**), which is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Not distinguishing static from dynamic features:** Examiners specifically look for the table-form distinction. Writing only "shape" or "pressure" without categorization loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the confusion matrix:** Defining FAR/FRR without first stating the four-cell confusion matrix (TP, FP, FN, TN) is incomplete.
> 5. **Not addressing forgery hierarchy:** A 7-mark question on forgeries must explicitly rank them (Random < Simple < Skilled < Simulated) and justify *why* skilled forgery is hardest.
> 6. **Missing the EER definition formula:** Writing only "EER is where FAR equals FRR" without the formal $\tau^*$ equation loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Signature Scan (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Signature Scan is a **behavioral biometric** based on the unique motor pattern of a person's signing act.
- **Two Paradigms:** **Static (offline)** uses 2D images; **Dynamic (online)** uses time-varying signals (pressure, velocity, pen tilt).
- **Sampling Rate (Online):** Typically **100–200 Hz** on digitizing tablets.
- **DPI (Offline):** Scanners operate at **300–600 DPI** for high-fidelity image capture.
- **Key Dynamic Features:** $x(t), y(t), p(t), v(t), a(t), \theta_a(t), \theta_p(t)$, stroke timing, pen-up/pen-down events.
- **Key Static Features:** Geometric moments, **Hu's seven invariant moments**, aspect ratio, slant, loops, connected components.
- **Hu Moments:** $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_7$ are invariant to translation, scale, and rotation — ideal for offline signature matching.
- **DTW:** Standard algorithm for aligning online signature time series of different lengths; allows non-linear temporal warping.
- **HMMs & GMMs:** Statistical models that capture the probabilistic distribution of signing dynamics, robust to intra-user variability.
- **Forgery Hierarchy (ascending threat):** Random → Simple → Skilled → Simulated. Skilled forgery is the most critical challenge.
- **Performance Metrics:** FAR (security), FRR (usability), EER (balanced accuracy), Accuracy, F1-Score.
- **DET Curve:** Plots FRR vs. FAR as $\tau$ varies; intersection gives EER.
- **Threshold Selection:** High $\tau$ = low FAR, high FRR (high security). Low $\tau$ = high FAR, low FRR (high usability).
- **Legal Standing:** Signature is the **only behavioral biometric with broad legal acceptance** for contracts and banking.
- **Limitations:** Intra-class variability due to age, illness, fatigue, emotional state; not as stable as physiological biometrics.
- **Applications:** Bank cheque verification, credit card authentication, e-signatures, legal document signing, forensic analysis.
- **Formula to Memorize (Top 3):**
  1. $v(t) = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$
  2. $\text{FAR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$, $\text{FRR} = \frac{FN}{FN+TP}$
  3. $M_{p,q} = \sum_x \sum_y x^p y^q I(x,y)$
- **Common Examiner Hook Words:** "Compare," "Differentiate," "Justify," "Sketch and explain," "Derive," "List with examples."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Keystroke Scan: Components and Working Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Keystroke Scan: Components and Working Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Keystroke Scan (Keystroke Dynamics / Typing Biometrics)** is a behavioral biometric authentication technique that verifies or identifies an individual by analyzing the unique, measurable characteristics of their typing rhythm on a conventional or specialized keyboard. The system captures temporal and pressure-based features of keyboard interaction events—namely key-press latencies, release latencies, and inter-key transitions—and matches them against a pre-enrolled user template using statistical or machine learning algorithms.

According to the KTU 2024 PECCT635 syllabus (Module 2), keystroke scan is classified as a **behavioral (non-physiological) biometric modality** that operates on the principle that each individual exhibits a consistent, subconsciously learned, and highly distinctive motor pattern when interacting with a keyboard, analogous to a handwritten signature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECCT635 Module 2):** Keystroke scan is treated as a "soft biometric" — it offers continuous authentication capability, requires no specialized hardware beyond a standard QWERTY keyboard, and is widely deployed in surveillance and insider-threat detection systems.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Dwell Time (Hold Time) | $T_d$ (ms) | Duration a key remains physically pressed |
| Flight Time (Latency) | $T_f$ (ms) | Time between release of one key and press of the next |
| Press-to-Press Time (Digraph Latency) | $T_{pp}$ (ms) | Time between successive key presses |
| Release-to-Press Time | $T_{rp}$ (ms) | Time gap between key release and next key press |
| Inter-key Interval | $T_{ik}$ (ms) | Aggregate time gap between adjacent keystrokes |
| Typing Speed | $v$ (cps) | Characters per second |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Musician's Touch":**
> Imagine two pianists playing the same melody on an identical piano. The sheet music (the typed text) is the same, but the *way* they touch the keys is fundamentally different. Pianist A holds the C major chord for **420 ms**, releases it, and strikes the next chord **90 ms** later. Pianist B holds the same chord for **610 ms** and pauses **160 ms** before the next strike. The **audience (system)** cannot tell *what* is being played from this, but the *temporal fingerprint* of each player is unmistakable. Keystroke biometrics works on this exact principle: it doesn't care **what** you type, it cares **how fast, how long, and in what rhythm** you type it.

## 1.4 Classification of Keystroke Biometrics

Keystroke scan can be deployed in **two operating modes**:

1. **Static (Verification at Login):** A user types a fixed password or passphrase; the system measures keystroke timing only during this single authentication event. Used for workstation login.
2. **Dynamic (Continuous Authentication):** The system monitors keystroke patterns in the background throughout the entire active session, periodically re-verifying the user. Used in high-security government, defense, and insider-threat systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** The industry-standard timing resolution for keystroke capture is **1 millisecond (1 ms)**. Modern systems can resolve down to **0.1 ms** using specialized hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Layered Logic Breakdown

The keystroke scan system can be decomposed into **five core operational layers**, each with a distinct "Why" and "How":

### Layer 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensor Layer)
- **What:** Captures raw keyboard events (key-down, key-up timestamps).
- **How:** Uses operating system hooks (Windows: `WH_KEYBOARD_LL`; Linux: `evdev` interface) or a hardware-level keyboard microcontroller (e.g., PS/2, USB HID interrupt packets polled at 125 Hz–1000 Hz).
- **Why:** To obtain a ground-truth timestamped event stream $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n\}$ where each $e_i = (key_i, t_{down,i}, t_{up,i})$.

### Layer 2 — Feature Extraction (Signal Processing Layer)
- **What:** Converts raw event stream into measurable biometric features.
- **How:** Computes dwell time, flight time, n-graph (n-gram) latencies, typing speed, error rate, and (in advanced systems) pressure/force vectors.
- **Why:** Raw events are too granular; features reduce dimensionality and increase class separability.

### Layer 3 — Template Storage (Database Layer)
- **What:** Stores the enrolled user's reference feature vector $\mathbf{T}_{\text{ref}}$.
- **How:** Typically takes $N$ enrolment samples (usually $N = 5$ to $10$ repetitions of a fixed or free text), computes their mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ to model intra-user variability.
- **Why:** Biometric features exhibit natural variability; a single sample is statistically insufficient.

### Layer 4 — Pattern Matching / Comparison Engine (Decision Layer)
- **What:** Computes a similarity/dissimilarity score $S$ between live sample $\mathbf{X}$ and stored template $\mathbf{T}_{\text{ref}}$.
- **How:** Uses one of the algorithms in §2.2 below.
- **Why:** A single scalar score is required for threshold-based binary decision.

### Layer 5 — Decision Module (Output Layer)
- **What:** Returns ACCEPT or REJECT.
- **How:** Compares $S$ against a pre-defined threshold $\tau$: if $S \geq \tau$, ACCEPT; else REJECT.
- **Why:** Translates the continuous similarity score into a discrete authentication decision.

## 2.2 Core Matching Algorithms

| Algorithm | Distance / Score Formula | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu_i)^2}$ | Simplest; ignores feature correlation |
| **Manhattan Distance** | $d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Mahalanobis Distance** | $d = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | Accounts for feature correlation & variance |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $S = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{x} \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \boldsymbol{\mu} \vert\vert}$ | Scale-invariant, vector-orientation-based |
| **Neural Network (MLP)** | $S = \sigma(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$ | Learns non-linear class boundaries |
| **Scaled Manhattan (Monrose-Rubin)** | $S = \sum w_i \cdot \vert x_i - \mu_i \vert$ | Weighted; used in seminal 1997 paper |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_d(i) = t_{up,i} - t_{down,i}$ | Dwell time of $i$-th keystroke |
| 2 | $T_f(i) = t_{down,i+1} - t_{up,i}$ | Flight time between key $i$ and key $i+1$ |
| 3 | $T_{pp}(i) = t_{down,i+1} - t_{down,i}$ | Press-to-press digraph latency |
| 4 | $d_E(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - \mu_i)^2}$ | Euclidean distance score |
| 5 | $d_M(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | Mahalanobis distance score |
| 6 | $S_{\cos}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}}{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert \boldsymbol{\mu}\vert\vert}$ | Cosine similarity |
| 7 | $\text{FAR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Acceptance Rate (security) |
| 8 | $\text{FRR} = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | False Rejection Rate (usability) |
| 9 | $\text{EER}$: point where $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ | Equal Error Rate (overall accuracy) |
| 10 | $T = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | Total digraphs in $N$-character text |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Continuous Authentication:** Government agencies (e.g., defense PSUs) use keystroke dynamics to detect insider threats in real time without disrupting workflow.
- **E-commerce Fraud Detection:** Banks monitor typing rhythm during online transactions to detect session hijacking.
- **Mobile Banking:** Smartphones use on-screen tap dynamics (a close cousin of keystroke scan) as a second factor.
- **Healthcare:** EHR systems in hospitals verify the identity of the clinician typing patient notes to prevent record-tampering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dwell Time and Flight Time from Raw Events

**Given:** A user types the string `"HELLO"`. The keyboard event timestamps (in ms) are recorded as:

| Key | $t_{down}$ (ms) | $t_{up}$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| H | 0 | 110 |
| E | 165 | 270 |
| L | 320 | 425 |
| L | 485 | 595 |
| O | 645 | 770 |

**Find:** (a) Dwell time for each key, (b) Flight time between each pair of consecutive keys, (c) Press-to-press latency for the first three digraphs.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Dwell Times using $T_d(i) = t_{up,i} - t_{down,i}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d(H) &= 110 - 0 = 110 \text{ ms} \\
T_d(E) &= 270 - 165 = 105 \text{ ms} \\
T_d(L_1) &= 425 - 320 = 105 \text{ ms} \\
T_d(L_2) &= 595 - 485 = 110 \text{ ms} \\
T_d(O) &= 770 - 645 = 125 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Flight Times using $T_f(i) = t_{down,i+1} - t_{up,i}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f(H \to E) &= 165 - 110 = 55 \text{ ms} \\
T_f(E \to L_1) &= 320 - 270 = 50 \text{ ms} \\
T_f(L_1 \to L_2) &= 485 - 425 = 60 \text{ ms} \\
T_f(L_2 \to O) &= 645 - 595 = 50 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Press-to-Press Latencies using $T_{pp}(i) = t_{down,i+1} - t_{down,i}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pp}(H \to E) &= 165 - 0 = 165 \text{ ms} \\
T_{pp}(E \to L_1) &= 320 - 165 = 155 \text{ ms} \\
T_{pp}(L_1 \to L_2) &= 485 - 320 = 165 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** Press-to-press latency is the *sum* of the preceding dwell time and the following flight time, i.e., $T_{pp}(i) = T_d(i) + T_f(i)$. Students frequently confuse these three timings in exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mahalanobis Distance Score Calculation

**Given:** A 2-D keystroke feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2) = (95, 60)$ from a live sample. The enrolled template has mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (100, 55)$ and covariance matrix:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 25 & 8 \\ 8 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Find:** Mahalanobis distance $d_M$ and decide ACCEPT/REJECT if threshold $\tau = 2.5$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the deviation vector $\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$:**

$$
\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 95 - 100 \\ 60 - 55 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$:**

First, find the determinant:

$$
\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = (25)(16) - (8)(8) = 400 - 64 = 336
$$

Then the inverse:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} = \frac{1}{336}\begin{bmatrix} 16 & -8 \\ -8 & 25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04762 & -0.02381 \\ -0.02381 & 0.07440 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the quadratic form $(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.04762 & -0.02381 \\ -0.02381 & 0.07440 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (0.04762)(-5) + (-0.02381)(5) \\ (-0.02381)(-5) + (0.07440)(5) \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.23810 - 0.11905 \\ 0.11905 + 0.37202 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -0.35715 \\ 0.49107 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the transpose $(-5, 5)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) &= (-5)(-0.35715) + (5)(0.49107) \\
&= 1.78575 + 2.45535 \\
&= 4.2411
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$
d_M = \sqrt{4.2411} \approx 2.059
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$
d_M = 2.059 \quad < \quad \tau = 2.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{ACCEPT}}
$$

The live sample is within the threshold and the user is authenticated.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EER Computation from FAR / FRR Curves

**Given:** A keystroke system reports:
- At threshold $\tau = 0.30$: FAR = 0.08, FRR = 0.02
- At threshold $\tau = 0.45$: FAR = 0.03, FRR = 0.05
- At threshold $\tau = 0.50$: FAR = 0.02, FRR = 0.07
