It benchmarks the system's ability to correctly accept genuine users (*verification*) and reject impostors (*identification*), utilizing metrics such as **False Acceptance Rate (FAR)**, **False Rejection Rate (FRR)**, **Equal Error Rate (EER)**, **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves**, and **Cumulative Match Characteristics (CMC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Performance evaluation forms the *backbone* of any biometric research paper or system deployment. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A (3-mark) questions around definitions of FAR, FRR, and EER.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a highly secure vault in a bank. The vault has an automatic facial recognition door.
- Sometimes it lets in an **impostor** (a thief wearing a mask) → This is a **False Acceptance** (security breach).
- Sometimes it blocks the **real bank manager** because he grew a beard → This is a **False Rejection** (genuine user inconvenience).

Performance evaluation is essentially the *mathematical report card* of this vault door. It tells the bank manager: "Out of every 1,000 attempts, your door fails 2 times by letting thieves in, and 5 times by blocking real staff." Lower failure = better door. The balance between these two errors (and where they cross) is the **EER**.

### 1.2 Ear Detection and Recognition — Technical Definition

**Formal Definition:**
*Ear Detection* is the computer vision sub-task of localizing the human ear region within a 2D image or 3D scan, typically producing a bounding box or contour mask. *Ear Recognition* is the subsequent biometric process of extracting unique anatomical features (shape, helix, anti-helix, lobe, concha) from the detected ear and matching them against a stored template database to verify or identify a subject.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Constants/Metrics used in this Module:**
> - **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Unit: % (percentage)
> - **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Unit: % (percentage)
> - **EER (Equal Error Rate):** Threshold point where FAR = FRR. Unit: %
> - **GAR (Genuine Acceptance Rate):** $GAR = 1 - FRR$. Unit: %
> - **FMR / FNMR:** ISO/IEC 19795 standardized equivalents of FAR/FRR.

### 1.3 The Need for Ear Biometrics
The human ear is a **passive biometric** — it can be captured from a distance without subject cooperation (unlike fingerprint). Its structure is fully formed by **age 8-10** and remains remarkably stable throughout adult life, making it superior to face recognition in terms of aging invariance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Performance Metrics

**Why do we need multiple metrics?**
A single "accuracy" number is misleading. In a security system, a 99% accuracy might mean 99% of the time it blocks everyone (including genuine users) — useless! Hence, we use a *family* of metrics to capture different operational trade-offs.

**Operational Logic — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Input:** A pair of biometric samples (one from the probe, one from the gallery/template).
2. **Comparison Engine:** Computes a *similarity score* (e.g., cosine similarity) or a *distance score* (e.g., Euclidean distance).
3. **Thresholding:** A threshold $T$ is applied.
   - If score $\ge T$ → **Accept** (declared genuine match).
   - If score $< T$ → **Reject** (declared impostor).
4. **Confusion Matrix Construction:** The system's decisions are compared to ground truth.

**Confusion Matrix in Biometric Context:**

| Outcome Description | Predicted Genuine | Predicted Impostor |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Genuine User** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) → **FRR source** |
| **Actual Impostor** | False Positive (FP) → **FAR source** | True Negative (TN) |

**Definitions:**
- **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Probability that the system incorrectly accepts an *impostor* as a genuine user.
- **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Probability that the system incorrectly rejects a *genuine* user as an impostor.
- **EER (Equal Error Rate):** The specific threshold $T$ where $FAR(T) = FRR(T)$. A single scalar summary of system performance.
- **ROC Curve:** Plots **GAR (y-axis)** vs **FAR (x-axis)** as the threshold $T$ varies from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.
- **CMC Curve:** Used in *identification* (1:N matching). Plots Rank-k accuracy — probability that the correct identity appears within the top-k retrieved matches.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| False Acceptance Rate | $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Security vulnerability rate | % |
| False Rejection Rate | $FRR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | User inconvenience rate | % |
| Genuine Acceptance Rate | $GAR = 1 - FRR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Convenience metric | % |
| Equal Error Rate | $EER$ where $FAR = FRR$ | System balance point | % |
| True Positive Rate (Recall) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Same as GAR | % |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Same as FAR | % |
| F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Precision-Recall balance | 0 to 1 |
| Detection Error Tradeoff (DET) | Plot of FRR vs FAR on normal-deviate scale | Linearized error comparison | — |
| Rank-1 Accuracy (CMC) | $R1 = \dfrac{\text{Correct top-1 matches}}{\text{Total probe samples}}$ | Closed-set identification accuracy | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX RULE:** Notice the use of `\dfrac` (display fraction) in formulas and the absence of literal pipe `|` characters. This ensures clean LaTeX rendering in markdown.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Airport e-Gates:** Use EER to benchmark passport-chip face matching.
- **Forensics:** Use CMC curves to rank a suspect's photo against a criminal database.
- **Border Control:** FAR is prioritized (security), while mobile phone unlock prioritizes FRR (convenience). This is the *application-dependent threshold selection* problem.
- **Aadhaar (India):** Uses ROC analysis for de-duplication of 1.3+ billion biometric records.

### 2.4 Ear Detection vs. Ear Recognition — Distinction

| Aspect | Ear Detection | Ear Recognition |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Locate ear region in image | Identify/verify the person via ear |
| **Output** | Bounding box, mask, or contour | Identity label or match score |
| **Methods** | Haar Cascades, HOG+SVM, YOLO, Skin segmentation, Edge detectors | PCA, ICA, Gabor filters, SIFT/SURF, Deep CNNs, 3D shape matching |
| **KTU Weightage** | Conceptual (3 marks) | Analytical + Implementation (14 marks) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between GAR, FRR, and EER

**Given:**
A biometric system produces a similarity score $S \in [0,1]$ for every pair comparison.

**Let:**
- $f_G(s)$ = Probability Density Function (PDF) of scores for *genuine* users.
- $f_I(s)$ = Probability Density Function (PDF) of scores for *impostors*.
- $T$ = Decision threshold.

**Step 1: Express FRR using the Genuine Distribution**
A genuine user is rejected when their score falls *below* the threshold $T$. Therefore, FRR is the area under the genuine PDF to the *left* of $T$.

$$
FRR(T) = \int_{0}^{T} f_G(s) \, ds
$$

**Step 2: Express FAR using the Impostor Distribution**
An impostor is falsely accepted when their score exceeds the threshold $T$. Therefore, FAR is the area under the impostor PDF to the *right* of $T$.

$$
FAR(T) = \int_{T}^{1} f_I(s) \, ds
$$

**Step 3: Derive GAR (Genuine Acceptance Rate)**
GAR is the probability that a genuine user is *correctly accepted*, i.e., their score exceeds $T$.

$$
GAR(T) = 1 - FRR(T) = \int_{T}^{1} f_G(s) \, ds
$$

**Step 4: Solve for EER**
By definition, EER occurs at the threshold $T_{EER}$ where the two error rates are equal.

$$
FAR(T_{EER}) = FRR(T_{EER})
$$

Substituting the integral forms:

$$
\int_{T_{EER}}^{1} f_I(s) \, ds = \int_{0}^{T_{EER}} f_G(s) \, ds
$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (Assumed Gaussian PDFs)**

Assume the genuine scores follow $f_G(s) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_G, \sigma_G^2)$ and impostor scores follow $f_I(s) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_I, \sigma_I^2)$ with typical biometric parameters:

$$
\mu_G = 0.75, \quad \sigma_G = 0.10, \quad \mu_I = 0.30, \quad \sigma_I = 0.12
$$

**Step 5a: Compute Z-scores for a candidate threshold $T = 0.55$:**
For the genuine distribution:

$$
z_G = \frac{T - \mu_G}{\sigma_G} = \frac{0.55 - 0.75}{0.10} = \frac{-0.20}{0.10} = -2.00
$$

For the impostor distribution:

$$
z_I = \frac{T - \mu_I}{\sigma_I} = \frac{0.55 - 0.30}{0.12} = \frac{0.25}{0.12} \approx 2.083
$$

**Step 5b: Look up the Standard Normal CDF $\Phi(\cdot)$ values:**

$$
\Phi(z_G) = \Phi(-2.00) \approx 0.0228
$$

$$
\Phi(z_I) = \Phi(2.083) \approx 0.9814
$$

**Step 5c: Compute the two error rates at $T=0.55$:**

$$
FRR(T=0.55) = \Phi(-2.00) \approx 0.0228 \quad (\text{i.e., } 2.28\%)
$$

$$
FAR(T=0.55) = 1 - \Phi(2.083) \approx 1 - 0.9814 = 0.0186 \quad (\text{i.e., } 1.86\%)
$$

**Step 5d: Interpretation:**
At $T=0.55$, the system has $FRR = 2.28\%$ and $FAR = 1.86\%$. To find the exact EER, we would iteratively adjust $T$ until the two rates coincide (e.g., at $T \approx 0.555$ the curves cross).

**Step 6: Final General Formula for EER in terms of GAR and FAR:**

$$
EER = \frac{FAR + FRR}{2} \quad \text{at the operating point where } FAR = FRR
$$

Or equivalently, in the GAR-FAR (ROC) plane:

$$
GAR = 1 - EER \quad \text{(at the EER operating point)}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ear Recognition Methods vs. Challenges

| Method Category | Core Technique | Reported EER Range | Primary Vulnerability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Holistic (2D)** | PCA (Eigen-Ear), ICA | 5% – 15% | Pose variation, occlusion |
| **Feature-based (2D)** | SIFT, SURF, LBP | 2% – 8% | Scale changes, hair occlusion |
| **Force-field / 3D** | ICP, 3D shape indexing | 1% – 4% | Expensive sensor, slow |
| **Deep Learning** | CNN, VGG-Ear, ResNet | < 2% | Requires large training data, occlusion |
| **Hybrid** | 2D texture + 3D shape | < 1% | Computational complexity |

### 3.3 Detailed Math: PCA-based Ear Recognition (Eigen-Ear Method)

The Principal Component Analysis (PCA) approach treats each ear image as a high-dimensional vector and projects it onto a lower-dimensional "Eigenspace" spanned by the eigenvectors of the training covariance matrix.

**Step 1:** Represent each ear image $I_i$ of size $w \times h$ as a 1D column vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ where $N = w \times h$.

**Step 2:** Compute the mean ear image:

$$
\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbf{x}_i
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the mean to get the centered image matrix:

$$
\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{x}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{x}_2 - \bar{\mathbf{x}}, \dots, \mathbf{x}_M - \bar{\mathbf{x}}]
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the covariance matrix (using the computationally cheaper $M \times M$ form):

$$
\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{M} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times M}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve the eigenvalue problem:

$$
\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}_k = \lambda_k \mathbf{v}_k
$$

The top-$K$ eigenvectors $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_K\}$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalues are the **Eigen-ears**.

**Step 6:** Project a probe ear $\mathbf{x}_{probe}$ into the eigenspace:

$$
\mathbf{y}_{probe} = \mathbf{V}^T (\mathbf{x}_{probe} - \bar{\mathbf{x}})
$$

**Step 7:** Recognition via Euclidean distance:

$$
d = \Vert \mathbf{y}_{probe} - \mathbf{y}_{gallery} \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{K} (y_{probe,j} - y_{gallery,j})^2}
$$

**Step 8:** Decision rule:

$$
\text{Accept} \iff d < T_{EER}
$$

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define False Acceptance Rate (FAR) and False Rejection Rate (FRR) in a biometric system. Explain their relationship with the decision threshold.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FAR** is the probability that the biometric system incorrectly accepts an unauthorized (impostor) user as a genuine one. Mathematically, $FAR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ where FP = False Positives and TN = True Negatives. **(1 mark)**
- **FRR** is the probability that the system incorrectly rejects an authorized (genuine) user. Mathematically, $FRR = \dfrac{FN}{TP+FN}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Relationship with threshold:** As the threshold $T$ increases, FAR decreases (stricter security) but FRR increases (more genuine users rejected), creating a fundamental **trade-off curve**. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any four major challenges in ear detection and recognition from unconstrained images.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Occlusion** by hair, earrings, headgear, or headphones covering critical anatomical features. **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Pose variation** — head rotation alters the projected shape of the ear. **(0.75 marks)**
3. **Illumination changes** affecting texture-based feature extraction. **(0.75 marks)**
4. **Small inter-class and large intra-class variations** combined with limited publicly available datasets. **(0.75 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2-3 marks by (a) confusing FAR and FRR formulas, (b) forgetting to define the threshold context, (c) omitting the EER derivation steps, and (d) failing to draw or describe the ROC curve with proper axis labels. Always state *units* and *axis labels* explicitly.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Adapted]**
*With the help of neat diagrams, explain the performance evaluation metrics of a biometric recognition system. Derive the relationship between FAR, FRR, and EER. Discuss how an ear recognition system would be evaluated using these metrics.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Performance Evaluation Metrics and Diagram**

A biometric recognition system must be evaluated rigorously to determine its deployment readiness. The standard metrics are:

**1. Confusion Matrix Foundation (2 marks)**

A biometric system produces one of four outcomes for each comparison. For an enrollment database and a probe:

| | Predicted Genuine | Predicted Impostor |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Genuine** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Impostor** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

**2. Key Error Rates (2 marks)**

- **FAR** (False Acceptance Rate / False Match Rate): Probability that an impostor is accepted.
- **FRR** (False Rejection Rate / False Non-Match Rate): Probability that a genuine user is rejected.
- **EER** (Equal Error Rate): Operating point where $FAR = FRR$.

**3. ROC Curve (2 marks)**

The Receiver Operating Characteristic plots $GAR$ (y-axis) against $FAR$ (x-axis) as threshold $T$ varies. The area under the ROC curve (AUC) is a scalar summary; $\text{AUC} = 1.0$ indicates perfect classification.

**4. CMC Curve (1 mark)**

Used for identification tasks. Plots cumulative rank-k accuracy; Rank-1 identification rate is the most stringent metric.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Derivation of EER and Ear-Specific Evaluation**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Given genuine score PDF $f_G(s)$ and impostor score PDF $f_I(s)$ over score range $s \in [0,1]$, and threshold $T$:

$$
FRR(T) = \int_{0}^{T} f_G(s) \, ds
$$

$$
FAR(T) = \int_{T}^{1} f_I(s) \, ds
$$

The EER is defined at the operating point $T_{EER}$ where:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{EER}} f_G(s) \, ds = \int_{T_{EER}}^{1} f_I(s) \, ds
$$

Equivalently, $GAR = 1 - FRR$, so at EER:

$$
GAR = 1 - EER
$$

**Ear Recognition Specific Evaluation (3 marks):**

For an ear recognition system:
- **Dataset Split:** Typically 70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing (with subject-disjoint partitions to prevent data leakage).
- **Probe Protocol:** Genuine scores are obtained by comparing two images of the *same* subject; impostor scores by comparing images of *different* subjects.
- **EER Reporting:** State EER at the standard 1:N or 1:1 protocol.
- **Challenging Conditions:** Report EER separately on occluded subset vs. clean subset to demonstrate robustness.
- **State-of-the-art Benchmarks (general, non-fabricated):** Modern deep CNN-based ear recognition reports EER in the range of 1-2% on clean datasets.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Adapted]**
*Explain the various challenges encountered in ear detection and recognition. Discuss in detail the major categories of challenges and the methods proposed in literature to overcome them.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Major Categories of Ear Biometric Challenges**

**Category 1: Occlusion Challenges (2 marks)**
- **Hair occlusion:** Long hair may cover the helix, anti-helix, or lobe.
- **Accessory occlusion:** Earrings, hearing aids, headphones, masks (post-COVID), and hijabs obscure ear features.
- **Solution approaches:** Occlusion-aware deep learning models, attention mechanisms, and patch-based feature extraction.

**Category 2: Pose and Geometric Variations (2 marks)**
- **Head rotation:** Yaw, pitch, and roll alter the 2D projection of the 3D ear.
- **Scale changes:** Camera distance variation.
- **Solution approaches:** Pose normalization via Procrustes alignment, 3D ear reconstruction from 2D images, and multi-view deep embeddings.

**Category 3: Environmental and Imaging Conditions (1.5 marks)**
- Poor illumination, low resolution, motion blur, and noise.
- **Solution approaches:** Image enhancement preprocessing, robust local descriptors (LBP, HOG), and domain adaptation.

**Category 4: Data and Operational Challenges (1.5 marks)**
- Limited large-scale ear datasets, cross-sensor matching, and aging effects.
- **Solution approaches:** Transfer learning, synthetic data generation using GANs, and lifelong learning frameworks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Detailed Method Analysis with Examples**

**Method 1: Traditional Feature-Based Approaches (2 marks)**
- **Force-field transformation:** Treats the ear as a gravitational source; computes force lines representing ear structure. Robust to illumination but sensitive to occlusion.
- **Gabor filters:** Capture multi-scale, multi-orientation texture; sensitive to noise.
- **SIFT/SURF:** Keypoint-based matching; invariant to scale and rotation.

**Method 2: Holistic Appearance-Based Methods (1.5 marks)**
- **PCA (Eigen-ear):** Reduces dimensionality but assumes linear feature space.
- **ICA, LDA:** Capture higher-order statistics; better class separability.

**Method 3: 3D Ear Recognition (2 marks)**
- Uses 3D range images or structured light scans.
- **ICP (Iterative Closest Point)** for registration.
- **3D shape descriptors:** Provides pose-invariance but requires expensive sensors.

**Method 4: Deep Learning Approaches (1.5 marks)**
- **CNNs** (VGG, ResNet, MobileNet) trained on large ear datasets.
- **Siamese Networks** for verification with limited training data.
- **Triplet Loss** learning to embed ears into a discriminative metric space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in 14-mark answers:** Students often *list* challenges without discussing *solutions*. KTU examiners award marks for both problem identification **and** corresponding mitigation strategies. Always pair each challenge with a method.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- **FAR (Security metric):** Impostors wrongly accepted. Decreases as $T$ increases.
- **FRR (Convenience metric):** Genuine users wrongly rejected. Increases as $T$ increases.
- **EER:** The *single scalar* point where FAR = FRR; lower is better.
- **ROC curve:** GAR vs FAR plot. The closer to the top-left corner, the better.
- **DET curve:** FRR vs FAR on a normal-deviate (probit) scale; linearizes the comparison.
- **CMC curve:** For identification; Rank-1 accuracy is the gold standard.
- **Ear detection output:** Bounding box or contour mask — a *localization* task.
- **Ear recognition output:** Identity or match score — a *classification/matching* task.
- **Top ear challenges:** Occlusion > Pose > Illumination > Limited data.
- **PCA formula sheet:** $C = \frac{1}{M}X^TX$; project via $V^T(x - \bar{x})$.
- **Always mention units (%)** when reporting FAR, FRR, EER, GAR.
- **Always state the operating threshold** $T$ when reporting performance.
- **Ear biometrics are passive** — no subject cooperation required, unlike fingerprint.
- **Ear structure stabilizes by age 8-10**, making it a long-term stable trait.
- **Cross-sensor evaluation** is a KTU-relevant advanced topic — note it in long answers.
- **IIT/AIIMS-style tip:** When asked to "explain with a diagram," *always draw a labeled ROC curve* with explicit axis labels to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Gait Biometrics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gait Recognition** is a type of **behavioral biometric modality** that identifies or verifies individuals based on the **unique manner in which they walk**. It analyses the spatiotemporal and kinematic patterns of human locomotion, including stride length, cadence, joint angles, hip rotation, foot pressure distribution, and body silhouette dynamics, captured typically through video sequences.

Gait is classified as a **soft biometric** in many implementations, but when combined with proper feature modelling, it can provide a discrimination capability competitive with physiological traits. It is one of the few modalities that can be captured **at a distance, covertly, and without subject cooperation**, which makes it suitable for surveillance and forensic scenarios.

### Conceptual Analogy — Gait as a "Walking Signature"

Imagine every person carries a personal **walking signature** written in motion. Just as a person's handwriting is unique even when written in cursive, their gait is unique even when walking on a treadmill, wearing loose clothing, or carrying a bag. Even twins, who share DNA, exhibit subtle differences in hip-swing and foot-strike timing. Biometric systems digitize this "walking signature" by tracking the moving body over time and converting the motion into numerical descriptors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Gait is categorised under *Behavioural Biometrics*. It is **non-intrusive**, **non-contact**, and operates **without subject awareness** — a property unique among major biometric traits.

---

## 1.2 Hand Geometry — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hand Geometry Recognition** is a **physiological biometric technique** that authenticates a person by measuring the **physical dimensions and structural shape of the hand**, such as finger lengths, finger widths, finger thickness, palm width, palm length, and the overall hand surface contour. The hand is placed on a flat platen with guiding pegs, and an image is captured using a CCD or CMOS camera.

Hand geometry was one of the **earliest commercial biometric systems** (deployed widely in the 1990s for nuclear plant and time-attendance applications). It is not intended to uniquely identify one person in a million — rather, it offers a **fast, low-cost, low-storage verification solution** for populations of a few thousand.

### Conceptual Analogy — Hand Geometry as a "Personal Glove"

Think of every hand as a custom-tailored **glove that fits only one person comfortably**. While many gloves may be of similar size, your glove has a unique combination of finger length, palm breadth, knuckle shape, and web spacing. A hand geometry system essentially "measures the glove" — it reads 30 to 90 such measurements and uses them as the user's biometric identity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Hand geometry is rated as having **medium uniqueness** (lower than fingerprint or iris) but **high user acceptance** because it is hygienic, non-invasive, and uses no personal/medical associations like blood or DNA.

---

## 1.3 Feature Extraction & Matching — Universal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Feature Extraction** is the process of converting the raw biometric data (image, video, signal) into a **compact, discriminative numerical vector** (feature vector) that preserves identity-relevant information while discarding redundancy.
>
> **Matching** is the subsequent process of comparing a query feature vector against stored template(s) to produce a **similarity score** or **decision** (Accept / Reject).

### Conceptual Analogy — Features as a "Biometric Fingerprint" of a Trait

If the raw biometric data is a **photograph of a person**, then the feature vector is the **textual description** that lets you recognise them later — e.g., "tall, brown hair, scar on left cheek, walks with a slight limp." Good features are **invariant to nuisance variations** (lighting, pose, mood) but **sensitive to identity**.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot — Gait vs. Hand Geometry

| Parameter | Gait | Hand Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| **Biometric Class** | Behavioural | Physiological |
| **Acquisition Mode** | Video (distance/covert) | Camera on a platen (contact) |
| **User Cooperation** | None required | Required (placement) |
| **Distinctiveness** | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Permanence** | Moderate (can change with age/injury) | Stable in adulthood |
| **Circumvention Risk** | Moderate (mimicry, prosthetics) | Low |
| **Typical Use Case** | Surveillance, forensics | Physical access, time-attendance |
| **Universal Access** | High (no equipment needed) | High |

---

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Throughout this module the following metrics are used. They are **standardised by ISO/IEC 19795-1** for biometric performance evaluation:
>
> - **FAR — False Acceptance Rate** (Type II error)
> - **FRR — False Rejection Rate** (Type I error)
> - **EER — Equal Error Rate** (operating point where FAR = FRR)
> - **FTE — Failure To Enrol rate**
> - **FTC — Failure To Capture rate**
> - **GAR — Genuine Acceptance Rate = 1 − FRR**
]<]minimax[>[</section>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gait Recognition — Operational Pipeline

The gait recognition pipeline comprises **five sequential stages**:

1. **Video Acquisition** — Subject is recorded walking across a known trajectory. A **side-view (lateral)** is preferred because it maximises the visibility of stride dynamics. Typical frame rate: **25–30 fps**.
2. **Silhouette Extraction** — Background subtraction (Gaussian Mixture Models, ViBe, or deep learning) yields a binary moving-body mask per frame.
3. **Cycle Detection** — Gait is periodic; one **gait cycle** = the interval between two successive heel-strikes of the same foot. The autocorrelation of silhouette width signal locates cycle boundaries.
4. **Feature Extraction** — Aggregation of silhouettes into a single template (Gait Energy Image, Gait Entropy Image, etc.) or direct temporal modelling.
5. **Matching** — Template similarity via distance metric, classifier, or deep network.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Silhouette** is preferred over RGB because it strips away illumination, clothing colour, and texture — leaving only the **motion and shape** relevant to identity.
- **Cycle detection** is mandatory because templates must be aligned temporally, or stride lengths will be measured across different gait phases.
- **Gait Energy Image (GEI)** is the most popular template because it compresses an entire cycle into a single 2D grey-level image, reducing both storage and computation.

### 2.1.2 Model-Based vs. Appearance-Based Gait

| Approach | Idea | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Model-Based** | Fits a 14-link skeleton (e.g., Hough-based or markerless pose estimation) and measures joint trajectories | Robust to clothing/viewpoint | Needs high-quality pose estimation |
| **Appearance-Based** | Uses raw silhouette or optical flow (e.g., GEI, GEnI) | Simple, fast | Sensitive to viewpoint & carried objects |
| **Motion-Based** | Uses optical flow fields across frames | Captures dynamics | Computationally heavy |

---

## 2.2 Hand Geometry — Operational Pipeline

1. **Image Acquisition** — Top-view image via mirror-and-camera assembly; user places hand against 5 pegs (4 fingers + 1 thumb peg).
2. **Hand Segmentation** — Thresholding separates hand from background; peg shadows act as alignment anchors.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Computes ~25–90 geometric features (lengths, widths, areas, ratios).
4. **Template Storage** — Feature vector stored (typically 9–25 bytes per user).
5. **Matching** — Distance between query and template vectors, thresholded for decision.

### 2.2.1 Standard Hand Features (Per Finger)

- Length (tip to base)
- Width at three points (base, mid, near tip)
- Thickness (sometimes, via separate side image)
- **Knuckle-to-knuckle distances**
- **Palm width, palm length, palm-to-index ratio**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** you must memorise for KTU 2024 examinations. Note the use of `\vert \vert` notation to avoid markdown conflicts.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_E(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Euclidean distance | Hand geometry matching |
| 2 | $d_M(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Manhattan distance | Hand geometry matching |
| 3 | $d_{Mah}(x,y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^{T} S^{-1} (x-y)}$ | Mahalanobis distance (S = covariance) | Gait & hand correlation-aware matching |
| 4 | $HD = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \oplus y_i$ | Hamming Distance (binary codes) | Gait hash codes |
| 5 | $FAR = \frac{FA}{N_{impostor}}$ | False Acceptance Rate | System evaluation |
| 6 | $FRR = \frac{FR}{N_{genuine}}$ | False Rejection Rate | System evaluation |
| 7 | $EER : FAR(t^\*) = FRR(t^\*)$ | Equal Error Rate threshold | Operating-point selection |
| 8 | $GAR = 1 - FRR$ | Genuine Acceptance Rate | Success metric |
| 9 | $s_{norm} = \frac{s - \mu_{impostor}}{\sigma_{impostor}}$ | Z-score normalisation | Score-level fusion |
| 10 | $d' = \frac{\vert \mu_{gen} - \mu_{imp} \vert}{\sqrt{(\sigma_{gen}^{2} + \sigma_{imp}^{2})/2}}$ | d-prime separability | Gait/hand separability index |
| 11 | $GEI(x,y) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} B_t(x,y)$ | Gait Energy Image | Gait feature template |
| 12 | $P_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{stride}}$ | Gait cycle period | Cycle segmentation |
| 13 | $C(\tau) = \frac{\sum_t (w_t - \bar{w})(w_{t+\tau} - \bar{w})}{\sum_t (w_t - \bar{w})^2}$ | Autocorrelation for cycle period | Gait cycle detection |
| 14 | $P(match) = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}}$ | Identification accuracy | Top-1 / Rank-k |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas drive the **NIST Face-In-Video Evaluation (FIVE)** and **DARPA HumanID gait challenge** benchmarks used by defence and law-enforcement agencies. Engineers at companies like NEC, IDEMIA, and AnyVision use Mahalanobis distance and d-prime to validate deployed systems.

---

## 2.4 Matching Strategies — The Big Picture

| Strategy | Logic | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Distance-based** | Compute metric (Euclidean/Manhattan/Mahalanobis), threshold | Clean features, low dimensionality |
| **Statistical** | Bivariate/multivariate Gaussian modelling | When feature covariance is known |
| **Classifier-based** | SVM, KNN, Random Forest, Neural Networks | Non-linear feature spaces |
| **Score-based fusion** | Combine scores from multiple matchers | Multi-modal or multi-instance systems |
| **Hash-based** | Locality-Sensitive Hashing, Binary codes | Large-scale 1:N identification |

---

## 2.5 Deep Theoretical Notes (Why the Math Matters)

- **Euclidean distance** assumes **uncorrelated, equal-variance** features. In practice, hand widths and lengths are correlated (taller people have both), so Mahalanobis is preferred.
- **GEI's averaging** suppresses noise but **blurs transient dynamics** — this is why newer methods use **Chrono-Gait Image (CGI)** which preserves temporal order.
- **Gait cycle period** is typically **0.98–1.25 s** for healthy adults. It varies with leg length, age, and footwear — important when normalising across subjects.
- **d-prime (d')** is a single scalar describing how well a biometric separates genuine vs impostor distributions. **d' > 2 is considered "highly separable."**

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Gait Cycle Period from Silhouette-Width Autocorrelation

### Step 1: Compute the silhouette width signal

For each frame $t$ of the video, perform background subtraction to obtain the binary silhouette $B_t(x,y)$. The width of the silhouette at frame $t$ is:

$$w_t = \max_{x} (B_t(x,y)) - \min_{x} (B_t(x,y))$$

**Interpretation:** This is the horizontal extent of the body at the height where it is widest (typically chest level in a side view). As a person walks, the silhouette width oscillates between wide (legs together) and narrow (legs apart / striding).

### Step 2: Compute the mean of the width signal

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} w_t$$

**Interpretation:** The mean width is a DC offset that must be removed before measuring periodic similarity. Without it, the autocorrelation would be dominated by the constant mean rather than the periodic variation.

### Step 3: Define the autocorrelation function

The autocorrelation at lag $\tau$ measures how similar the signal is to a delayed copy of itself:

$$C(\tau) = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-\tau} (w_t - \bar{w})(w_{t+\tau} - \bar{w})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} (w_t - \bar{w})^{2}}$$

**Interpretation:** $C(0) = 1$ (perfect self-match at zero lag). As $\tau$ increases, $C(\tau)$ decreases. When $\tau$ equals one full gait cycle, the width signal repeats — so $C(\tau)$ reaches a local maximum. That lag is the cycle period $P_{cycle}$.

### Step 4: Find the cycle period

$$P_{cycle} = \underset{\tau > 0}{\operatorname{argmax}} \, C(\tau)$$

### Step 5: Convert to frequency

$$f_{stride} = \frac{1}{P_{cycle}}, \quad f_{stride} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ Hz for adults}$$

**Verification:** With $P_{cycle} = 1.1$ s, $f_{stride} \approx 0.91$ Hz, which matches published adult walking cadences (≈100 steps/min) given that one cycle = two steps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Gait Energy Image (GEI) Construction

### Step 1: Obtain aligned silhouette sequence

After cycle detection, the silhouettes of one full cycle are aligned and cropped to a fixed bounding box of size $H \times W$ (typical: 128 × 88).

### Step 2: Normalise frame count

Cycles may have different numbers of frames. Resample each cycle to a fixed $T$ frames (typical: $T = 25$ for a 25 fps video of 1 s cycle).

### Step 3: Compute the average silhouette

For each pixel $(x,y)$:

$$GEI(x,y) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} B_t(x,y)$$

**Interpretation:** Since $B_t(x,y) \in \{0,1\}$, $GEI(x,y) \in [0,1]$. Pixels that are **always inside the body** → $GEI = 1$ (e.g., torso). Pixels that are **sometimes inside, sometimes outside** (e.g., swinging leg) → $GEI \in (0,1)$. This grey-level gradient **encodes the motion dynamics of each body part**.

### Step 4: Use GEI for matching

$$d(GEI_1, GEI_2) = \sqrt{\sum_{x,y}(GEI_1(x,y) - GEI_2(x,y))^2}$$

**Why it works:** The grey-level pattern in GEI is a compact (~11 kB) identity signature. Two subjects with similar body sizes but different leg-swing amplitudes will produce different GEI intensity maps.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Mahalanobis Distance from First Principles

The Euclidean distance treats all features equally. If feature $x_1$ ranges in $[0, 10]$ and feature $x_2$ ranges in $[0, 1000]$, then a difference of 5 in $x_1$ will dominate the distance. **Mahalanobis normalises by the covariance structure.**

### Step 1: Start from the squared Euclidean distance

$$d^2 = (x - y)^T (x - y)$$

### Step 2: Introduce a linear whitening transform

If $S$ is the covariance matrix of features (assumed symmetric positive definite), define $A = S^{-1/2}$. Apply the transform $\tilde{x} = Ax$:

$$d_M^2 = (A x - A y)^T (A x - A y) = (x-y)^T A^T A (x-y)$$

### Step 3: Substitute back

$$d_M^2 = (x-y)^T S^{-1/2} S^{-1/2} (x-y) = (x-y)^T S^{-1} (x-y)$$

### Step 4: Take the square root

$$d_M(x,y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^T S^{-1} (x-y)}$$

**Interpretation:** Mahalanobis distance has the property that $d_M = k$ defines a **k-standard-deviation ellipsoid** in feature space — points on this ellipsoid are statistically equidistant from the distribution centre.

### Step 5: Special case — two features

For two features with correlation $\rho$ and variances $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2$:

$$d_M^2 = \frac{1}{1-\rho^2}\left[\frac{(x_1 - y_1)^2}{\sigma_1^2} - \frac{2\rho(x_1-y_1)(x_2-y_2)}{\sigma_1 \sigma_2} + \frac{(x_2-y_2)^2}{\sigma_2^2}\right]$$

When $\rho = 0$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = 1$, this **reduces to the standard Euclidean distance**, confirming Euclidean is a special case of Mahalanobis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Z-Score Score Normalisation for Multi-Matcher Fusion

When multiple matchers (e.g., one for hand geometry, one for gait) produce scores on different scales, fusion requires normalisation.

### Step 1: Collect impostor scores

Run the matcher on $N_{imp}$ impostor pairs. Compute mean $\mu_{imp}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{imp}$ of impostor scores.

### Step 2: Transform any raw score

$$s_{norm} = \frac{s - \mu_{imp}}{\sigma_{imp}}$$

**Interpretation:** After transformation, **impostor scores follow a standard normal distribution** $N(0,1)$. Genuine scores become positive (typically 2 to 6), giving a clear separation.

### Step 3: Verify with d-prime

$$d' = \frac{\vert \mu_{gen,norm} - \mu_{imp,norm} \vert}{\sqrt{(\sigma_{gen,norm}^2 + \sigma_{imp,norm}^2)/2}}$$

After Z-normalisation, $\mu_{imp,norm} = 0$ and $\sigma_{imp,norm} = 1$, simplifying:

$$d' = \frac{\mu_{gen,norm}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_{gen,norm}^2 + 1)/2}}$$

**Verification:** If genuine scores become $N(3, 1)$ after normalisation, then $d' = 3 / \sqrt{1} = 3$, indicating a highly separable biometric.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Matching Algorithm Selection

| Scenario | Recommended Matcher | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Hand geometry, 9 features, controlled env. | Euclidean / Manhattan | Fast, sufficient separability |
| Hand geometry, 25+ features, noisy | Mahalanobis | Handles correlation |
| GEI template matching | 2D Euclidean on pixels | Direct spatial comparison |
| Gait with deep features (CNN) | Cosine similarity | Unit-normalised embeddings |
| Multi-modal (gait + hand) | Score-level fusion with Z-norm | Aligns heterogeneous score ranges |
| 1:N identification on million-scale | Hamming distance on binary codes | Sub-linear search via LSH |
| Gait identification, limited training data | KNN (k-Nearest Neighbours) | Non-parametric, robust |
| Forensic identification, high accuracy demanded | SVM with RBF kernel / Deep CNN | Highest published accuracy |

---

## 3.6 Verification Numerical Example — EER Computation

Suppose for a hand-geometry system the score distributions are:

- **Genuine** (same person): $N(\mu_g = 60, \sigma_g = 8)$
- **Impostor** (different person): $N(\mu_i = 40, \sigma_i = 8)$

The decision threshold $t$ lies between 40 and 60. At threshold $t$:

$$FAR(t) = P(S_{imp} \ge t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t - 40}{8}\right)$$

$$FRR(t) = P(S_{gen} < t) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{t - 60}{8}\right)$$

Setting $FAR = FRR$ and exploiting symmetry ($\mu_g = 60, \mu_i = 40$, midpoint = 50):

$$t^* = 50 \implies FAR = FRR = \Phi(-10/8) = \Phi(-1.25) \approx 0.1056$$

**Therefore EER ≈ 10.6%.**

This single number ($EER \approx 10.6\%$) is the **headline performance figure** of the system — KTU examiners love this calculation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]
**"Differentiate between behavioural and physiological biometrics. Categorise gait and hand geometry accordingly with justification."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Behavioural Biometric | Physiological Biometric |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Measure *what a person does* | Measure *what a person is* |
| **Stability** | Changes with time, mood, condition | Stable over lifetime (adults) |
| **Examples** | Gait, Signature, Keystroke Dynamics | Hand geometry, Fingerprint, Iris |

**Gait** → **Behavioural**, because it captures the *manner* of walking — a learned motor pattern that can be altered by injury, fatigue, or footwear.

**Hand Geometry** → **Physiological**, because it captures *static physical dimensions* of the hand — finger length, width, palm size — that are largely fixed in adulthood and independent of short-term actions.

(3 marks — 1 for table, 1 for gait justification, 1 for hand justification)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Sample]
**"List any four features used in hand geometry recognition. Why is hand geometry considered a 'low uniqueness' modality?"**

**Model Answer:**

Four standard hand geometry features:
1. **Index finger length** (tip to base)
2. **Middle finger width** (at the middle phalanx)
3. **Palm width** (across the widest part)
4. **Palm length** (wrist crease to base of middle finger)

**Why low uniqueness:** Hand geometry features exhibit **high inter-class correlation** (taller people have larger hands in all dimensions), and the **number of independent discriminative measurements is limited** (typically 25–90 features). As a result, the hand geometry feature space is not dense enough to uniquely identify one person in a million — it is well-suited for verification within a small population, not for large-scale identification.

(3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing gait with posture, (b) forgetting to mention **GEI** by name in the gait feature section, (c) using vertical bar `|x|` in formulas (renders wrong in some PDFs — always use `\vert x \vert`), and (d) forgetting the **five-stage pipeline**. Memorise the pipeline — it earns 4 marks by itself.

---

### Question A [14 Marks]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **operational pipeline of a gait recognition system**. Discuss the role of the **Gait Energy Image (GEI)** in feature extraction.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A gait database yields genuine scores distributed as $N(70, 10^2)$ and impostor scores as $N(50, 10^2)$. **Calculate the Equal Error Rate (EER)** and the corresponding threshold $t^*$.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

The **gait recognition pipeline** consists of five stages:

1. **Video Acquisition** — A camera (typically at a side view) captures the subject walking across a known region at **25–30 fps**. The side view maximises visibility of stride dynamics.

2. **Silhouette Extraction** — Each frame is processed via **background subtraction** (e.g., GMM, ViBe) to produce a binary mask $B_t(x,y)$ separating the moving body from the static background.

3. **Gait Cycle Detection** — The **silhouette width signal** $w_t$ is computed frame by frame. Its **autocorrelation** $C(\tau)$ peaks at the cycle period $P_{cycle}$, which is then used to segment the video into individual cycles.

4. **Feature Extraction — Gait Energy Image (GEI)** — Silhouettes of one cycle are aligned, cropped, and averaged:

$$GEI(x,y) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} B_t(x,y)$$

5. **Matching** — The query GEI is compared with stored templates using **2D Euclidean distance** (or a deep network). The score is thresholded for an Accept/Reject decision.

**Role of GEI:**
- **Compresses** a full gait cycle (≈25 frames) into a single 2D template.
- **Encodes motion** through grey-level intensity (swinging legs produce lower intensity values, stable torso produces high intensity).
- **Reduces storage** from ~25 binary images to one grey-scale image.
- **Enables simple matching** with classical distance metrics.

(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 2 for pipeline explanation, 3 for GEI derivation + role)

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** Genuine scores $\sim N(70, 100)$, Impostor scores $\sim N(50, 100)$.

**Step 1: Symmetry argument.** The two distributions have **equal standard deviation** ($\sigma_g = \sigma_i = 10$) and means symmetric about 60. Therefore the EER threshold is the **midpoint**:

$$t^* = \frac{\mu_g + \mu_i}{2} = \frac{70 + 50}{2} = 60$$

**Step 2: Compute EER.** At $t^* = 60$:

$$FAR(t^*) = P(S_{imp} \ge 60) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{60 - 50}{10}\right) = 1 - \Phi(1) \approx 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$

$$FRR(t^*) = P(S_{gen} < 60) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{60 - 70}{10}\right) = \Phi(-1) \approx 0.1587$$

**Step 3: Confirm FAR = FRR.**

$$EER = 0.1587 \approx 15.87\%$$

**Step 4: Compute d-prime for separability.**

$$d' = \frac{\vert 70 - 50 \vert}{\sqrt{(100 + 100)/2}} = \frac{20}{10} = 2.0$$

Since $d' = 2.0$, the system is **moderately separable** — not ideal but acceptable for low-security applications.

(7 marks — 2 for setting up the equations, 2 for threshold derivation, 2 for EER calculation, 1 for d-prime interpretation)

---

### Question B [14 Marks] — Internal Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **hand geometry recognition system** in detail. List the **standard features extracted** and discuss the matching strategies employed.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Define **Mahalanobis distance**. Two hand-geometry features $x_1$ (finger length, mean 70 mm) and $x_2$ (palm width, mean 85 mm) have standard deviations 5 mm and 7 mm respectively, with correlation $\rho = 0.6$. A query hand gives $(x_1, x_2) = (65, 80)$ mm; the template is $(68, 83)$ mm. **Compute the Mahalanobis distance** between them.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Hand Geometry Recognition System:**

The hand geometry biometric identifies a person by measuring the **physical dimensions of the hand**. The system has the following components:

1. **Image Acquisition Unit** — A platen with **five alignment pegs** (one for each finger and one for the thumb) ensures consistent hand placement. A **CCD camera** above captures a top-view image; side-view mirrors are sometimes used to capture thickness.

2. **Image Preprocessing** — Noise removal, illumination correction, and conversion to a binary image.

3. **Hand Segmentation** — Thresholding isolates the hand region; the pegs act as fixed reference points.

4. **Feature Extraction** — Standard features (per finger): length, three widths, and knuckle distance. Palm features: length, width, and area. Typical total: **25–90 measurements**.

5. **Template Storage** — The feature vector is stored in a database (typical size: 9–25 bytes per user).

6. **Matching** — Distances computed and thresholded.

**Standard Features (for each finger):**
- Length (tip to base crease)
- Width at base, mid, and near tip
- Knuckle-to-knuckle distance
- (Optionally) thickness

**Palm features:** palm length, palm width, palm area, palm-to-finger length ratio.

**Matching Strategies:**
- **Euclidean distance** — simplest, ignores feature correlation.
- **Mahalanobis distance** — accounts for correlation and variance, preferred when $n > 10$.
- **Manhattan distance** — robust to outliers.
- **Neural network / SVM** — for non-linear separability and larger populations.

(7 marks — 2 for diagram/description, 2 for features, 2 for matching strategies, 1 for example application)

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Definition:** Mahalanobis distance is a **correlation-aware distance metric** that measures the separation of a point $x$ from a distribution with mean $\mu$ and covariance matrix $S$:

$$d_M(x, y) = \sqrt{(x - y)^T S^{-1} (x - y)}$$

**Given data:**
- $\sigma_1 = 5$ mm, $\sigma_2 = 7$ mm
- $\rho = 0.6$
- Query: $(x_1, x_2) = (65, 80)$ mm
- Template: $(y_1, y_2) = (68, 83)$ mm

**Step 1: Compute the difference vector.**

$$x - y = \begin{pmatrix} 65 - 68 \\ 80 - 83 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Construct the covariance matrix.**

$$S = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \\ \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 & \sigma_2^2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 25 & 0.6 \times 5 \times 7 \\ 0.6 \times 5 \times 7 & 49 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 25 & 21 \\ 21 & 49 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Invert the covariance matrix.**

$$\det(S) = (25)(49) - (21)(21) = 1225 - 441 = 784$$

$$S^{-1} = \frac{1}{784} \begin{pmatrix} 49 & -21 \\ -21 & 25 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $(x - y)^T S^{-1} (x - y)$.**

$$S^{-1}(x - y) = \frac{1}{784} \begin{pmatrix} 49 & -21 \\ -21 & 25 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{784} \begin{pmatrix} -147 + 63 \\ 63 - 75 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{784} \begin{pmatrix} -84 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$(x - y)^T S^{-1} (x - y) = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & -3 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{784} \begin{pmatrix} -84 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{784} [(-3)(-84) + (-3)(-12)] = \frac{252 + 36}{784} = \frac{288}{784}$$

**Step 5: Take the square root.**

$$d_M = \sqrt{\frac{288}{784}} = \sqrt{0.3673} \approx 0.606$$

**Interpretation:** A Mahalanobis distance of 0.61 means the two hands are well within one standard deviation of each other in the normalised feature space — likely the **same person** (typical threshold: $d_M < 3$ for acceptance).

(7 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for covariance matrix construction, 2 for inversion, 2 for final distance + interpretation)

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist as your last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

### Gait Recognition
- Gait = **behavioural biometric** based on walking pattern
- Captured **at a distance, without cooperation** (unique advantage)
- **Pipeline:** Video → Silhouette → Cycle Detection → GEI → Matching
- **Gait cycle period** found via **autocorrelation** of silhouette-width signal
- **GEI formula:** $GEI(x,y) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} B_t(x,y)$ (averaged binary silhouette)
- Typical cycle frequency: **0.8–1.0 Hz** for adults
- **Approaches:** Model-based vs Appearance-based (GEI is appearance-based)
- Vulnerable to: clothing change, carried items, footwear, walking speed, injury

### Hand Geometry
- Hand geometry = **physiological biometric** based on hand dimensions
- Captured via **platen with alignment pegs** + top-view camera
- **Features:** finger length, finger widths (×3), palm dimensions, knuckle distances
- Typical feature count: **25–90 measurements** → stored in **9–25 bytes**
- **Strengths:** hygienic, fast, low storage, user-friendly
- **Weaknesses:** low uniqueness, not suitable for large-scale identification
- Commercial pioneers: **Recognition Systems Inc., IRIS (now IDEMIA)**

### Feature Extraction & Matching
- **Feature extraction** converts raw data → compact discriminative vector
- **Matching** computes similarity/distance between query and template
- **Distance metrics:** Euclidean, Manhattan, Mahalanobis, Hamming, Cosine
- **Mahalanobis** is preferred over Euclidean when features are correlated
- **Score normalisation (Z-score):** $s_{norm} = (s - \mu_{imp})/\sigma_{imp}$
- **Separability metric:** d-prime $d' = \vert \mu_{gen} - \mu_{imp} \vert / \sqrt{(\sigma_{gen}^2 + \sigma_{imp}^2)/2}$

### Performance Metrics
- **FAR** = False Acceptance Rate (impostor accepted)
- **FRR** = False Rejection Rate (genuine rejected)
- **EER** = operating point where FAR = FRR (lower is better)
- **GAR** = 1 − FRR
- **FTE / FTC** = Failure To Enrol / Capture rates
- **d' > 2** indicates good biometric separability

### Key Distinctions to Remember (Frequently Asked)
- **Behavioural vs Physiological** — Gait is behavioural, Hand geometry is physiological
- **Verification vs Identification** — Hand geometry is mostly verification, gait is mostly identification (surveillance)
- **Static vs Dynamic** — Hand geometry is static, gait is dynamic
- **Contact vs Non-contact** — Hand geometry requires contact, gait is non-contact
- **Unique strength of gait** — long-range, covert, no cooperation
- **Unique strength of hand geometry** — simplicity, hygiene, low cost

### Common Numerical Question Types
1. Compute EER given two normal distributions of scores
2. Compute Mahalanobis distance given covariance matrix
3. Compute d-prime separability index
4. Compute GEI pixel intensity for a given set of binary silhouettes
5. Compute gait cycle period from autocorrelation peak
]<]minimax[>[</section>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security of Biometric Systems: Adversary Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security of Biometric Systems: Adversary Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biometric System Security** refers to the set of mechanisms, protocols, cryptographic protections, and architectural safeguards designed to prevent unauthorized access, manipulation, or compromise of a biometric recognition pipeline. An **Adversary Attack** on a biometric system is any deliberate act — by a malicious entity — intended to subvert the integrity, confidentiality, availability, or authenticity of the system, the captured biometric samples, the stored templates, the feature representations, or the final decision.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECCT635 – Biometric Security**, the adversary attack surface is classified along the entire biometric processing pipeline. The seminal framework by **Ratha, Bolle, and Pankanti (2001)** identified **eight specific attack points** where an adversary can strike, ranging from the physical sensor all the way to the decision module output.

The principal categories of adversary attacks recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus include:

| # | Attack Class | Target |
|---|--------------|--------|
| 1 | **Presentation / Spoof Attack** | Sensor (fake biometric: gummy finger, face mask, iris print) |
| 2 | **Replay Attack** | Channel between sensor and feature extractor |
| 3 | **Template Tampering / Injection** | Template database |
| 4 | **Masquerade / Brute-force** | Matcher / Decision module |
| 5 | **Override / Hill-climbing** | Final decision / output |
| 6 | **Insider / Administrative Attack** | System administration layer |
| 7 | **Covert Acquisition** | User's raw biometric at source |
| 8 | **Feature Manipulation** | Communication channel or pre-matcher stage |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fortress with Many Doors"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Biometric Bank Vault**
>
> Imagine a futuristic bank vault that unlocks *only* when it recognizes your **fingerprint, face, or iris**. The vault has a pipeline: (1) A scanner reads your finger, (2) it converts the image into a mathematical "signature," (3) it compares the signature to the one on file, and (4) it opens the door.
>
> Now imagine a thief wanting to break in. The thief can:
> - 🧤 **Fake your finger** out of silicone and place it on the scanner (Spoof Attack)
> - 🎥 **Record the signal** as it travels from the scanner to the processor and replay it later (Replay Attack)
> - 🗄️ **Hack the database** and replace your stored signature with theirs (Template Tampering)
> - 🔁 **Try thousands of forged signatures** until one is accepted (Brute-force / Masquerade)
> - 🚪 **Bypass the door entirely** by sabotaging the decision logic (Override Attack)
>
> Each stage of the pipeline is a **door** — and a security engineer must lock *all* of them, not just the obvious front entrance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Security Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (Bold) used to quantify resistance of a biometric system against adversary attacks:**
> - **FAR (False Acceptance Rate)** — Probability that an impostor is accepted. A *higher* FAR means the system is *more vulnerable* to brute-force.
> - **FRR (False Rejection Rate)** — Probability that a genuine user is rejected.
> - **EER (Equal Error Rate)** — Operating point where FAR = FRR. Lower EER ⇒ better inherent security.
> - **IAPMR (Impostor Attack Presentation Match Rate)** — Probability that a **deliberately crafted spoof** is accepted. *Critical* for liveness detection evaluation.
> - **BPCER (Bonafide Presentation Classification Error Rate)** — Probability that a real user is falsely classified as an attacker by liveness detector.
> - **ACER (Average Classification Error Rate)** = $\frac{\text{IAPMR} + \text{BPCER}}{2}$ — used in ISO/IEC 30107-3 PAD testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ratha–Bolle–Pankanti Attack Taxonomy (KTU High-Priority)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A biometric system is a *pipeline of trust*. Each subsystem is a separate vulnerability. The Ratha model enumerates **8 attack points**, numbered **A1 through A8**.

### 🔹 Attack Point A1 — Fake Biometric at the Sensor
- **Where:** Physical sensor surface.
- **How:** Adversary presents an **artifact** (gummy finger, contact lens with printed iris, silicone face mask, high-resolution photograph).
- **Countermeasure:** **Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** / **Liveness Detection** using skin elasticity, blood flow (PPG), 3D depth, texture analysis (LBP), or deep CNN-based PAD.

### 🔹 Attack Point A2 — Replay of Captured Signal
- **Where:** Channel between **sensor and feature extractor**.
- **How:** Adversary intercepts the raw digitized biometric signal and replays it later.
- **Countermeasure:** **Challenge–Response protocols** (e.g., randomize illumination, prompt user to blink, use sequence of gestures); **encryption** of the inter-module channel; **time-stamping**.

### 🔹 Attack Point A3 — Feature Extractor Override
- **Where:** Feature extraction module.
- **How:** Adversary replaces the genuine feature extractor with a Trojan version that outputs attacker-controlled feature vectors.
- **Countermeasure:** **Code signing**, **trusted execution environments (TEE)**, **hardware security modules (HSM)**.

### 🔹 Attack Point A4 — Feature Modulator Tampering
- **Where:** Feature modulation / pre-matcher stage.
- **How:** Adversary modifies the feature vectors to look similar to a target user's template.
- **Countermeasure:** **Template encryption**, **secure feature hashing**, **watermarking** of feature vectors.

### 🔹 Attack Point A5 — Matcher Substitution
- **Where:** The matching / comparison module.
- **How:** Adversary substitutes the matcher so it always returns a "match" — bypassing the comparison logic entirely.
- **Countermeasure:** **Audit logs**, **redundant matching**, **secure boot**, **TEE-based matcher isolation**.

### 🔹 Attack Point A6 — Template Database Tampering
- **Where:** The stored template database.
- **How:** Adversary **adds, deletes, or modifies** stored templates (e.g., insert the attacker's own template under a legitimate user's ID).
- **Countermeasure:** **Hashing**, **digital signatures** on templates, **cancelable biometrics**, **biometric cryptosystems** (Fuzzy Vault, Fuzzy Commitment).

### 🔹 Attack Point A7 — Channel Interception (Template-in-Transit)
- **Where:** The channel between **template DB and matcher**.
- **How:** Adversary sniffs templates in transit and uses them for **masquerade or hill-climbing** offline.
- **Countermeasure:** **TLS / end-to-end encryption**, **mutual authentication**, **one-time tokens**.

### 🔹 Attack Point A8 — Decision Override
- **Where:** The final accept/reject output.
- **How:** Adversary directly forces a "match" decision at the final stage.
- **Countermeasure:** **Audit trails**, **multi-party authorization**, **out-of-band confirmation** (e.g., OTP on phone).

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Attack Success

Let the legitimate similarity score between a probe $P$ and the genuine template $T_g$ follow distribution $f_{\text{genuine}}(s)$, and an impostor probe against $T_g$ follow $f_{\text{impostor}}(s)$. Define a **decision threshold** $\tau$ such that the system accepts when $s \geq \tau$.

> [!NOTE]
> **For a standard impostor (no attack):**
> $$\text{FAR}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} f_{\text{impostor}}(s)\, ds$$
>
> **For a Presentation Attack (spoof):**
> $$\text{IAPMR}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} f_{\text{spoof}}(s)\, ds$$
>
> The **margin of vulnerability** is the increase in match probability that an attacker gains by switching from a random impostor sample to a crafted spoof:
> $$\Delta = \text{IAPMR}(\tau) - \text{FAR}(\tau)$$
>
> A well-designed PAD subsystem reduces $\Delta$ toward $0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\text{FAR} = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} f_{\text{impostor}}(s)\, ds$ | FAR, $\tau$ | Probability of impostor acceptance |
| 2 | $\text{FRR} = \int_{-\infty}^{\tau} f_{\text{genuine}}(s)\, ds$ | FRR, $\tau$ | Probability of genuine rejection |
| 3 | $\tau^* : \text{FAR}(\tau^*) = \text{FRR}(\tau^*)$ | EER | Equal Error Rate operating point |
| 4 | $\text{IAPMR}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} f_{\text{spoof}}(s)\, ds$ | IAPMR | Spoof acceptance rate (per ISO/IEC 30107-3) |
| 5 | $\text{BPCER}(\tau_{\text{PAD}}) = \int_{\tau_{\text{PAD}}}^{\infty} f_{\text{genuine-PAD}}(s)\, ds$ | BPCER | Genuine user wrongly rejected by liveness |
| 6 | $\text{ACER} = \frac{\text{IAPMR} + \text{BPCER}}{2}$ | ACER | Average PAD error |
| 7 | $\text{Match Risk} = 1 - (1-p)^N$ | $p$, $N$ | Probability attacker succeeds in $N$ independent attempts (geometric) |
| 8 | Hill-climbing convergence: $s_{k+1} = s_k + \eta \cdot \nabla_s S(P, T)$ | $\eta$ | Iterative score-driven synthesis attack |
| 9 | $H(\mathbf{x}) \geq -\log_2 \max_i p_i$ | $H$ | Min-entropy lower bound — governs brute-force resistance |
| 10 | Cancelable transform: $T' = f(T; K)$ | $f$, $K$ | Non-invertible salted template |

> **Note on absolute-value notation:** All vertical bars inside the table above are LaTeX-rendered using the strict column separator rule. For inline math outside tables, we use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in formulas.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Border Control (e- Gates):** IAPMR is the *single most cited metric* in ICAO / ISO PAD testing. A spoof acceptance at an airport is a national-security incident.
> - **Mobile Banking (Face Unlock):** Hill-climbing attacks have been demonstrated against commercial face matchers; cancelable templates defend against leaked template databases.
> - **Aadhaar / UIDAI (India):** The largest civilian biometric deployment uses **cancelable biometric tokens** + **HSM** to mitigate Ratha attack points A6 and A7.
> - **NIST FRVT-PAD** benchmarks continuously evaluate vendor systems against IAPMR/BPCER/ACER.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Brute-Force Attack Probability After $N$ Independent Trials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **most frequently asked KTU derivations** in the security-of-biometric-systems unit.

**Given:** A biometric matcher has a per-trial False Acceptance Rate $\text{FAR} = p$. An adversary makes $N$ *independent* trials against the system (e.g., by submitting $N$ candidate spoofs).

**Step 1 — Single-trial failure probability.**
If the probability of a *single* successful impersonation is $p$, then the probability of *failure* on a single attempt is:
$$q = 1 - p$$

**Step 2 — Probability of failure across $N$ independent trials.**
Because the trials are independent, the joint probability that *all* $N$ trials fail is the product of the individual failure probabilities:
$$P(\text{all } N \text{ fail}) = q^N = (1-p)^N$$

**Step 3 — Probability of at least one success.**
By the complement rule, the probability that the adversary succeeds in **at least one** of the $N$ attempts is:
$$P(\text{success in } N) = 1 - P(\text{all fail}) = 1 - (1-p)^N$$

**Step 4 — Form the final expression.**

$$\boxed{\,P_N = 1 - (1 - p)^N\,}$$

**Step 5 — Approximation for small $p$ and large $N$.**
Using the first-order Taylor expansion $(1-p)^N \approx e^{-Np}$ for small $p$:
$$P_N \approx 1 - e^{-Np}$$

This is the *exponential saturation curve* characteristic of brute-force resistance.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- A fingerprint matcher has $\text{FAR} = 0.0001$ (i.e., $p = 10^{-4}$).
- The system is configured to allow $N = 1000$ attempts before lockout.
- Compute $P_{1000}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1000} &= 1 - (1 - 10^{-4})^{1000} \\
&= 1 - (0.9999)^{1000} \\
&\approx 1 - e^{-1000 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.1} \\
&= 1 - 0.9048 \\
&\approx 0.0952
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 9.5% cumulative impersonation risk after 1000 attempts — well above the operational tolerance for high-security systems, which forces engineers to deploy **lockout**, **rate-limiting**, or **multi-factor** augmentation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Hill-Climbing Attack Convergence Bound

The **hill-climbing attack** is an *iterative* score-driven attack at Ratha's point A8: the adversary submits synthetic probes and nudges them in the direction that increases the match score until the system accepts.

**Setup:** Let $S(\mathbf{x})$ be the matcher's similarity score as a function of the synthetic probe $\mathbf{x}$. The adversary updates $\mathbf{x}$ iteratively:
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \eta_k \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} S(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
where $\eta_k$ is a small learning-rate step.

**Step 1 — Gradient magnitude bound.**
Assume $S$ is $L$-Lipschitz:
$$\vert S(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - S(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \leq L \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert\vert$$

**Step 2 — Step-size selection.**
Choose $\eta_k = \frac{\delta}{L}$ for a small $\delta > 0$, so:
$$S(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \geq S(\mathbf{x}_k) + \delta$$

**Step 3 — Number of iterations to reach threshold $\tau$ from initial score $S_0$.**
$$K \geq \left\lceil \frac{\tau - S_0}{\delta} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Final attack complexity bound.**

$$\boxed{\,K_{\text{hill}} = \mathcal{O}\!\left( \frac{\tau - S_0}{\eta L} \right)\,}$$

This is why production matchers enforce **score quantization**, **per-session rate limits**, and **decision-threshold randomization** — each one *raises* $K_{\text{hill}}$ and lengthens the time required for a successful hill-climbing attack.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Attack to Countermeasure

| Ratha Point | Attack | Critical Asset | Top Countermeasure | Risk Metric |
|-------------|--------|----------------|--------------------|-------------|
| A1 | Spoof / Fake biometric | Sensor | Liveness detection (PAD) | IAPMR |
| A2 | Replay | Sensor→Extractor channel | Challenge–response | Replay success rate |
| A3 | Feature extractor Trojan | Feature extractor | Trusted execution / code signing | Compromise probability |
| A4 | Feature manipulation | Feature vectors | Template encryption | Match false-positive lift |
| A5 | Matcher substitution | Matcher | Secure boot + audit | Match false-positive rate |
| A6 | Template DB tampering | Stored templates | Cancelable biometrics / Fuzzy Vault | Template irreversibility |
| A7 | Template sniffing | DB→Matcher channel | TLS / mutual auth | Eavesdropping success |
| A8 | Decision override | Output stage | Multi-factor confirmation / out-of-band | Hill-climbing iterations $K_{\text{hill}}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Biometric Pipeline with 8 Ratha Attack Points

<!-- (Mermaid used as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow per the protocol) -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[User / Subject] -->|presents biometric| S1
    S1[Sensor Capture] -->|raw signal| S2
    S2[Feature Extractor] -->|feature vector| S3
    S3[Template DB Lookup] -->|template| S4
    S4[Matcher / Comparator] -->|score| S5
    S5[Decision Module] -->|accept or reject| Out[Final Output]

    %% Attack injection points (red labels) with simple alphanumeric IDs
    A1[Attack Point A1 - Spoof at Sensor] -.->|fake finger or face mask| S1
    A2[Attack Point A2 - Replay on Channel] -.->|intercepted raw signal| S2
    A3[Attack Point A3 - Feature Extractor Override] -.->|Trojan module| S2
    A4[Attack Point A4 - Feature Manipulation] -.->|modified feature| S3
    A5[Attack Point A5 - Matcher Substitution] -.->|Trojan matcher| S4
    A6[Attack Point A6 - Template DB Tampering] -.->|insert or modify| S3
    A7[Attack Point A7 - Channel Interception DB to Matcher] -.->|sniffed template| S4
    A8[Attack Point A8 - Decision Override] -.->|force accept| S5

    %% Countermeasure layer (green) attached as a parallel defense
    CM[Countermeasure Layer - PAD - Cancelable Biometric - HSM - TLS - TEE]:::defense
```
