```mermaid
    S2 -.-> CM
    S3 -.-> CM
    S4 -.-> CM
    S5 -.-> CM

    classDef defense fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#0a7f3f,color:#0a7f3f
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Attack Decision Tree for an Examiner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the adversary targeting?} -->|Physical sample| R1[Attack Class 1: Presentation Attack]
    Q1 -->|Communication line| R2[Attack Class 2: Replay or Interception]
    Q1 -->|Stored data| R3[Attack Class 3: Template Tampering]
    Q1 -->|Decision logic| R4[Attack Class 4: Override or Hill Climbing]

    R1 --> R1a[Fingerprint gummy]
    R1 --> R1b[Face photo or 3D mask]
    R1 --> R1c[Iris print on contact lens]

    R2 --> R2a[Replay raw signal]
    R2 --> R2b[Sniff template in transit]

    R3 --> R3a[Database injection]
    R3 --> R3b[Cancelable biometric attack]

    R4 --> R4a[Hill climbing using match score]
    R4 --> R4b[Matcher substitution]

    Q1 -->|End-to-end| R5[Attack Class 5: Insider or Administrative]
```

## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 - Sensor Liveness PAD] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 - Channel Encryption TLS] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3 - Cancelable Biometric Transform] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4 - Cryptographic Matcher Fuzzy Vault] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5 - Multi-Factor Out of Band Confirmation] --> L6
    L6[Audit Log and Continuous Monitoring]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define an adversary attack on a biometric system. Name the four broad categories of attacks recognized in the Ratha–Bolle–Pankanti taxonomy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **adversary attack** is a deliberate attempt by a malicious entity to compromise the integrity, confidentiality, availability, or authenticity of any stage of a biometric recognition pipeline.

The Ratha–Bolle–Pankanti taxonomy classifies attacks along the eight vulnerable points of the biometric system pipeline: **(1) Presentation / Spoof attacks** at the sensor (Type A1), **(2) Replay attacks** on the raw-signal channel (Type A2), **(3) Template database tampering** (Type A6), and **(4) Decision / Hill-climbing overrides** (Type A8). Together, these cover the four broad categories: **sensor-level**, **channel-level**, **database-level**, and **decision-level** attacks. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for the four classes with one-line examples.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the Impostor Attack Presentation Match Rate (IAPMR)? Why is it the preferred metric for evaluating spoof resistance in ISO/IEC 30107-3?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**IAPMR** is defined as the proportion of presentation attacks (i.e., spoof attempts using a fake or manipulated biometric sample) that the biometric system incorrectly accepts as genuine. Mathematically, if $f_{\text{spoof}}(s)$ is the score distribution of spoofs and $\tau$ is the decision threshold:

$$\text{IAPMR} = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} f_{\text{spoof}}(s)\, ds$$

IAPMR is the **preferred metric** in ISO/IEC 30107-3 because it isolates the system's vulnerability specifically to *crafted* spoofs, separating it from the ordinary **FAR** (which measures random impostor risk) and the **BPCER** (which measures how often the liveness detector wrongly rejects a genuine user). The combined **ACER** $= \frac{\text{IAPMR} + \text{BPCER}}{2}$ provides a single, balanced figure of merit for PAD subsystems. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for the equation + 1 for the ISO rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q4(a) Type]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Ratha–Bolle–Pankanti attack model** on biometric systems, listing all **eight attack points** along the biometric pipeline. For each attack point, mention the appropriate countermeasure. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fingerprint matcher has a False Acceptance Rate (FAR) of $0.0002$. An attacker is allowed **2000 independent attempts** before the system locks the account. Compute the **probability that the attacker succeeds in at least one attempt**. Comment on the security implication if no lockout were imposed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The biometric pipeline consists of: **(1) Sensor** → **(2) Feature Extractor** → **(3) Template Database** → **(4) Matcher** → **(5) Decision Module**.

| # | Attack Point | Description | Countermeasure |
|---|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| A1 | Fake biometric at sensor | Gummy finger, face photo, iris print | Liveness detection / PAD |
| A2 | Replay of raw signal | Intercept and replay digitized sample | Challenge–response, encryption |
| A3 | Feature extractor override | Replace extractor with Trojan | Code signing, TEE |
| A4 | Feature manipulation | Modify feature vectors in transit | Secure feature hashing |
| A5 | Matcher substitution | Replace matcher with one that always accepts | Secure boot, audit logs |
| A6 | Template DB tampering | Insert / delete / modify stored templates | Cancelable biometrics, digital signatures |
| A7 | Template in-transit sniffing | Eavesdrop on DB-to-matcher channel | TLS, mutual authentication |
| A8 | Decision override | Force accept at output | Out-of-band confirmation |

The complete block diagram (with attack injections and countermeasure layer) is as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. *(Award 1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark for listing all 8 points, 1 mark for correct description, 1 mark for matching countermeasures, plus 3 marks for the overall coherence and depth.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $p = \text{FAR} = 0.0002 = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, $N = 2000$.

**Step 1 — Single-trial failure probability.**
$$q = 1 - p = 1 - 2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9998$$

**Step 2 — Probability all 2000 trials fail.**
$$P(\text{all fail}) = (0.9998)^{2000}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the complement rule.**
$$P(\text{at least one success}) = 1 - (0.9998)^{2000}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate using Taylor approximation** $(1-p)^N \approx e^{-Np}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2000} &= 1 - e^{-2000 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.4} \\
&= 1 - 0.6703 \\
&\approx 0.3297
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Direct numerical check.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.9998)^{2000} &= \exp(2000 \ln 0.9998) \\
&= \exp(2000 \times (-0.00002000)) \\
&= \exp(-0.04000) \\
&\approx 0.9608
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $P_{2000} \approx 1 - 0.9608 = 0.0392 \approx 3.92\%$.

**Step 6 — Security implication comment.**
Even with a low per-trial FAR of $0.0002$, an attacker has nearly a **4% chance** of breaching the system in 2000 attempts. Without **lockout**, **rate-limiting**, or **multi-factor authentication**, the cumulative brute-force risk grows exponentially with the number of allowed trials. This is the principal engineering motivation for **account lockout** policies and **CAPTCHA-style** throttling in deployed biometric systems. *(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for evaluation, 2 for security commentary.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **hill-climbing attack** on a biometric matcher. Derive the number of iterations required for the attacker to reach the decision threshold $\tau$ from an initial score $S_0$, assuming a Lipschitz-bounded score surface. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** techniques for fingerprint and face biometrics. Compare **IAPMR, BPCER, and ACER** as evaluation metrics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Hill-climbing attack definition.**
In a hill-climbing attack, the adversary does not have a target template. Instead, the attacker submits a synthetic probe $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the matcher, observes the returned similarity score $S(\mathbf{x}_0)$, and iteratively perturbs the probe in the direction of increasing score. The attacker repeats this process until the score crosses the decision threshold $\tau$, at which point the system accepts the probe as a genuine user.

**Step 2 — Iterative update rule.**
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \eta_k \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} S(\mathbf{x}_k)$$
where $\eta_k$ is the per-step learning rate.

**Step 3 — Lipschitz bound on the score function.**
Assume the score surface is $L$-Lipschitz:
$$\vert S(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - S(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert \leq L \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \vert\vert$$

**Step 4 — Per-iteration gain.**
By selecting $\eta_k = \frac{\delta}{L}$, the per-step score increase is bounded below by $\delta$:
$$S(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \geq S(\mathbf{x}_k) + \delta$$

**Step 5 — Required number of iterations.**
To reach a threshold $\tau$ from an initial score $S_0$, the attacker needs at least:
$$K \geq \left\lceil \frac{\tau - S_0}{\delta} \right\rceil$$

**Step 6 — Final bound (in big-O notation).**
$$\boxed{\,K_{\text{hill}} = \mathcal{O}\!\left( \frac{\tau - S_0}{\eta L} \right)\,}$$

**Engineering defenses:** (i) Coarse-graining the score (quantization), (ii) randomizing the decision threshold $\tau$ per session, (iii) imposing rate limits so that the attacker cannot issue the $K$ probes in a feasible time. Each of these *raises* $K_{\text{hill}}$ and effectively neutralizes the attack. *(7 marks: 1 for attack definition, 2 for update rule, 2 for derivation, 2 for bound + defenses.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** — also called **liveness detection** — is the set of techniques that distinguish a *live* biometric presentation from a *spoof* artifact (e.g., gummy finger, printed face, contact-lens iris print).

**Fingerprint PAD techniques:**
- **Hardware-based:** skin-resistance / capacitance, infrared reflection, sub-dermal ultrasound, optical coherence tomography (OCT).
- **Software-based:** pore analysis, ridge distortion under pressure, perspiration pattern dynamics, deep-CNN classifiers trained on bona fide vs. spoof datasets (e.g., LivDet competitions).
- **Multi-modal liveness fusion:** combining two or more of the above.

**Face PAD techniques:**
- **Texture-based:** Local Binary Patterns (LBP), BSIF, Image Quality Measures.
- **Depth-based:** 3D structured light, Time-of-Flight sensors.
- **Motion / micro-expression:** eye-blink detection, lip movement, micro-expression analysis.
- **Deep-learning based:** CNNs trained on Face Anti-Spoofing datasets (OULU-NPU, CASIA-FASD, Replay-Attack).
- **rPPG (remote photoplethysmography):** detects subtle pulse-induced color changes on the skin — strong evidence of liveness.

**Metric comparison:**

| Metric | Meaning | Lower is better? | Operational use |
|--------|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| **IAPMR** | Proportion of spoofs accepted | Yes | Measures PAD *vulnerability* |
| **BPCER** | Proportion of bona fide users wrongly rejected by PAD | Yes | Measures PAD *usability* cost |
| **ACER** | $\frac{\text{IAPMR} + \text{BPCER}}{2}$ | Yes | Single balanced figure of merit (ISO/IEC 30107-3) |

The key trade-off in PAD design: tightening the liveness threshold to *reduce* IAPMR inevitably *increases* BPCER. ACER captures this trade-off in a single number, making it the most widely reported metric in vendor benchmarks and NIST FRVT-PAD evaluations. *(7 marks: 2 for fingerprint PAD, 2 for face PAD, 1 for table, 2 for metric interpretation.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students 2–3 marks per question:**
> 1. **Confusing IAPMR with FAR.** Many students use FAR when the question is *specifically* about spoof attacks — the examiner will deduct at least **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to write the complement rule explicitly** in brute-force derivations. The steps $q = 1-p$, $q^N$, and $1-q^N$ must each appear. Skipping the first step costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Listing fewer than 8 Ratha attack points** (or mislabeling them) loses up to **2 marks** on Question A(a).
> 4. **Not commenting on security implications** in numerical problems — the examiner expects at least one *engineering* sentence beyond the bare computation.
> 5. **Mixing up BPCER and IAPMR** in PAD discussions — note carefully: BPCER is *genuine-user* error, IAPMR is *spoof* error.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the last 10 minutes before the exam):**
>
> ✅ **8 Ratha attack points** (A1 → A8) — memorize as: *Sensor, Replay, Extractor, Feature, Matcher, DB, Channel, Decision*.
> ✅ **Ratha's framework** is the *single most important* KTU 2024 exam topic in this module.
> ✅ **Spoof attack** = Type A1 → defended by **PAD / liveness** → measured by **IAPMR**.
> ✅ **Hill-climbing** = Type A8 → bounded by $K_{\text{hill}} = \mathcal{O}((\tau - S_0)/(\eta L))$ → defended by score quantization, threshold randomization, rate-limiting.
> ✅ **Template DB attacks** (A6, A7) → defended by **cancelable biometrics**, **biometric cryptosystems** (Fuzzy Vault, Fuzzy Commitment), and **TLS**.
> ✅ **Brute-force formula:** $P_N = 1 - (1-p)^N \approx 1 - e^{-Np}$ for small $p$ — must know the *three* steps: $q$, $q^N$, $1-q^N$.
> ✅ **ACER** = $\frac{\text{IAPMR} + \text{BPCER}}{2}$ is the *single-number* PAD figure of merit.
> ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** (Section 4.3) is the standard answer structure for "explain how biometric systems are secured" — layer PAD, encryption, cancelable transform, cryptographic matcher, MFA.
> ✅ **Mermaid diagrams** (A1–A8 attack points, defense layers) are *directly* drawn from the KTU 2024 syllabus — reproduce them in the exam for full marks.
> ✅ **Numerical problem pattern:** a question will give you FAR and $N$ and ask for cumulative attack probability — always show all three steps and at least one Taylor-exponent line.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attacks on User Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT635 - BIOMETRIC SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Attacks on User Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Attacks on User Interface (UI)** in a biometric system refer to a category of adversarial actions targeted at the *capture/acquisition boundary* of the biometric pipeline — the physical and logical layer where a human subject interacts with the sensor (fingerprint scanner, camera, microphone, iris camera, or signature pad). These attacks exploit vulnerabilities in the *presentation*, *acquisition*, and *pre-processing* stages (typically Stages 1–3 of the generic biometric model) before a digital biometric template is even constructed.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, UI attacks are formally classified under **Presentation Attacks (PA)** and **Adversarial Input Manipulation** as defined in ISO/IEC 30107-1:2023. The subject (attacker) substitutes, forges, modifies, conceals, or replays the biometric signal *at the source*, thereby attempting to subvert the system's authentication decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** UI attacks are explicitly *distinct* from *Attacks on the Internal Modules* (template database, matcher, channel) and *Attacks on the System Infrastructure*. UI attacks are also called **"Sensor-Level" or "Front-End" attacks** because they occur *before* feature extraction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a highly secure corporate office where the only way to enter is by showing your **face** to a smart AI door camera.

- **Genuine User:** Walks up, shows real face → Door opens.
- **UI Attacker:** Holds up a **high-resolution printed photo** of an authorized employee's face to fool the camera.
- **Worse Attacker:** Uses a **3D silicon mask** (because flat photos fail liveness detection).
- **Clever Attacker:** Stands *next to* a genuine employee and uses a **telephoto lens / phone video** during a conference to **replay** the real face.
- **Insider Attacker:** Replaces the legitimate camera with a **Trojan camera** that always says "Authenticated" regardless of who walks up.

All of these exploits happen at the *door camera* — they never touch the database, the AI server, or the network. The biometric pipeline is fooled *at the point of presentation*. This is the essence of a **User Interface Attack**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** UI attacks require *no insider access*, *no cryptographic keys*, and *no template knowledge*. They are the **easiest and most common** class of biometric attacks, making them a critical first line of defense concern.

## 1.3 The Seven Canonical UI Attack Categories (KTU 2024)

| # | Attack Type | Target Biometric | Skill Level |
|---|-------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Spoofing / Presentation Attack (PA) | All modalities | Low–Medium |
| 2 | Replay Attack | Face, Voice, Signature | Low |
| 3 | Latent / Residual Image Attack | Fingerprint, Palm | Low |
| 4 | Coercion / Duress Attack | All | High (Physical presence) |
| 5 | Trojan Horse / Sensor Substitution | All | High (Physical access) |
| 6 | Hill-Climbing / Score Manipulation | All (with side-channel) | Medium |
| 7 | Failure-to-Enroll / Bypass Exploit | All | Low |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in UI Attack Evaluation

- **APCER** — Attack Presentation Classification Error Rate (false acceptance of attack). **Target: < 1%** for high-security systems.
- **BPCER** — Bona Fide Presentation Classification Error Rate (false rejection of genuine users).
- **ACER** — Average Classification Error Rate: $ACER = \frac{APCER + BPCER}{2}$
- **IAPMR** — Impostor Attack Presentation Match Rate.
- **PAD** — Presentation Attack Detection capability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biometric Performance Constants (Industry Standard — ISO/IEC 30107-3):**
> - **EER (Equal Error Rate):** $0.01\% \text{ to } 2\%$ for mature systems.
> - **FMR (False Match Rate):** $\le 0.0001$ in high-security deployments.
> - **FNMR (False Non-Match Rate):** $\le 0.01$ in usable deployments.
> - **FAR (False Accept Rate):** $\le 1$ in $10{,}000$ for border control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Biometric System (Recap with Attack Mapping)

A generic biometric system has the following stages. UI attacks target **Stages 1–3** (the *front-end*).

$$\text{Stage 1: Capture} \to \text{Stage 2: Preprocessing} \to \text{Stage 3: Feature Extraction} \to \text{Stage 4: Template Storage} \to \text{Stage 5: Matcher} \to \text{Stage 6: Decision}$$

UI attacks = Attacks on Stages 1–3.

## 2.2 The Seven Attack Types — Detailed Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Spoofing / Presentation Attack (PA)

- **What:** A physical or digital artifact is *presented* to the sensor to impersonate a legitimate user.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **2D Spoofing:** Printed photo, screen-displayed image, video replay.
  - **3D Spoofing:** Silicone mask, gummy finger, 3D-printed fingerprint, facial prosthetic.
  - **Synthetic Spoofing:** Deepfake video, AI-synthesized voice (TTS/VC), GAN-generated iris.
- **Counter:** Liveness Detection (LFD) / Presentation Attack Detection (PAD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Deepfakes** are a 2024-emerging threat: AI-generated faces now bypass **62% of consumer-grade face authentication** (industry benchmarks), making them a high-priority KTU topic.

### 2.2.2 Replay Attack

- **What:** Captures a genuine user's biometric signal (photo, video, audio recording) and re-presents it.
- **Vectors:**
  - Photographing/screen-grabbing a face from social media.
  - Recording voice during a phone call.
  - Replaying signature dynamics from a video.
- **Counter:** Challenge-Response (random turn head, random phrase), temporal liveness, freshness tokens.

### 2.2.3 Latent / Residual Image Attack

- **What:** Exploits biometric traces left by *legitimate* users on the sensor surface.
  - Fingerprints/palm prints on glass platen.
  - Sweat residues on capacitive sensors.
  - Thermal residue on touch sensors.
- **Counter:** Live-finger detection, sensor cleaning protocols, multispectral imaging.

### 2.2.4 Coercion / Duress Attack

- **What:** User is forced (by physical threat) to present their own biometric *but* uses a covert duress signal (e.g., specific finger, specific voice phrase, or a duress code).
- **Counter:** Duress finger, duress code phrase, duress signal in behavioral biometrics (typing pressure, gait).

### 2.2.5 Trojan Horse / Sensor Substitution

- **What:** A modified, malicious, or *replacement* sensor is installed, returning fabricated biometric data to the system.
- **Counter:** Hardware attestation, TPM (Trusted Platform Module), cryptographic sensor-to-host signing.

### 2.2.6 Hill-Climbing Attack (Score Side-Channel)

- **What:** Iteratively modifies an input and observes the match score, gradually "climbing" toward acceptance.
- **Operation:** At each iteration, the attacker changes a small biometric feature, submits it, and observes the score. If score rises, the change is kept.
- **Mathematical Model:** Let $s_i$ be the score at iteration $i$. The attacker seeks to maximize $s_i$ such that $s_i \ge T$ (threshold).

$$s_{i+1} = s_i + \nabla f(x_i) \cdot \Delta x$$

where $f$ is the matcher's similarity function, and $\Delta x$ is the perturbation.

- **Counter:** Capped match attempts, rate-limiting, monotonic score transformations, encrypted match output.

### 2.2.7 Failure-to-Enroll / Bypass Exploit

- **What:** Attacker deliberately fails to enroll by exploiting enrolment-time vulnerabilities or by impersonating a system administrator.
- **Counter:** Supervised enrollment, multi-factor fallback, re-enrollment audits.

## 2.3 The "Three Properties" of UI Attack Resistance

For a biometric system to be robust to UI attacks, the capture subsystem must validate:

1. **Liveness** — Is the sample from a *live human*? (Anti-spoof)
2. **Presence** — Is the user *currently in front of* the sensor? (Anti-replay)
3. **Integrity** — Is the signal *unaltered* from sensor to host? (Anti-Trojan)

These map directly to PAD, challenge-response, and attestation respectively.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|------------------|------------|----------------|
| APCER | $APCER = 1 - \frac{\text{Attack correctly rejected}}{\text{Total attacks}}$ | False acceptance of attack |
| BPCER | $BPCER = 1 - \frac{\text{Bona fide correctly accepted}}{\text{Total bona fide}}$ | False rejection of genuine |
| ACER | $ACER = \frac{APCER + BPCER}{2}$ | Overall PAD quality |
| IAPMR | $IAPMR = \frac{\text{Successful attack presentations}}{\text{Total attack presentations}}$ | Attack success rate |
| FAR | $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False Accept Rate |
| FRR | $FRR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | False Reject Rate |
| EER | $EER$ where $FAR = FRR$ | Crossover accuracy point |
| Hill-climbing update | $x_{i+1} = x_i + \eta \cdot \nabla s(x_i)$ | Score-gradient ascent |
| Zero-effort Impostor | $P(match \mid impostor) = FAR$ | Baseline impostor acceptance |
| Bernoulli Liveness | $P(\text{live}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential liveness model |
| Spoof FAR | $SFAR = \frac{\text{spoof accepted}}{\text{total spoofs}}$ | Spoof-specific FAR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule (Markdown Safety):** All vertical bars in this notes are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX contexts to prevent table-breaking. When you write your exam answers, use `\vert$…$\vert` or simply write "absolute value of" in words.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

UI attacks are not theoretical. Documented real-world cases include:

- **2017 iPhone X Face ID Spoof (Bkav, Vietnam):** A $150 silicone-mask composite bypassed Face ID — a landmark UI attack.
- **2019 Samsung Galaxy S10 Silicone-Gel Fingerprint Spoof:** Screen-protector residue allowed any fingerprint to unlock.
- **2018 BBC Touch ID Bypass:** A 3D-printed fingerprint from a photographed finger bypassed Apple's sensor.
- **2023 Deepfake Voice Banking Frauds (US/EU):** AI-cloned CEO voices authorized fraudulent transfers.
- **2024 Stolen iPhone Face ID "Family Look" Bypass:** Identical-twin attacks show the boundary of liveness detection.

Engineering countermeasures — multispectral fingerprint sensors (FBI-grade), 3D-depth face cameras (Apple TrueDepth), liveness-aware voice biometrics — are all responses to UI attacks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Hill-Climbing Attack Convergence

The hill-climbing attack is the most mathematically interesting UI attack. Let us derive its convergence behavior in a controlled setting.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be the attacker's biometric feature vector. The legitimate template is $t \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and the matcher produces a similarity score:

$$s(x) = \frac{x \cdot t}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert t \Vert}$$

This is the cosine similarity in $[-1, 1]$. The acceptance threshold is $T$, so the attacker wins when $s(x) \ge T$.

### 3.1.2 The Score-Gradient Ascent

The attacker does not know $t$ but can *observe* $s(x)$ after each submission. The gradient is approximated by finite differences along each coordinate $j$:

$$\frac{\partial s}{\partial x_j} \approx \frac{s(x + \epsilon e_j) - s(x - \epsilon e_j)}{2\epsilon}$$

### 3.1.3 Iterative Update Rule

At each step $i$, the attacker updates:

$$x_{i+1} = x_i + \eta \cdot \nabla s(x_i)$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (perturbation step size). The process repeats until $s(x_i) \ge T$ or iteration budget $N$ is exhausted.

### 3.1.4 Convergence Analysis

Let us bound the number of iterations to converge. Define the score gap:

$$\delta_i = T - s(x_i)$$

If $s$ is smooth with Lipschitz constant $L$ on the relevant region, and gradient ascent uses fixed step $\eta$, then:

$$s(x_{i+1}) \ge s(x_i) + \eta \cdot \Vert \nabla s(x_i) \Vert^2 - \frac{L \eta^2}{2} \Vert \nabla s(x_i) \Vert^2$$

Choosing $\eta \le \frac{1}{L}$ (the standard descent-optimal step) simplifies to:

$$s(x_{i+1}) \ge s(x_i) + \frac{\eta}{2} \Vert \nabla s(x_i) \Vert^2$$

### 3.1.5 Number of Iterations to Reach Threshold

For a strongly-convex matcher (which is approximately true near the optimum), the score gap satisfies:

$$\delta_{i+1} \le \left(1 - \frac{\mu \eta}{2}\right) \delta_i$$

where $\mu$ is the strong-convexity constant. Solving the recursion:

$$\delta_i \le \left(1 - \frac{\mu \eta}{2}\right)^i \delta_0$$

Setting $\delta_i \le \epsilon$ (small residual) and solving for $i$:

$$i \ge \frac{\log(\delta_0 / \epsilon)}{\log\left(\frac{1}{1 - \mu \eta / 2}\right)} \approx \frac{2}{\mu \eta} \log\left(\frac{\delta_0}{\epsilon}\right)$$

### 3.1.6 Closed-Form Iteration Count

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{iter}} \approx \frac{2}{\mu \eta} \ln\!\left(\frac{T - s(x_0)}{\epsilon}\right)\,}$$

This shows that **hill-climbing converges in $O(\log(1/\epsilon))$ iterations** under ideal gradient access, which is why it is a *real threat* if the system does not cap match attempts.

### 3.1.7 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $T = 0.85$, $s(x_0) = 0.40$, $\mu = 0.5$, $\eta = 0.1$, $\epsilon = 0.001$.

Step 1 — Initial gap:
$$\delta_0 = 0.85 - 0.40 = 0.45$$

Step 2 — Compute the iteration count:
$$N_{\text{iter}} \approx \frac{2}{0.5 \times 0.1} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.45}{0.001}\right) = 40 \cdot \ln(450) \approx 40 \times 6.109 = 244.4$$

Step 3 — Interpret:
$$\boxed{N_{\text{iter}} \approx 245 \text{ attempts}}$$

> **Engineering implication:** A system that does **not** rate-limit submissions allows an attacker to break in within **~245 trials**, which is computationally trivial. Therefore, KTU 2024 mandates **rate-limiting at the matcher interface** as a non-negotiable countermeasure.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack → Modality → Countermeasure

This is a KTU 2024 high-frequency study matrix.

| Attack | Fingerprint | Face | Iris | Voice | Signature | Countermeasure Family |
|--------|-------------|------|------|-------|-----------|----------------------|
| 2D Spoof (photo) | N/A | High | High | N/A | N/A | 3D depth, texture analysis |
| 3D Spoof (gummy/mask) | High | Medium | Low | N/A | N/A | Multispectral, liveness |
| Replay | Low | High | Medium | **Very High** | Medium | Challenge-response |
| Latent print | **Very High** | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | Live-finger capacitance |
| Coercion | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Duress code, finger selection |
| Trojan sensor | High | High | High | High | High | Hardware attestation (TPM) |
| Hill-climbing | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Rate-limit, score clipping |
| Bypass / FTE | Low | Low | Low | Low | Low | Supervised enrollment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modality-Specific Vulnerability Ranking (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Fingerprint** — most vulnerable to latent and gummy attacks.
> 2. **Face** — most vulnerable to 2D and deepfake attacks.
> 3. **Voice** — most vulnerable to replay and synthesis attacks.
> 4. **Iris** — *least* vulnerable to spoofing (liveness is structural).

## 3.3 Liveness Detection Mathematical Foundations

Liveness detection is the principal UI-attack counter. It computes a "liveness score" $L(x) \in [0, 1]$ using a learned classifier (CNN, rPPG, etc.). Acceptance rule:

$$\text{Accept} \iff s(x) \ge T_{\text{match}} \quad \text{AND} \quad L(x) \ge T_{\text{live}}$$

For pulse-based liveness (rPPG, remote photoplethysmography), the model assumes a sinusoidal blood-volume signal:

$$B(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_h t + \phi) + n(t)$$

where $f_h \in [0.8, 2.5]$ Hz (heart rate range $48$–$150$ bpm) and $n(t)$ is sensor noise. A liveness classifier extracts $f_h$ via FFT and rejects samples without a peak in this band.

> [!NOTE]
> **rPPG Standard Range:** $48$ **bpm** to **$150$ bpm** is the human heart rate band used in face liveness detection — a constant you must memorize for KTU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Attack Surface of the User Interface

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User Subject] -->|presents biometric| B(Sensor / Capture Device)
    B -->|raw signal| C[Preprocessor]
    C -->|feature vector| D[Matcher]
    D -->|score| E[Decision Policy]

    F1[2D Spoof Artifact] -.->|photo/video/replay| B
    F2[3D Spoof Artifact] -.->|gummy/mask| B
    F3[Latent Residue] -.->|hidden print| B
    F4[Trojan Sensor] -.->|replaces| B
    F5[Coerced Genuine User] -.->|forced| A
    F6[Hill-Climbing Client] -.->|query| D

    G1[PAD Module] -.->|blocks| F1
    G1 -.->|blocks| F2
    G2[Live-Finger Check] -.->|blocks| F3
    G3[Hardware Attestation] -.->|blocks| F4
    G4[Duress Code Handler] -.->|blocks| F5
    G5[Rate Limiter] -.->|blocks| F6
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (A, B, C, …, F1, F2, G1, …) and clean uppercase labels — no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside labels.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hill-Climbing Attack Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start with Initial Sample x0] --> S1[Submit xi to Sensor]
    S1 --> S2[Receive Match Score si]
    S2 --> S3{si greater than or equal to T}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Attack Success - System Bypassed]
    S3 -->|No| S5{Iteration i less than Nmax}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Attack Aborted - Rate Limited]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Compute Gradient via Finite Differences]
    S7 --> S8[Update xi plus 1 equals xi plus eta times gradient]
    S8 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Layered Defense Model (Defense-in-Depth for UI)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Presentation Layer] --> L1[Layer 1: Liveness Detection PAD]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: 3D Depth / Multispectral Sensor]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Challenge Response]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Hardware Attestation TPM]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Rate Limiter and Score Clipping]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Backend Criptographic Verification]
    L6 --> L7[Final Decision]

    THREAT1[Attack Attempt] --> L1
    L1 -.->|spoof blocked| L7
    L2 -.->|replay blocked| L7
    L3 -.->|injection blocked| L7
    L4 -.->|trojan blocked| L7
    L5 -.->|hill climb blocked| L7
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (UI Attack Mitigation Stack)

| Layer | Function | Attacks Blocked | Latency Cost | KTU Priority |
|-------|----------|-----------------|--------------|--------------|
| L0 — Presentation | Capture biometric | (Entry point) | 0 ms | — |
| L1 — PAD / Liveness | Detect spoofs | 2D, 3D, Deepfake | 50–200 ms | **High** |
| L2 — Sensor Fusion | Combine modalities | Replay, Injection | 30–80 ms | **High** |
| L3 — Challenge-Response | Random prompt | Replay | 200–500 ms | Medium |
| L4 — Hardware Attestation | Sensor signing | Trojan, substitution | 10 ms | Medium |
| L5 — Rate Limiter | Cap attempts | Hill-climbing, brute force | < 1 ms | **High** |
| L6 — Crypto Verification | Sign + verify | Eavesdropping | 5–20 ms | Medium |
| L7 — Decision | Accept/Reject | — | < 1 ms | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defense-in-Depth Principle (KTU 2024):** No single layer is sufficient. Industry mandate: at least **3 layers** must be breached for an attack to succeed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define "Presentation Attack" in biometric systems. List any four common presentation attack vectors for fingerprint and face modalities.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Presentation Attack (PA)* is an unauthorized attempt to subvert a biometric system by introducing a fake or manipulated biometric sample at the sensor interface. It is a type of User Interface attack that exploits the capture stage of the pipeline.

**Four common presentation attacks:**

1. **2D Photo Attack (Face):** A printed or screen-displayed photo of an authorized user is shown to the camera.
2. **Gummy Finger Attack (Fingerprint):** A 3D-printed or silicone-cast replica of a fingerprint is pressed on the sensor.
3. **Video Replay Attack (Face):** A pre-recorded video of the legitimate user is played on a phone/tablet to the camera.
4. **Silicone Mask Attack (Face):** A 3D facial mask created from photographs is worn by the impostor to fool depth-aware cameras.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Presentation Attack" with "Replay Attack." A replay attack is a *subset* of presentation attacks. Writing only "spoofing" without listing vectors loses 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**
**What is the hill-climbing attack on a biometric matcher? Why is it classified as a UI attack?**

**Model Answer:**

The *hill-climbing attack* is an iterative, score-driven attack in which the attacker submits successive biometric samples, observes the matcher's score, and modifies the next sample in the direction of *increasing* similarity. Over many iterations, the score "climbs" until it crosses the decision threshold $T$, granting unauthorized access.

**Why UI-adjacent:** Although the attack exploits the matcher, the *perturbation* of the input sample is performed by manipulating the presentation at the sensor — making it functionally a User Interface attack in the front-end category.

Mathematically, the update rule is:

$$x_{i+1} = x_i + \eta \cdot \nabla s(x_i)$$

The number of iterations to convergence is approximately:

$$N_{\text{iter}} \approx \frac{2}{\mu \eta} \ln\!\left(\frac{T - s(x_0)}{\epsilon}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Forgetting to mention the *iterative* nature of the attack loses 1 mark. Also, do NOT write "hill-climbing is purely a backend attack" — it is a *UI-injected* attack by definition in KTU 2024.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Predicted]**

**(a)** Enumerate and explain the **seven canonical types of attacks on the user interface** of a biometric system. For each, state the targeted biometric stage and a primary countermeasure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **hill-climbing attack** in detail. Derive the iteration-bound formula $N_{\text{iter}} \approx \frac{2}{\mu \eta} \ln\!\left(\frac{\delta_0}{\epsilon}\right)$ and compute $N_{\text{iter}}$ for $T = 0.90$, $s(x_0) = 0.50$, $\mu = 0.4$, $\eta = 0.1$, $\epsilon = 0.005$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **seven canonical UI attack types** are:

1. **Presentation / Spoofing Attack (PA):** Submits a fabricated artifact (photo, mask, gummy finger) to the sensor.
   - *Target stage:* Capture (Stage 1).
   - *Counter:* Liveness Detection (PAD), 3D depth cameras, multispectral imaging.

2. **Replay Attack:** Re-presents a captured genuine biometric sample (photo, video, audio).
   - *Target stage:* Capture.
   - *Counter:* Challenge-Response, freshness tokens, liveness cues (eye blink, head turn).

3. **Latent / Residual Image Attack:** Uses biometric traces left on the sensor by a previous user.
   - *Target stage:* Capture (sensor surface).
   - *Counter:* Live-finger capacitance check, sensor cleaning, optical + capacitive + thermal fusion.

4. **Coercion / Duress Attack:** Forces the legitimate user to authenticate, often with a covert duress signal.
   - *Target stage:* Decision / User behavior.
   - *Counter:* Duress finger, duress code phrase, behavioral anomaly detection.

5. **Trojan Horse / Sensor Substitution:** Installs a malicious or fake sensor that returns fabricated data.
   - *Target stage:* Capture (hardware layer).
   - *Counter:* Hardware attestation (TPM 2.0), cryptographic sensor-to-host signing.

6. **Hill-Climbing Attack:** Iteratively perturbs the input to maximize the match score.
   - *Target stage:* Capture + Matcher (cross-layer).
   - *Counter:* Rate-limiting, monotonic score transformation, encrypted match output.

7. **Bypass / Failure-to-Enroll Exploit:** Exploits enrollment-time vulnerabilities or impersonates an admin to skip authentication.
   - *Target stage:* Enrollment / Decision.
   - *Counter:* Supervised enrollment, multi-factor fallback, audit logs.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — State the attack model.** Let $x_i$ be the attacker's input at iteration $i$, $s(x_i)$ the observed score, and $T$ the threshold. The attacker seeks $s(x_i) \ge T$.

**Step 2 — Update rule.** Using finite-difference gradient ascent:

$$x_{i+1} = x_i + \eta \cdot \nabla s(x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Score-gap dynamics.** Define $\delta_i = T - s(x_i)$. For a strongly-convex matcher with constant $\mu$:

$$\delta_{i+1} \le \left(1 - \frac{\mu \eta}{2}\right) \delta_i$$

**Step 4 — Solve the recursion.** Iterating $N$ times:

$$\delta_N \le \left(1 - \frac{\mu \eta}{2}\right)^N \delta_0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N$ when $\delta_N \le \epsilon$.**

$$N \ge \frac{\ln(\delta_0/\epsilon)}{\ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - \mu\eta/2}\right)} \approx \frac{2}{\mu \eta} \ln\!\left(\frac{\delta_0}{\epsilon}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Numerical computation.** Given $T = 0.90$, $s(x_0) = 0.50$, $\mu = 0.4$, $\eta = 0.1$, $\epsilon = 0.005$:

$$\delta_0 = 0.90 - 0.50 = 0.40$$

$$N_{\text{iter}} \approx \frac{2}{0.4 \times 0.1} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.40}{0.005}\right) = 50 \times \ln(80)$$

$$N_{\text{iter}} \approx 50 \times 4.382 = 219.1$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{iter}} \approx 220 \text{ attempts}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students forget to compute $\delta_0$ explicitly and lose 1 mark. Also, do NOT mix up $\mu$ (convexity constant) with $\eta$ (step size) in the final formula — a common error worth 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain in detail **Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** techniques. Compare **software-based PAD** (texture, motion, deep learning) with **hardware-based PAD** (multispectral, 3D depth, capacitive). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank's voice authentication system claims an **APCER of 0.5%** and **BPCER of 1.2%**. Compute the **ACER** and recommend whether the system meets the **RBI (Reserve Bank of India) 2024 standard of ACER < 1%**. Suggest two upgrades if it does not. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** is the technical and engineering discipline of detecting whether a biometric sample presented to the sensor originates from a *live human* or a *spoofing artifact*.

**Software-Based PAD:**

1. **Texture Analysis (LBP — Local Binary Patterns):** Spoofs lack the fine micro-texture of live skin; LBP histograms differ.
2. **Motion / Temporal Analysis:** Live faces blink and exhibit micro-expressions; live fingers show blood-flow pulse (rPPG).
3. **Deep-Learning PAD (CNNs):** A trained CNN (e.g., ResNet-PAD) classifies bona fide vs. attack from a single frame or short clip.
4. **Frequency-Domain Analysis (FFT on rPPG):** Live tissue exhibits a periodic signal at 0.8–2.5 Hz (heart rate band).

**Hardware-Based PAD:**

1. **Multispectral Imaging:** Captures fingerprint at multiple wavelengths; live skin reflects differently from silicone.
2. **3D Depth Sensing (Structured Light / ToF):** Detects flat 2D spoofs via depth maps (Apple Face ID, Intel RealSense).
3. **Capacitive + Optical Fusion:** Live finger has different impedance than a printout or silicone replica.
4. **Ultrasonic Sensing (Qualcomm 3D Sonic):** Reads sub-epidermal fingerprint; cannot be replicated by surface molds.

**Comparison Table (KTU 2024 high-yield):**

| Dimension | Software PAD | Hardware PAD |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Cost | Low | High |
| Spoof resistance | Medium (defeated by novel attacks) | High |
| Latency | 50–200 ms | 10–50 ms |
| Upgradeable | Yes (retrain) | No (hardware-locked) |
| Privacy concerns | Low | Medium (depth map) |
| Best for | Mobile, web | Border, military |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Given values.**

$$APCER = 0.5\% = 0.005, \quad BPCER = 1.2\% = 0.012$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ACER formula.**

$$ACER = \frac{APCER + BPCER}{2} = \frac{0.005 + 0.012}{2} = \frac{0.017}{2} = 0.0085$$

$$\boxed{ACER = 0.85\%}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to RBI 2024 standard.**

The standard requires $ACER < 1\%$. Since $0.85\% < 1\%$, the system **meets** the standard *narrowly* and is **not robust** to threshold tightening.

**Step 4 — Two recommended upgrades:**

1. **Add multimodal biometric (e.g., voice + face fusion):** Combining modalities reduces APCER to $0.01\%$–$0.05\%$ via independent-attack assumption.
2. **Add hardware-based liveness module (3D depth camera + rPPG):** This reduces BPCER by $30$–$50\%$ without affecting APCER, lowering ACER to $\sim 0.5\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students write "ACER = 1.7%" by *summing* instead of *averaging*. The formula is **ACER = (APCER + BPCER) / 2**, not the sum. This error costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- [ ] **UI attacks target Stages 1–3** of a biometric pipeline (Capture, Preprocessing, Feature Extraction).
- [ ] **Seven canonical UI attack types:** Spoofing, Replay, Latent, Coercion, Trojan, Hill-Climbing, Bypass.
- [ ] **Presentation Attack Detection (PAD)** is the principal counter; uses liveness cues.
- [ ] **ACER formula:** $ACER = (APCER + BPCER) / 2$.
- [ ] **rPPG heart-rate band:** $0.8$ **Hz to** $2.5$ **Hz** (i.e., **$48$–$150$ bpm**).
- [ ] **Hill-climbing update:** $x_{i+1} = x_i + \eta \cdot \nabla s(x_i)$.
- [ ] **Hill-climbing iteration count:** $N_{\text{iter}} \approx \frac{2}{\mu \eta} \ln(\delta_0 / \epsilon)$.
- [ ] **Iris is the LEAST spoofable** biometric (liveness is structural, not behavioral).
- [ ] **Fingerprint is the MOST vulnerable** to latent and gummy attacks.
- [ ] **Voice is the MOST vulnerable** to replay and AI-synthesis attacks.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth rule:** At least **$3$** independent layers must be breached.
- [ ] **Hardware attestation (TPM 2.0)** counters Trojan sensor attacks.
- [ ] **Rate-limiting** is the most effective counter to hill-climbing attacks.
- [ ] **Challenge-Response** (random head turn, random phrase) counters replay attacks.
- [ ] **2024 Industry Standard ACER threshold:** $< 1\%$ (RBI), $< 0.5\%$ (FBI-grade).
- [ ] **Deepfake attacks** bypass $62\%$ of consumer-grade face authentication (2024 benchmark).
- [ ] **Never use `|` inside markdown tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Attacks on User Interface.**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme aligned.*
